Your are mixing multiple questions with assertions which may or may not be true. So I'll take a stab at this, comment if it doesn't make sense to you. To answer the question in the title, you invest in an IRA because you want to save money to allow you to retire. The government provides you with tax incentives that make an IRA an excellent vehicle to do this. The rules regarding IRA tax treatment provide disincentives, through tax penalties, for withdrawing money before retirement. This topic is covered dozens of times, so search around for more detail. Regarding your desire to invest in items with high "intrinsic" value, I would argue that gold and silver are not good vehicles for doing this. Intrinsic value doesn't mean what you want it to mean in this context -- gold and silver are commodities, whose prices fluctuate dramatically. If you want to grow money for retirement over a long period, of time, you should be invested in diversified collection of investments, and precious metals should be a relatively small part of your portfolio.

Morningstar is often considered a trusted industry standard when it comes to rating mutual funds and ETFs. They offer the same data-centric information for other investments as well, such as individual stocks and bonds. You can consult Morningstar directly if you like, but any established broker will usually provide you with Morningstar's ratings for the products it is trying to sell to you. Vanguard offers a few Emerging Markets stock and bond funds, some actively managed, some index funds. Other investment management companies (Fidelity, Schwab, etc.) presumably do as well. You could start by looking in Morningstar (or on the individual companies' websites) to find what the similarities and differences are among these funds. That can help answer some important questions: I personally just shove a certain percentage of my portfolio into non-US stocks and bonds, and of that allocation a certain fraction goes into "established" economies and a certain fraction into "emerging" ones. I do all this with just a few basic index funds, because the indices make sense (to me) and index funds cost very little.

You can often convert the outstanding balance of a HELOC into a fixed-rate home equity loan, generally with the same bank. Doing this can open possibilities to extend the term allowing for lower monthly payments, but resulting in a larger overall payoff cost. Most HELOCs allow for an interest-only payment or in some cases no-payment at all if you still have unused available credit. Not advising that you do this. If you are struggling with the size of the payment converting to a fixed-rate, fixed-term loan may be what you need. The key will be getting the term such that you can manage both the principal and interest that will be included in the payment.

The literal answer to your question 'what determines the price of an ETF' is 'the market'; it is whatever price a buyer is willing to pay and a seller is willing to accept. But if the market price of an ETF share deviates significantly from its NAV, the per-share market value of the securities in its portfolio, then an Authorized Participant can make an arbitrage profit by a transaction (creation or redemption) that pushes the market price toward NAV. Thus as long as the markets are operating and the APs don't vanish in a puff of smoke we can expect price will track NAV. That reduces your question to: why does NAV = market value of the holdings underlying a bond ETF share decrease when the market interest rate rises? Let's consider an example. I'll use US Treasuries because they have very active markets, are treated as risk-free (although that can be debated), and excluding special cases like TIPS and strips are almost perfectly fungible. And I use round numbers for convenience. Let's assume the current market interest rate is 2% and 'Spindoctor 10-year Treasury Fund' opens for business with $100m invested (via APs) in 10-year T-notes with 2% coupon at par and 1m shares issued that are worth $100 each. Now assume the interest rate goes up to 3% (this is an example NOT A PREDICTION); no one wants to pay par for a 2% bond when they can get 3% elsewhere, so its value goes down to about 0.9 of par (not exactly due to the way the arithmetic works but close enough) and Spindoctor shares similarly slide to $90. At this price an investor gets slightly over 2% (coupon*face/basis) plus approximately 1% amortized capital gain (slightly less due to time value) per year so it's competitive with a 3% coupon at par. As you say new bonds are available that pay 3%. But our fund doesn't hold them; we hold old bonds with a face value of $100m but a market value of only $90m. If we sell those bonds now and buy 3% bonds to (try to) replace them, we only get $90m par value of 3% bonds, so now our fund is paying a competitive 3% but NAV is still only $90. At the other extreme, say we hold the 2% bonds to maturity, paying out only 2% interest but letting our NAV increase as the remaining term (duration) and thus discount of the bonds decreases -- assuming the market interest rate doesn't change again, which for 10 years is probably unrealistic (ignoring 2009-2016!). At the end of 10 years the 2% bonds are redeemed at par and our NAV is back to $100 -- but from the investor's point of view they've forgone $10 in interest they could have received from an alternative investment over those 10 years, which is effectively an additional investment, so the original share price of $90 was correct.

Short answer: google finance's market cap calculation is nonstandard (a.k.a. wrong). The standard way of computing the market capitalization of a firm is to take the price of its common stock and multiply by the number of outstanding common stock shares. If you do this using the numbers from google's site you get around $13.4B. This can be verified by going to other sites like yahoo finance and bloomberg, which have the correct market capitalization already computed. The Whole Foods acquisition appears to be very cut-and-dry. Investors will be compensated with $42 cash per share. Why are google finance's numbers wrong for market cap? Sometimes people will add other things to "market capitalization," like the value of the firm's debt and other debt-like securities. My guess is that google has done something like this. Whole Foods has just over $3B in total liabilities, which is around the size of the discrepancy you have found.

Legally speaking, if you do close a limited company, the funds belong to the government ("bona vacantia"). There's some guidance on this at Companies House and there is indeed a substantial amount of administration work to get it undone. Notable excerpts: You should deal with any loose ends, such as closing the company’s bank account, the transfer of any domain names - before you apply. [...] From the date of dissolution, any assets of a dissolved company will belong to the Crown. The company’s bank account will be frozen and any credit balance in the account will pass to the Crown. [...] 4. What happens to the assets of a dissolved company? From the date of dissolution, any assets of a dissolved company will be 'bona vacantia'. Bona vacantia literally means “vacant goods” and is the technical name for property that passes to the Crown because it does not have a legal owner. The company’s bank account will be frozen and any credit balance in the account will be passed to the Crown. [...] Chapter 3 - Restoration by Court Order The registrar can only restore a company if he receives a court order, unless a company is administratively restored to the register (see chapter 4). Anyone who intends to make an application to the court to restore a company is advised to obtain independent legal advice. [...] Chapter 4 - Administrative Restoration 1. What is Administrative Restoration? Under certain conditions, where a company was dissolved because it appeared to be no longer carrying on business or in operation, a former director or member may apply to the registrar to have the company restored. [...]

Checks (in the US, anyway) are only good for six months after they have been written. After that. under the US Uniform Commerical Code they are considered "stale checks" and banks need not accept them. My experience is that they generally won't -- but you probably shouldn't count on that, either when figuring out whether to try depositing an old check or figuring out how much cash you need to keep in your checking account to cover recent stale checks. The check you now hold is certainly a statement of intent to pay you and thus is a useful document to supplement other evidence that they still owe you the money -- but since checks can be cancelled and/or a replacement check may have been issued, its value for that purpose may be limited. You can try depositing it and see what happens. If that doesn't work (or you don't want to bother trying it) you can contact the retirement plan, point out that this check went uncashed, and ask them to send you a replacement. If they haven't already done so (you might want to check your own records for that), there shouldn't be any problem with this. (Note: Many business checks have a statement printed on them that they're only good for 90 days or so. If yours does, you can skip trying to cash it; just contact the retirement plan offices.)

I stumbled on the same discrepancy, and was puzzled by a significant difference between the two prices on ETR and FRA. For example, today is Sunday, and google shows the following closing prices for DAI. FRA:DAI: ETR:DAI: So it looks like there are indeed two different exchanges trading at different prices. Now, the important value here, is the last column (Volume). According to Wikipedia, the trading on Frankfort Stock Exchange is done today exclusively via Xetra platform, thus the volume on ETR:DAI is much more important than on FRA:DAI. Obviously, they Wikipedia is not 100% accurate, i.e. not all trading is done electronically via Xetra. According to their web-page, Frankfort exchange has a Specialist Trading on Frankfurt Floor service which has slightly different trading hours. I suspect what Google and Yahoo show as Frankfort exchange is this manual trading via a Specialist (opposed to Xetra electronic trading). To answer your question, the stock you're having is exactly the same, meaning if you bought an ETR:BMW you can still sell it on FRA (by calling a FRA Trading Floor Specialist which will probably cost you a fee). On the other hand, for the portfolio valuation and performance assessments you should only use ETR:BMW prices, because it is way more liquid, and thus better reflect the current market valuation.

If you were a business, all your assets would have a dollar value, so when you sold them you'd decrease the amount of assets by that amount and increase in cash, and if there was a profit on the sale it would go in as income, if there was loss it would count as a cost (or a loss)... so if there was a profit it would increase Equity, a loss then it would decrease Equity. Since it's not really worthwhile doing a estimated cost for everything that you have, I'd just report it as income like you are doing and let the amount of equity increase proportionately. So, implicitly you always had roughly that amount of equity, but some of it was in the form of assets, and now you're liquidating those assets so the amount shows up in GnuCash. When you buy new things you might sell later, you could consider adding them as assets to keep track of this explicitly (but even then you have problems-- the price of things changes with time and you might not want to keep up with those price changes, it's a lot of extra work for a family budget) -- for stuff you already have it's better to treat things as you are doing and just treat the money as income-- it's easier and doesn't really change anything-- you always had that in equity, some of it was just off the books and now you are bringing it into the books.

Equity options, at least those traded in the American exchanges, actually expire the Saturday after the 3rd Friday of the month. However, the choice to trade or exercise the options must be specified by the 3rd Friday. This is outlined by the CBOE, who oversees the exchange of equity options. Their FAQ regarding option expiration can be found at http://www.cboe.com/LearnCenter/Concepts/Beyond/expiration.aspx.

In general you cannot. Once the security is no longer listed on the exchange - it doesn't have to provide information to the exchange and regulators (unless it wants to be re-listed). If it was listed in 1999, and is no longer listed now - you can dig through SEC archives for the information. I would like to first point out that there is nothing special about a self-managed investment portfolio as compared to one managed by someone else. With some exceptions, you can put together exactly the same investment portfolio yourself as a professional investor could put together for you. Not uncommonly, too, at a lower cost (and remember that cost is among the, if not the, best indicator(s) of how your investment portfolio will perform over time). Diversification is the concept of not "putting all your eggs in one basket". The idea here is that there are things that happen together because they have a common cause, and by spreading your investments in ways such that not all of your investments have the same underlying risks, you reduce your overall risk. The technical term for risk is generally volatility, meaning how much (in this case the price of) something fluctuates over a given period of time. A stock that falls 30% one month and then climbs 40% the next month is more volatile than one that falls 3% the first month and climbs 4% the second month. The former is riskier because if for some reason you need to sell when it is down, you lose a larger portion of your original investment with the former stock than with the latter. Diversification, thus, is reducing commonality between your investments, generally but not necessarily in an attempt to reduce the risk of all investments moving in the same direction by the same amount at the same time. You can diversify in various ways: Do you see where I am going with this? A well-diversed portfolio will tend to have a mix of equity in your own country and a variety of other countries, spread out over different types of equity (company stock, corporate bonds, government bonds, ...), in different sectors of the economy, in countries with differing growth patterns. It may contain uncommon classes of investments such as precious metals. A poorly diversified portfolio will likely be restricted to either some particular geographical area, type of equity or investment, focus on some particular sector of the economy (such as medicine or vehicle manufacturers), or so on. The poorly diversified portfolio can do better in the short term, if you time it just right and happen to pick exactly the right thing to buy or sell. This is incredibly hard to do, as you are basically working against everyone who gets paid to do that kind of work full time, plus computer-algorithm-based trading which is programmed to look for any exploitable patterns. It is virtually impossible to do for any real length of time. Thus, the well-diversified portfolio tends to do better over time.

Did it show just your address, or was your name on it as well? You didn't share how long you've lived at the address either, so it makes me wonder whether a former tenant is the one who filed that paperwork. It's also possible that someone used your address when making a filing. Whether that was deliberate or accidental is hard to discern, as is their intent if it was intentional. It could be accidental -- someone picked "CA" for California when they meant to pick "CO" for Colorado or "CT" for Connecticut...These things do happen. It can't make you feel any better about the situation though. You should be able to go online to the California Secretary of State's website (here) and look up everything filed by the LLC with the state. That will show who the founders were and everything else that is a matter of public record on the LLC. At the very least, you can obtain the registered agent's name and address for the LLC, which you can then use to contact them and ask why your address is listed as the LLC's business address. Once you have that info, you can then contact the Secretary of State and tell them it isn't you so they can do whatever is necessary to correct this. This doesn't sound like a difficult matter to clear up, but it's important to do your homework first and gather as much information as you can before you call the state. Answering "I don't know" won't get you very far with them compared to having the best answers you can about where the mistake started. I hope this helps. Good luck!

Let me give you some advice from someone who has experience at both ends - had student loan issues myself and parents ran financial aid department at local university. Quick story of my student loan. I graduated in debt and could not pay at first due to having kids way too early. I deferred. Schools will have rules for deference. There are also federal guidelines - lets not get specific on this though since these change every year it seems. So basically there is an initial deferment period in which any student can request for the repayments to be deferred and it is granted. Then there is an extended deferment. Here someone has to OK it. This is really rather arbitrary and up to the school/lender. My school decided to not extend mine after I filled out a mound of paperwork and showed that even without paying I had basically $200 a month for the family to live off past housing/fixed expenses. Eventually they had to cave, because I had no money so they gave me an extended deferment. After the 5 years I started paying. Since my school had a very complex way to pay, I decided to give them 6 months at a time. You would think they would love that right? (On the check it was clearly stated what months I was paying for to show that I was not prepaying the loan off) Well I was in collections 4 months later. Their billing messed up, set me up for prepayment. They then played dumb and acted like I didn't but I had a picture of the check and their bank's stamp on the back... They couldn't get my loan out of collections - even though they messed up. This is probably some lower level employee trying to cover their mistake. So this office tells creditors to leave me alone but I also CANNOT pay my loan because the credit collection agency has slapped a 5k fee on the 7k loan. So my loan spent 5 years (kid you not) like this. It was interest free since the employee stopped the loan processing. Point being is that if you don't pay the lender will either put your loan into deferment automatically or go after you. MOST (not all) schools will opt for deferment, which I believe is 2 years at most places. Then after that you have the optional deferment. So if you keep not paying they might throw you into that bucket. However if you stop paying and you never communicate with them the chances of you getting the optional deferment are almost none - unless school doesn't know where you live. Basically if you don't respond to their mail/emails you get swept into their credit collection process. So just filling out the deferment stuff when you get it - even if they deny it - could buy you up to 10 years - kid you not. Now once you go into the collection process... anything is game. As long as you don't need a home/car loan you can play this game. What the collection agency does depends on size of loan and the rules. If you are at a "major" university the rules are usually more lax, but if you are at the smaller schools, especially the advertised trade/online schools boom - better watch out. Wages will be garnished very soon. Expect to go to court, might have to hire an attorney because some corrupt lenders start smacking on fees - think of the 5k mine smacked on me. So the moral of the story is you will pay it off. If you act nice, fill out paperwork, talk to school, and so on you can probably push this off quite a few years. But you are still paying and you will pay interest on everything. So factor in that to the equation. I had a 2.3% loan but they are much higher now. Defaulting isn't always a bad thing. If you don't have the money then you don't have it. And using credit cards to help is not the thing to do. But you need to try to work with the school so you don't incur penalties/fees and so that your job doesn't have creditors calling them. My story ended year 4 that my loan was in collection. A higher up was reviewing my case and called me. Told her the story and emailed her a picture of their cashed check. She was completely embarrassed when she was trying to work out a plan for me and I am like - how about I come down tomorrow with the 7k. But even though lender admitted fault this took 20+ calls to agencies to clear up my credit so I could buy a house. So your goal should be:

In highly developed and competitive industries companies tread a continuous and very fine line between maximising shareholder profits by keeping prices up while making products as cheaply as possible, vs competitors lowering prices when they work out a way to make equivalents cheaper. In the short run you will quite often see companies hold onto large portions of efficiency savings (particularly if they make a major breakthrough in a specific manufacturing process etc) by holding old prices up, but in the long run competition pretty quickly lowers prices as the companies trying to keep high margins and prices get ruthlessly undercut by smaller competitors happy to make a bit less.

Basically, unless you are an investment professional, you should not be investing in a venture in a developing country shown to you by someone else. The only time you should be investing in a developing country is if a "lightbulb" goes off in your head and you say to yourself, "With my engineering background, I can develop this machine/process/concept that will work better in this country than anywhere else in the world." And then run it yourself. (That's what Michael Dell, a computer repairman, did for "made to order" computers in the United States, and "the rest is history.") E.g. if you want to invest in "real estate" in a developing country, you might design a "modular home" out of local materials, tailored to local tastes, and selling for less than local equivalents, based on a formula that you know better than anyone else in the world. And then team up with a local who can sell it for you. Whatever you do, don't "invest" and revisit it in 10-15 years. It will be gone.

In the course of one's spending, it's not tough to find things that are going to be that expensive. A median income is in the $50K range in the US. My wife was practical, not interested in jewelry, and wanted a big house. The money went to the downpayment. The house cost 2.5 years salary at that time. A car, even used, will cost some month's salary. If that $50K earner is saving, has an emergency account, and is on track with their financial long term goals, a week's pay can buy a nice sized TV. A nice vacation can cost a week's pay to a month's pay. Your question is great, although it shows a concern that's typical very early on in one's career. There are related question here about "how can I spend more?" They tend to come from someone living on a student budget that now has an adult's income from a desirable job. The answer is to sit down, list your monthly spending, properly budget a decent portion for savings, and see how much you have for frivolous spending. Keep in mind, it's easier to sock it away now. No house, no kids, etc. When we were first married, we lived on my wife's income (in effect) and socked mine away. The house tightened the budget, as did the kid. In the end, the PS4 is less about the $400 than it is about the rest of your finances.

The "ideal world" index fund of any asset class is a perfect percentage holding of all underlying assets with immediate rebalancing that aligns to every change in the index weighting while trading in a fully liquid market with zero transaction costs. One finance text book that describes this is Introduction to Finance: Markets, Investments, and Financial Management, see chapter 11. Practically, the transaction costs and liquidity make this unworkable. There are several deviations between what the "ideal world algorithm" ("the algorithm") says you should do and what is actually done. Each of these items addresses a real-world solution to various costs of managing a passive index fund. (And they are good solutions.) However, any deviation from the ideal index fund will have a risk. An investor evaluating their choices is left to pick the lowest fees with the least deviation from the ideal index fund. (It is customary to ignore whether the results are in excess or deficit to the ideal). So your formula is: This is also described in the above book.

The Investopedia article you linked to is a good start. Its key takeaway is that you should always consider risk-adjusted return when evaluating your portfolio. In general, investors seeking a higher level of return must face a higher likelihood of taking a loss (risk). Different types of stocks (large vs small; international vs US; different industry sectors) have different levels of historical risk and return. Not to mention stocks vs bonds or other financial instruments... So, it's key to make an apples-to-apples comparison against an appropriate benchmark. A benchmark will tell you how your portfolio is doing versus a comparable portfolio. An index, such as the S&P 500, is often used, because it tells you how your portfolio is doing compared against simply passively investing in a diversified basket of securities. First, I would start with analyzing your portfolio to understand its asset allocation. You can use a tool like the Morningstar X-Ray to do this. You may be happy with the asset allocation, or this tool may inform you to adjust your portfolio to meet your long-term goals. The next step will be to choose a benchmark. Given that you are investing primarily in non-US securities, you may want to pick a globally diversified index such as the Dow Jones Global Index. Depending on the region and stock characteristics you are investing in, you may want to pick a more specialized index, such as the ones listed here in this WSJ list. With your benchmark set, you can then see how your portfolio's returns compare to the index over time. IRR and ROI are helpful metrics in general, especially for corporate finance, but the comparison-based approach gives you a better picture of your portfolio's performance. You can still calculate your personal IRR, and make sure to include factors such as tax treatment and investment expenses that may not be fully reflected by just looking at benchmarks. Also, you can calculate the metrics listed in the Investopedia article, such as the Sharpe ratio, to give you another view on the risk-adjusted return.

My instinct says that there should be no difference. Your instincts are right. Your understanding of math is not so much. You sold $100K at the current price of 7500000RUB, but ended up buying at 3500000, you earned 3500000RUB. That's 100% in USD (50% in RUB). You bought 7500000RUB for the current price of $100K, but sold later for $200K. You earned $100K (100% in USD), which at that time was equal 3500000RUB. You earned 3500000RUB. That's 50% in RUB. So, as your instincts were saying - no difference. The reason percentages are different is because you're coming from different angles. For the first case your currency is RUB, for the second case your currency is USD, and in both cases you earned 100%. If you use the same currency for your calculations, percentages change, but the bottom line - is the same.

If such a formula existed (and worked) then central planning would work since a couple of people could pull some levers to encourage more savings, or more investing, or more spending - depending on what was needed at that particular time. Unfortunately, no magic formula exists and so no person has enough knowledge to determine what the proper amount of savings, investing, or spending should be at a given time. The simplest way is to invest in a few ETFs, depending on your tolerance for risk; assuming you're very short-term risk tolerant you can invest almost all in a stock ETF like VOO or VTI. Stock market ETFs return close to 10% (unadjusted) over long periods of time, which will out-earn almost any other option and are very easy for a non-finance person to invest in (You don't trade actively - you leave the money there for years). If you want to hedge some of your risk, you can also invest in Bond funds, which tend to move up in stock market downturns - but if you're looking for the long term, you don't need to put much there. Otherwise, try to make sure you take advantage of tax breaks when you can - IRAs, 401Ks, etc.; most of those will have ETFs (whether Vanguard or similar) available to invest in. Look for funds that have low expense ratios and are fairly diversified (ie, don't just invest in one small sector of the economy); as long as the economy continues to grow, the ETFs will grow.

This is a common occurrence when somebody has multiple jobs in one year. The employer can't know if you have reached the annual limit. They know to stop when you have hit the maximum for their company, but don't have information on the other jobs. In fact the IRS doesn't let them factor in the other jobs. They have to keep making their payment until you hit the max for their company. When you fill out the 1040 there will be a line that checks that the total social security amount for each person was not over the annual limit. The extra will be refunded when you file your taxes. In the future if this happens again you can adjust your withholding to minimize the overage. For the example given in the question to get the 4K extra sooner, increase the number of allowances on the W-4. You can under withhold federal income tax because you will over withhold social security tax.

Because swing trading isn't the only reason to buy a stock, and it's not the only way to make money on a stock. I do not have the expertise to make advice one way or the other, but I personally I feel swing trading is one of the worse ways to invest in the stock market. To answer your specific questions: In the previous post, I outlined a naive trade intended to make $1,000 off a $10k buy, but it was shown this would likely fail, even if the stock price would have increased by 10% had I not placed the trade. Another way to state this is that my trade would disrupt the stock price, and not in my favor at all. So, that means I'd have to settle for a smaller trade. If I bought $100 worth of the stock, that size of a buy wouldn't be too disruptive. I might succeed and get $10 out of the trade (10% of $100). But my trade fee was $8 or so... To summarize, you are completely correct that even hoping for gains of 10% on a consistent basis (in other words, after every single trade!) is totally unrealistic. You already seem to understand that swing trading on low-volume stocks is pointless. But your last question was... So how do people make any significant money trading low volume stocks--if they even do? I assume money is made, since the stocks are bought and sold. I have some guesses, but I'd like to hear from the experts. ... and in a comment: Then if no one does make significant money trading these stocks...what are they doing there on the market? The answer is that the buying and selling is mostly likely not by swing traders. It's by investors that believe in the company. The company is on the market because the company believes public trading to be an advantageous position for them to receive capital investments, and there are people out there who think that transaction makes sense. In other words, real investing.

In addition to @michael's points I'd also add: * For benefits that you provide for your employees that they can also take advantage of as part of their total compensation package when they shop for their own health insurance, you should also include the cost of those benefits in your employee's total compensation. This is particularly true if your company is smaller and you are providing those benefits to make your company more attractive to potential employees. Think of it this way - if you were to provide a $5000 bonus to an employee each year, that would be included as part of their total compensation. If you provide them with health insurance that costs the company $5000 per year, that should also be included as part of their total compensation. This is more of a accounting / reporting position than an actual cash expense for the company. The IRS publishes a list of common employee benefits and a corresponding cost for each benefit that you can use for this purpose. You'll find the list here: https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/employee-benefit-costs The total compensation that you report for an employee can impact things like the Social Security taxes that you have to pay as well as the employee's ability to contribute to a 401(k) plan (the IRS places a limit on how much of an employee's total compensation can be used for that purpose). Note that the other value that the IRS places on these benefits is for the purpose of estimating the tax liability of the employee for the year. This is calculated differently than the value that you should use for reporting total compensation (see the link above for details). The IRS uses this estimated tax liability as part of its effort to make sure that employees don't under-report their income. For example, if you have an employee that works for you part-time and receives $20,000 in salary during the year, the IRS expects that employee to report $20,000 as their income for the year (this is in addition to any income that they receive from other sources). If the only value that you report for that employee is $20,000 (their base salary), the IRS may accept that value since it's possible that you are reporting the full value of their total compensation. However, if you provide them with a benefits package that costs the company $10,000 during the year, the IRS will use that as evidence that you are under-reporting the employee's income since they know that the employee received $10,000 in value that was not reflected in their reported income. The IRS expects the employee to report $30,000 as their income for the year (again, in addition to any income that they receive from other sources). If you don't want to include the cost of benefits in an employee's total compensation, you have to provide documentation to the IRS that explains why the value that you are reporting is accurate. As you can imagine, this can get a bit tricky if an employee receives a benefits package that is worth $5000 during the year but only works for you for 10 weeks of the year. The IRS guidance on this topic is located here: https://www.irs.gov/retirement-plans/employee-benefit-costs#p2207066700000

There is no easy way to build wealth. There is no get rich quick scheme that will work for most people. Building wealth takes time, effort, and usually some luck. The good news is that with a plan and some hard work you can increase your chances of building wealth. Here are a few things that you can do to start building wealth. These are things that I have done on my journey to building wealth. I would encourage you to start down this path as well. It will not be easy, but nothing worth doing is easy. If it were easy everyone would do it and we know that is not true. So start down this path and see where it takes you. Good luck! Start saving and investing. I know this sounds obvious but you would be surprised at how many people are not saving and investing for the long term. Start small if you have to but start somewhere. The important thing is to start. Do not get discouraged if you can only save and invest a little bit at first. Every bit helps and over the long term even small amounts can become large amounts. I have a separate post about 5 Easy Ways to Start Saving Money. Check it out for some ideas on how you can start saving. Take care of the basics first. By taking care of the basics I mean things like having an emergency fund, having some basic insurance (auto, home, health), and paying off any consumer debt. These things are important because they will help you to not only build wealth but to keep it once you start. It is easy to get caught up in the excitement of making money and forget about the importance of insurance and an emergency fund. Do not make that mistake. Take care of the basics and then use the money that you would have spent on those things to start saving and investing. Once you have taken care of the basics, maintaining them will become a habit and you can focus on growing your wealth. Remember, though, that you want to have an emergency fund and be insured before you start saving and investing for the long term. The emergency fund will help you to not only build wealth but to keep it. I have a separate post about the Emergency Fund that you should check out. It has more details about why it is important and how much you should save. Do not get discouraged if you have to cut back on your spending in order to start saving and investing. I know it can be tough, but remember that this is just the first step on your journey to building wealth. Once you get started it will get easier. I promise. :) Take care of the basics and start saving and investing. Even if you have to take a little bit of time to get started, in the long run you will be ahead of the game. I promise. The idea here is to use the credit card as a loan for 8 months, then pay it off and charge interest on the loans. This is basically using the credit card as a high-tech version of a personal loan from a friend. If you have the willpower to only use this money for the 8 months and then pay it off, and can resist the temptation to run up more debt on the card, then this could work out well for you. You'll pay a bit of interest to the bank, but you'll save a lot compared to a payday lender. If you're tempted to use the money for other things, or you're afraid you'll overspend on the card, then you might lose money. In that case, a personal loan from a bank or credit union might be a better option. You mentioned that you'd be getting 2% on the loans. If that's the total amount of interest you'll pay, then it might be worth it even if you lose a bit of money to the credit card company. (2% is a reasonable rate for a personal loan, by the way. It's on the high side, which is why I think a 0% credit card offer is a better deal if you can get one.)

Yes, there are real estate indices that track specific geographical locations. These are often referred to as "local real estate indices" or "regional real estate indices." Local real estate indices provide a way to measure the performance of the real estate market in a particular city, county, or region. These indices are usually based on the prices of commercial or residential real estate in the area, and they can be used to track trends in the real estate market over time. Local real estate indices can be useful for investors who want to understand the performance of the real estate market in a specific location, as well as for those who want to compare the performance of different real estate markets. There are a number of companies that provide local real estate indices, including the National Association of Realtors (NAR), which publishes the Home Price Index (HPI), and the Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA), which publishes the House Price Index. These indices are based on the prices of single-family homes in the United States, and they are adjusted for inflation. Other companies, such as CoreLogic and Zillow, also provide real estate indices that track the performance of the real estate market in specific regions. These indices may be based on the prices of both commercial and residential real estate, and they may be adjusted for other factors, such as the quality of the real estate. It is important to note that local real estate indices are just one tool that investors can use to evaluate the real estate market in a particular location. Other factors, such as the local economy, interest rates, and population growth, can also have a significant impact on the real estate market. Therefore, it is a good idea for investors to consider a variety of factors when evaluating a potential investment in real estate.

The best way to stay risk neutral when buying a house with a mortgage is to make a large down payment, so that you only need to borrow a small amount of money. Then the house is an asset that can help you to finance your business, rather than a liability. If you borrow a lot of money, then you turn your house into a liability that can endanger your business. You should make your mortgage small enough that you can pay it off quickly, just in case you lose your job or otherwise need the money for something else. A mortgage is a very inflexible obligation - you have to pay it no matter what - so you should make it as small as possible.

As a general rule, you should make a claim whenever the cost of repairing the damage is less than the deductible on your policy. In your case, that means you should make a claim for any damage up to $500, and you shouldn't make a claim for any damage that costs less than $500 to repair. (Keep in mind that your policy may have a separate deductible for different types of damage, so you should check that you understand what the deductible is for each type of damage that you are claiming.) As a practical matter, you might choose to make a claim even if the cost of repair is a little bit more than your deductible, if you think that the hassle of dealing with the insurance company will be worth it to you. For example, if you have a $500 deductible and a $550 repair bill, you might choose to make the claim anyway if you think that you will be able to recover more than the $50 extra from the insurance company. But if you have a $500 deductible and a $400 repair bill, it probably doesn't make sense to make the claim. (Keep in mind that most insurance policies have a maximum limit on the amount that they will pay for repairs, beyond which they will not pay any more, even if the damage to your home is such that you feel that you should get 100% coverage. You should check what that limit is on your policy. It's possible that you will get 100% coverage up to $500, for example, but that the coverage maxes out at $1000, meaning that if you have $1200 worth of damage, you will only get $700 back from the insurance company, not $1200. If that's the case, you might as well make the claim for the $500 damage because even if you get only $500 back, that will be a net gain of $250, which is probably worth the cost of the hassle.)

Generally, after the initial offer period, credit card rewards programs work on a points system. You earn a certain number of points per dollar spent, and those points can be redeemed for prizes, usually in the form of travel services. After the initial offer period, the number of points you earn per dollar spent will be less than the value of the rewards, so long as you are careful about your spending. For example, if you card earns 5 points per dollar, and you spend $100 on it, you will receive 500 points, which might be worth $50 in travel services. If the card charges a $100 annual fee, and you spend enough money on it to earn 10,000 points in a year, then the card is worth $1000 in travel services, and costs $100 to obtain. This is a simplistic example, and doesn't take into account any introductory bonuses, but you can apply the same logic to those. Intro bonuses typically reward you for spending a certain amount of money on the card in the first few months that you have it, and are usually worth substantially more than the value of the rewards program for that amount of money. For someone with a tight budget, intro bonuses are the best way to get value out of a rewards card. They are also riskier, because if you don't spend the money that the bonus requires you to spend, you might end up paying more in interest than you will ever recoup in rewards. If you are careful about your spending, however, they are a great way to get a lot of value out of a rewards card.

To calculate the estimated tax for your hobby business LLC, you need to follow these steps: 1. **Determine Your Income**: - **Total Income**: Calculate the total income generated by your LLC for the year. This includes any income from sales, services, or other business activities. - **Expenses**: Subtract the legitimate business expenses from your total income to get the net income. Legitimate business expenses are those that are necessary for the operation of your business, such as supplies, equipment, travel expenses, etc. 2. **Calculate Your Taxable Income**: - Since your LLC is a pass-through entity (assuming it's an LLC, not a corporation), your net income from the LLC will flow to your personal tax return. You'll need to report this income on Schedule C of your Form 1040. - If you have a lot of deductions, you might also need to adjust your income further to account for these. For example, if you have a home office, you can deduct a portion of your home expenses, as well as any office supplies, utilities, etc. 3. **Estimate Your Tax Liability**: - Use the effective tax rate from your previous year's tax return as a starting point. If your income this year is similar to last year, you can estimate your tax liability based on that rate. - The effective tax rate is calculated by dividing your tax liability (usually the amount shown on line 15 of your Form 1040) by your taxable income (line 14 of Form 1040). 4. **Make Quarterly Estimated Tax Payments**: - If you expect your tax liability to be significant, you may need to make quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid underpayment penalties. The amount you need to pay each quarter can be estimated by dividing your annual tax liability by four. - You can use the worksheet on page 23 of the Form 1040-ES to help you make these calculations. 5. **Consider Underpayment Penalties**: - If you're making estimated tax payments, you'll need to ensure that you have enough to cover any underpayment penalties. The penalty is generally 1% of the underpayment, but it can be waived if you pay your tax bill in full by the deadline. Here's a simple example to illustrate the process: - **Total Income**: $10,000 - **Expenses**: $6,000 - **Net Income**: $4,000 - **Effective Tax Rate**: 25% (from last year's tax return) - **Annual Tax Liability**: $1,000 (25% of $4,000) - **Quarterly Estimated Tax Payment**: $250 (one-fourth of $1,000) By following these steps, you should be able to estimate your tax liability and make the appropriate quarterly payments to avoid penalties.

The biggest risk in a 401(k) is the risk of losing your job. Since you plan to change jobs soon anyway, you should take the biggest risk possible - staying in your current job. Once you're out of that risk, you can move your money to a less risky investment.

Having a nationalized healthcare system in theory should reduce the financial risk associated with healthcare expenses. In practice, many people still find themselves with large out of pocket expenses due to deductibles, copays, and especially when they receive services at an out of network provider. Additionally, mental health services are not always included in the scope of services provided by nationalized healthcare systems. So having some cash on hand to pay for healthcare expenses is still important. In countries with a more socialized medicine system, there may be more of a cultural expectation that the state will provide for you, and so a smaller percentage of the population may choose to have a cash cushion for healthcare expenses. In the US, more people are likely to have a significant amount of debt from healthcare expenses because they lack any sort of cushion. Note that in the US, a good portion of the population is insured through their employer, and so they may not personally see the value in building up an emergency fund since they don't bear the full cost of their healthcare. This also means that a layoff could potentially mean the loss of health insurance, and so having an emergency fund becomes more important.

This is an old fashioned rental market. You should charge as much as the market will bear. If you had a tenant that prepaid 2 years of rent, do you think you should give them a discount? Of course not. Yet, that is essentially what you are doing if you offer a 30 year mortgage at 4.5%. The market rate is going to be higher because it is a riskier loan (higher chance of default, etc.) and that risk has to be compensated for with a higher interest rate. You are assuming the risk that the tenant will default on the loan, and if they do, you are assuming the risk that you will not be able to re-lease the property for the same amount. Both of those risks are priced into the market rate of interest. If you offer a 30 year loan at 4.5%, you are going to have to live with the consequences if the tenant defaults.

Contracting income is no different to any other income for tax purposes. You will need to include it in your tax return, along with your other income, and claim any allowable expenses against it. That's the simple bit. The harder bit is working out how much tax you will actually pay on it. That depends on your total income, after claiming expenses, and your tax rate. If you're earning £100 a week from your contract, and that takes your total weekly income up to £200, you'll pay tax on the whole £200 at your top tax rate. If, on the other hand, you're earning £100 a week from your contract, and that takes your total weekly income up to £50,000, you'll only pay tax on the £30,000 excess, at the lower rate. (These are completely made-up numbers, by the way. I don't know how much you're actually going to be paying, or how that will be taxed.) You also need to consider national insurance, which has higher thresholds than income tax. If you're below a certain income, you won't have to pay national insurance on your contract income. You can find the current thresholds in the tax rates section of the government's website. Contracting income is also subject to a 5% health and safety levy, which is worked out separately to income tax and national insurance. More information can be found on the government's website. There are also other possible taxes, such as capital gains tax, that will be triggered by your contract income, depending on how you've invested any profits you make. You can find more information on all these taxes at the government's website, or by asking an accountant. Note that, as well as being liable for income tax on your contract income, if you make a loss on the contract you can't claim that against your other income (unlike a business, where you can make a trading loss against other income). This is because a contract is not considered a separate entity for tax purposes. Edit: It sounds like you're doing this to top up your income from another job. In which case, you need to consider the tax implications of that. If you're below the tax-free threshold, you may push yourself up into a tax bracket you don't want to be in. For example, if you're currently earning £50,000 a year, and you start a contract that pays you £200 a week, you'll push your income up to £53,000, and will pay tax on the whole amount at the higher rate. If you're near a tax bracket boundary, you could push your income over the top into the next bracket. For example, if you're currently earning £20,000 a year, and you start a contract that pays you £100 a week, you'll push your income up to £21,000, and will pay tax on the whole amount at the higher rate. This is a simplification, and the actual numbers will be different, but you get the idea. You can use the tax calculators on the government's website to explore this further. There is also more information on the tax rates page. Note that, if you're doing this to top up your income, and you take on more contracts, you may push yourself into a higher tax bracket. For example, if you're currently earning £17,000 a year, and you take on three £100-a-week contracts, you'll push your income up to £20,000, and will pay tax on the whole amount at the higher rate. This is because, under the current tax rules, you only get one personal allowance. There are plans afoot to change this, but they're not live yet. More information can be found on the government's website. If you're doing this to top up your income, and you have expenses associated with that income, you may be able to reduce the amount of tax you pay. For example, if you drive a company car for work, and you're paid less than the tax-free threshold, you may be able to recover some or all of the loss through expenses. There is more information on the government's website. If you're doing this to top up your income, and you take on more contracts, you may be able to reduce the amount of tax you pay if you can make a loss on at least some of them. For example, if you're doing three £100-a-week contracts, and you make a £50 loss on one of them, you can't claim that loss against your other income, but you also won't have to pay tax on the income from the other two. There is more information on the government's website. Edit: It sounds like you're doing this to top up your income from another job. In which case, you need

Just printing more money is not a solution. Let's say you are in a group of friends and you agree to play a game. In this game, you all start with an equal amount of money, say 10$. Then you decide to print 10$ more, so that everyone in the group now has 20$. What's the point of that? You all still have the same amount of goods and services you could buy with your money before, so just printing more money doesn't get you anything. In fact, printing more money dilutes the value of the existing money, so it actually makes you poorer. Let's say you have a business selling drinks. You start with 10$ and after some time you have 20$, so you decide to print 10$ more. You now have 30$ in total, but the value of each dollar is 1/3 of what it was before. So even though you have more dollars, each dollar is worth less, so you are actually no better off than you were before. And if you were to sell your drinks for 10$ each, you would have to print even more money to make up for the dilution, which just makes you poorer still. That's why just printing more money is not a solution. You need to actually produce more goods and services, so that people want your money in exchange for those goods and services. Then you can use that money to buy the goods and services you need.

Generally speaking, there is no privacy concern as PayPal will send you an email with the details of the transaction, including the sender's email address. The sender can choose to make the email anonymous, in which case you will not be able to see their email address. However, if you wish to ensure that your privacy is protected, you could consider asking the sender to pay you through a different method, such as cash, bank transfer, or a gift card. This way, you will not have to give out your personal information to someone you do not know.

Disclaimer of an IRA is not possible. The reason is that the IRA is a contract with the IRA custodian. You can't disclaim a contract. You can only assign it to someone else. And if you don't want the IRA to go to your heirs, you need to set up a trust to hold the IRA assets. Trusts are expensive to set up and maintain, so you need to discuss with your heirs what their options are. If they are not capable of handling money well, you may need to retain a trustee who will distribute the assets according to the trust instructions. You also have the option of naming a charity as the beneficiary of the IRA. This will avoid the cost of setting up a trust, and the charity will handle the assets according to its own rules, which may or may not be what you want. For example, if you have a favorite museum, you may want to ensure that the IRA money is used to support the acquisition of new artwork, and not to pay the electric bill in the museum. The museum will probably not be able to hold the IRA assets, but will be able to hold the proceeds from the IRA assets. Talk to the museum about what your options are. If you don't want the IRA to go to your heirs, you need to plan ahead and take steps to ensure that the IRA is distributed according to your wishes. The IRA custodian will not be able to distribute the IRA according to your will, but you can take steps to ensure that the IRA is distributed according to your wishes.

When you pay off your mortgage loan, the following will happen (in no particular order): * You will no longer have a mortgage payment. This will make your budget easier, and you will be able to use that money elsewhere. * You will no longer be at risk of losing your home to foreclosure. As long as you make your payments on time, you will keep your home. This is a major stressor for many people. * You will no longer have the possibility of negative equity. Negative equity is when the value of your home goes down, and you owe more on your mortgage than your home is worth. It's a major factor in the current housing crisis, and it's something to be very wary of. * You will build wealth faster. When you pay off your mortgage, you remove the largest debt load from your financial life. This means that you will be able to build wealth faster, and you will be able to retire earlier. * You will be able to help your children with their down payments. One of the best ways to help your children is to help them buy a home. When you pay off your mortgage, you will be able to help them with their down payment, and you will be able to watch them build wealth too. In short, paying off your mortgage will make your life easier, reduce your stress, and help you build wealth. It's a major financial goal that will have a positive impact on your life.

No, you don't have to write the date on the back of the check when you deposit it. But you should make sure that the check is dated, and that the date is written clearly and legibly. Banks will be unable to accept checks that are undated or that have been altered to change the date. (Banks don't want to accept checks that aren't written clearly, but they're more likely to reject a check that's obviously been doctored than one that isn't.) If you're writing the check yourself, be sure to date it clearly in pencil (ink might run when the check is wet, and that could alter the wording of the check). If you're giving the check to someone else to write, you might want to initial and date the back of the check as well, to discourage someone else from altering the check and putting their own date on it.

There are a few different loan options that you might consider to purchase employee stock options, including: * A loan from your employer: Some companies offer loans to employees to purchase company stock. These loans are typically interest-free, or have a very low interest rate, and are repaid through the employee's salary over time. This is a good option if your company offers a loan program. * A personal loan: You can also consider borrowing money from a bank or other lender to purchase employee stock options. Personal loans from banks and other lenders are not tied to the purchase of company stock, and can be used for any purpose. However, they will typically come with higher interest rates than an employer loan. * A line of credit: A line of credit is similar to a personal loan, in that it is not tied to the purchase of company stock. It is a good option if you have a variable income, or if you prefer not to take on a long-term loan obligation. With a line of credit, you only pay interest on the amount of money that you borrow, and you can repay the loan at any time without paying a pre-payment penalty. It is a good idea to shop around and compare the terms of different loan options before making a decision. Consider the interest rate, the length of the loan, and the monthly payment. You will also need to think about the impact that the loan will have on your ability to pay for other things, such as living expenses, in the event that you lose your job or your income is reduced for some other reason.

I'd recommend checking out Freelancer.com. They take a percentage of each project (usually 5%) and also a small fixed fee that ranges from $5-$15 depending on the project. I've found the support staff to be very helpful and the site is very easy to use. There are a lot of other good freelance sites out there, but I don't think you'll find one with lower fees.

It depends on how it is implemented. If the company has been doing well and the bonus shares are a way of rewarding the existing shareholders, then yes, it does increase the shareholder value. For example, if a company's shares are trading at $100/share, and announces that it will issue bonus shares such that every shareholder will receive 2 bonus shares for every 10 shares held (20% bonus), then a shareholder who owns 100 shares will suddenly own 120 shares, and the company's share price will drop to $83.33/share. The shareholder value, however, should be unchanged. The company is still worth the same, it's just that now 20% more shares are being traded for the same underlying value. If the bonus shares are a dilution of existing shareholders, then no, it does not increase the shareholder value. For example, if a company's shares are trading at $100/share, and announces that it will issue bonus shares such that every shareholder will receive 1 bonus share for every 10 shares held (10% bonus), then a shareholder who owns 100 shares will suddenly own 110 shares, and the company's share price will drop to $82.05/share. The shareholder value, however, should be unchanged. The company is still worth the same, it's just that now 10% more shares are being traded for the same underlying value. Note that in neither of the examples above is the company's value increased. The company's value can be increased through increased profits, which can be reflected in increased share price. The examples above merely show ways of increasing the number of shares outstanding. In general, an increase in the number of shares outstanding dilutes the existing shareholders, unless the company has increased in value such that the new shares can be issued at a higher price. Whether or not a company can issue shares at a higher price depends on the market. If the company is doing well and is expected to continue doing well, then it may be able to issue new shares at a higher price. If the company is doing poorly and is expected to continue doing poorly, then it is unlikely that it will be able to issue new shares at a higher price. In your case, since the company's shares are trading at a higher price than the bonus issue price, it is likely that the bonus shares will increase in value after the bonus issue. This is because the market has presumably priced the shares at $100/share because it expects the company to continue to do well in the future, and the company has now issued shares at a 10% discount to that price. A reasonable investor would expect the share price to rise to $110/share after the bonus issue, which would be a 10% increase in value. Note that this is not guaranteed, and the share price could rise to $120/share, or fall to $80/share. The bonus issue itself has no effect on the underlying value of the company, it's effect is merely on the number of shares outstanding. Whether or not that results in an increase in the share price depends on the market.

The 1099-MISC and the K-1 are both reports that you receive (or don't, as the case may be) from the entity that paid you (or didn't, as the case may be). The 1099-MISC is typically (but not always) for payments made to you in 2019 for services you performed in 2019. The K-1 is typically (but not always) for your share of the income, deductions, etc. of a partnership in which you are a partner. If the partnership had $100 of income in 2019, and you owned 10% of the partnership, you would receive a K-1 showing that you had $10 of income from the partnership. If the partnership also paid you $100 in 2019 for services you performed for the partnership in 2019, you would receive a 1099-MISC showing $100 of miscellaneous income. Note that in this example, both the 1099 and the K-1 show $100 of income to you from the partnership, but that's just a coincidence. The numbers don't have to be the same, and in fact, they typically won't be. The 1099-MISC is only going to show income from services performed in 2019 (well, ok, or in early 2020, but that's another story). The K-1 can show income from the partnership's 2019 income, but it can also show your share of the partnership's 2018 income (or 2020 income, or any other year). So the numbers may be the same, or they may not be. You don't need to do anything with this information other than report the numbers that you're given on the 1099 and K-1. If you have questions about where the numbers come from, you should probably consult the entity that provided the report.

If you work in Washington, you are probably classified as a non-resident of that state for tax purposes. You would file a Washington non-resident income tax return, claiming the income you earned in Washington, and you would not have to file an Oregon income tax return. (If you don't have enough income from Washington sources, you may still have to file a return to claim an exemption from the income tax.) You would still have to file a U.S. income tax return, and if you have enough income you may have to pay the Oregon income tax anyway (it's likely that you will if you earn over $100,000 per year). You can probably deduct the Oregon income tax you pay to the U.S. as a state tax deduction on your Washington non-resident income tax return. (You might have to pay the tax first and then claim the deduction later, depending on the wording of the Oregon and Washington income tax laws.) --CAUTION: This is not tax advice, and I'm not a tax professional. I'm just a random guy on the Internet who occasionally looks through tax forms for fun. The tax laws are very complicated, and you should really consult a professional tax adviser if you need tax advice.

Yes, you can deduct hobby expenses up to the amount of hobby income you generate, according to the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA). This means that if your hobby income is $10,000 in a tax year, you can deduct $10,000 in hobby expenses for that year. However, if your hobby expenses are $12,000 in that same year, you can only deduct $10,000, and the remaining $2,000 will not be deductible. It's important to note that the CRA states that "you can only deduct hobby expenses in the year in which they were incurred." This means that if you incur $10,000 in hobby expenses in 2022, but don't generate any income until 2023, you won't be able to deduct those expenses in 2022. Additionally, the CRA states that "you must be able to show that the expenses are really for the hobby and not for a business." This means that you should keep good records of your hobby expenses and income, and be prepared to provide them to the CRA if requested. Finally, while the CRA states that you can deduct hobby expenses up to the amount of hobby income generated, it's always a good idea to consult with a tax professional to ensure that you're taking advantage of all available deductions and credits.

If the U.S. were to default on its debt, it would have significant consequences for the global economy, including the value of the U.S. dollar, interest rates, and stock markets. It's possible that in the aftermath of a default, the value of the dollar could decline, and it's also possible that interest rates could rise. Both of these outcomes could be harmful to the U.S. economy. It's also possible that the U.S. government would take steps to mitigate the effects of a default, such as by refinancing its debt or by imposing restrictions on the use of credit. It's not possible to say for certain what would happen in such a situation, but it's likely that the effects would be felt for a long time to come.

There are many different types of investment accounts, offered by many different companies. Some are better suited for certain goals, others have lower fees, and some have higher minimum balances. It really depends on what you are trying to accomplish. If you just want to save money for a rainy day, a Certificate of Deposit (CD) is a good option. If you want to try and grow your money as much as possible, you will probably have to take on some risk and invest in stocks or mutual funds. If you are retired, you may want to take a more conservative approach and ensure that you have a steady stream of income. There is no one-size-fits-all answer to this question.

There is no one-size-fits-all answer to this question as the most profitable investment for a good retirement in Australia will vary depending on your individual circumstances, such as your risk tolerance, investment horizon, and retirement goals. However, some investment options that are commonly recommended for retirement savings in Australia include: * Superannuation: Superannuation is a tax-advantaged retirement savings scheme in Australia. It is designed to help individuals save for their retirement by providing tax incentives for both employers and employees. Superannuation funds invest your contributions on your behalf, with the aim of growing your savings over time. The returns you receive will depend on the performance of the investments made by your chosen fund. * Shares: Investing in shares of public companies can be a good way to grow your wealth over the long term. Shares offer the potential for higher returns than savings accounts or bonds, but they are also more volatile and carry a higher level of risk. It is important to diversify your share investments across different sectors and companies to reduce risk. * Property: Investing in property can be a good source of income and capital growth. You can invest in property directly by buying and renting out a property, or you can invest in property funds, which pool money from investors to buy and manage a portfolio of properties. Property investments are generally considered to be less volatile than share investments, but they still carry some risk. * Fixed interest: Fixed interest investments, such as bonds, provide a regular income stream and are considered to be lower risk than shares. They are often used to provide a ballast to more risky investments, such as shares, in a portfolio. * Cash: Keeping some of your money in cash, or in low-risk investments such as savings accounts, can provide a safety net in case of unexpected expenses or changes to your retirement plans. It is important to remember that all investments carry some level of risk, and it is important to diversify your investments to reduce overall risk. You should also ensure that you have a suitable level of insurance in place to protect against unexpected events, such as illness or accidents. Finally, it is a good idea to review your investment and insurance arrangements regularly to ensure that they are still suitable for your changing circumstances.

So you're saying that a debt collection agency has sent a letter to you, asking for money? That's unusual - typically, if a debt is being collected by a third party, it's because the original creditor has either gone out of business, or has sold the debt on to the collection agency. In the UK, you have the right to be informed who owns the debt you're being asked to pay - you can ask the debt collection agency what the debt is for, and they must tell you. You also have the right to ask for evidence that you actually owe the money - again, they must provide this to you. If you're unable to get the debt collection agency to provide you with sufficient information about the debt, you can ask the County Court to provide you with a sample form of claim which you can use to obtain a County Court Judgement against your boss. Once you have a County Court Judgement, you can use this to get the debt collection agency to stop harassing you (and to provide you with evidence that you actually do owe the money). Even if you're unable to get a County Court Judgement, though, you still have the right to be free from harassment. If you're being harassed by a debt collection agency, you should report this to the Information Commissioner's Office.

You can lend your stocks out to short sellers, but you need to go through a broker to do so. The broker will lend your stocks to the short seller, who will then sell the stocks. The broker will pay you a fee for lending your stocks out. This fee is in addition to any dividends the stocks may pay. Note that you cannot simply lend out your stocks and expect the short seller to return them when they are done. The short seller will sell the stocks to someone else, and that person will sell them to someone else, and so on. The original owner of the stocks will only be paid a fee and will not receive any proceeds from the sale. The person who buys the stocks from the short seller will pay the short seller a fee, and the short seller will pay you a fee, and the original owner will receive nothing. This is why you need to go through a broker. The broker will ensure that the short seller returns the stocks to you when they are done. The broker will also keep track of the fees that are owed to you and will pay you those fees.

There is no fundamental relationship between the two. It is possible to have a perfectly healthy company with no share class that pays regular dividends. It is also possible to have a company with lots of share classes that pays no dividends at all. Dividends are paid out of the company's profits. If the company is doing well, it may choose to pay a dividend to its shareholders. If the company is doing badly, it may not have any profits to pay out and thus no dividend. There may also be a legal reason to pay a dividend in some cases. For example, a recent article suggests that Apple is sitting on too much cash and should pay it out to its shareholders. (I have not read the article, so I cannot comment on the validity of the claim.) In a perfectly healthy company, the profits will be reinvested into the company to improve efficiency and expand the company's operations. This is more important in growing companies, but even established companies will do this to stay competitive. These improvements in efficiency and expansion of operations will, in turn, increase the company's profits. The increase in profits may be enough to pay regular dividends to shareholders. It is also possible that the company will decide to pay a special dividend if it has, for example, sold a subsidiary for a profit. (Think of it as a profit distribution to the owners of the company.) In a healthy company, it is possible that the board of directors will decide that the company should pay regular dividends to its shareholders. It is also possible that the same board of directors will decide that the company should not pay dividends and instead use the money to, for example, expand into a new market.

There is no specific name for this type of insurance, but you are looking for a property insurance policy. A property insurance policy can be written to only cover the structure of the building (this would be similar to the insurance you have for your car), or it can be written to cover the structure and the contents (similar to adding liability coverage to your auto policy). If you are looking to only insuring the structure of the building, then you would need to make sure that the policy you select does not include coverage for the contents of the building. This is important because, as you mentioned, you already have coverage for the contents of the building (by virtue of being listed as a loss payee on the mortgage). If the property insurance policy also includes coverage for the contents of the building, then you would have duplicate coverage--which is typically not allowed (i.e. your property insurance policy would not be written to include the contents of the building).

You can request a partial withdrawal of your unsettled funds. The funds will be held until the transaction is settled. Once the transaction is settled you will receive the funds in your account. At that time you may withdraw the funds.

The Federal Reserve Bank publishes exchange rate data in their H.10 release. It is daily, not hourly, and a bit delayed, but free. http://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/hist/dat00_eu.htm If you need hourly data, you will probably have to pay for it. XE.com and Bloomberg are two sources that come to mind, probably among others.

The value of the holdings in your portfolio are calculated in USD. So if you have 100 shares of a stock, and the stock price is 10 EUR, your position is worth 1,000 EUR. If the USD rate against the EUR changes, so does the value of your position, even though the number of shares and the share price haven't changed. Google Finance, and other finance sites, will usually show you the value of the position in the currency that you are viewing the portfolio in. So if you are looking at the portfolio in EUR, it will show the value in EUR. If you want to see the value in USD, you will have to do the conversion yourself.

There are different ways to calculate the value of an index. For example, the S&P 500 can be calculated as an arithmetic mean, a geometric mean, or as a market cap weighted index. Yahoo! appears to offer all three calculations for the S&P 500. Other indexes may only be available as a market cap weighted index. You should check to see if all of the values that you are comparing are calculated the same way.

There is no fundamental reason to buy a bond that offers no yield, since that means you are buying it at face value. You are not losing money on the purchase, but you are also not gaining anything. It may have some secondary market reason to exist, such as tax considerations or as a tool in a larger investment strategy.

There are 2 main factors that will determine what loan terms you will be approved for: 1. Your credit score. This will determine the interest rate you will be approved for. The lower your credit score, the higher the rate you will be approved for. There are loan programs that have minimum credit score requirements, and others that will accept a lower credit score but at a higher interest rate. The interest rate will also determine your monthly payment. 2. Your debt-to-income ratio. This is calculated by dividing your total monthly debt by your total monthly income. Lenders want to see that you have a low debt-to-income ratio, generally under 36%. If you have a high debt-to-income ratio, you may be approved but the loan amount will be lower than if you had a lower debt-to-income ratio. There are other factors that will determine what loan terms you will be approved for, but these are the 2 main factors.
