When you go long in a stock, you are essentially betting that the stock price will go up in the future. When you go short in a stock, you are betting that the stock price will go down in the future. Going long in a stock involves buying the stock and holding it, with the hope that the stock price will increase and you will be able to sell it at a higher price in the future. Going short in a stock involves selling the stock and holding the position, with the hope that the stock price will decrease and you will be able to buy it back at a lower price in the future. In both cases, the mechanism involved is the same - you are entering into a contract with someone else to buy or sell the stock at a agreed upon price and quantity. The main difference is that when you go long, you are the buyer in the contract, and when you go short, you are the seller in the contract. In either case, the goal is to make a profit by buying or selling the stock at a favorable price.

It depends on what kind of spending it is. If it is on durable goods, then it is saved. If it is on services, then it is spent. Investment in plant and equipment is saved, and counts as investment. Investment in inventory counts as investment, but if the inventory is not sold, it does not add to GDP. Exports add to GDP, imports subtract from it. If the spending is for consumption, it subtracts from savings. If it is for investment, it does not. It is a transfer of funds from one account to another. It is only when the investment is sold that it adds to GDP. Then it is counted as both investment and consumption, and the amount is reduced from the investment account and added to the consumption account. That is, I think, how it works. I could be wrong.

Investing the rest is a matter of risk tolerance, time horizon, and liquidity needs. A financial advisor can help you develop an investment strategy that fits your situation. They can also help you find investment vehicles that match your risk tolerance. The reason I bring up liquidity is that it sounds like you may be in a hurry to find investment vehicles. This is a mistake. Investments take time to mature, and you can actually do more damage by constantly moving money from one thing to another. (I'm thinking of the taxes incurred, the fees paid, the time lost in rebalancing, etc.) If you have a long time horizon, you can wait for the right opportunity, which may be in 6 months or a year, to make a change to your investment strategy. You'll also be able to take a more relaxed approach to risk since you have more time to wait out any downturns. (And you'll pay less in fees and taxes to boot!) I mention all of this not to discourage you, but to set realistic expectations. Investing is a marathon, not a sprint. You may not get the results you're looking for if you approach it with a short time horizon or a high risk tolerance. But if you have the stomach for it, and the time to devote to it, you can do quite well.

In Germany, there are several ways to invest your money in a halal manner. Here are a few options: 1. Islamic Banks: There are several Islamic banks in Germany that offer halal investment options, such as the Islamic Bank of Germany, Arabische Investitionsbank, and Dubai Islamic Bank. These banks offer investment products that are compliant with Islamic law, such as equity investments, sukuk (Islamic bonds), and commodity investments. 2. Investment Clubs: There are also halal investment clubs in Germany, such as the Islamic Investment Club, which invests in a diversified portfolio of equities, bonds, and commodities in a halal manner. 3. Online Investment Platforms: There are several online investment platforms in Germany that offer halal investment options, such as Kassabank and BayWa r.e. These platforms allow you to invest in a variety of assets, including stocks, bonds, and real estate, in a halal manner. 4. Real Estate: Investing in real estate can also be a halal way to invest your money in Germany. You can consider investing in a real estate investment trust (REIT), which is a company that owns and operates income-generating real estate properties. REITs are a good option for those looking for a steady stream of income from their investments. It's important to note that you should always do your due diligence and research any investment opportunity thoroughly before investing your money. Halal investment options are no exception. Make sure that the investment is compliant with Islamic law and that you understand the risks and rewards of the investment before you invest your money.

Does a growing economy mean the economy is becoming less efficient? Not at all. Economic growth can occur in the context of constant or even improving efficiency. Economic growth, at its most fundamental level, is an increase in the production of goods and services. Improving efficiency is a means to that end. A factory that produces 100 units of a good in 10 hours, then improves its efficiency such that it can produce the same good in 8 hours, has seen an improvement in efficiency. It can now produce 125 units in 10 hours, or 5 units in an hour, a 25% increase in output. That is economic growth. An economy that is not growing is an economy that is not producing more goods and services. It is possible to have economic growth without improvements in efficiency, but it is harder and requires more inputs (labour, capital, natural resources). It is also possible to have improvements in efficiency without economic growth, if the economy is not large enough to begin with. A tiny economy that improves its efficiency can still be far from an economy that is "growing".

Real estate can be a profitable investment if you buy right and rent right. But remember, real estate is a business. You need to buy properties that are good investments, not necessarily good homes. A good home is one that is well built, well designed, in a nice neighborhood, has good schools, and is big enough for your needs. A good investment is one that has good cash flow, appreciates in value, and is easy to sell. It sounds like what you want is a home that is a good investment, which means it will probably have to be a rental property. You want to rent it to a good tenant, which means you probably will have to turn down a lot of tenants who can't or won't pay the rent on time, or who are likely to destroy the place. You also probably will have to pay a property manager to find and screen tenants, and to deal with maintenance calls. And you will have to deal with the stress of having a tenant who doesn't pay rent or who destroys your property. So, to summarize: real estate can be a good investment if you buy the right property, in the right place, at the right time. But it's a lot of work, and a lot of stress, and you might not be able to sell it easily if you decide to get out of the business. And if you just want to make money, there are probably easier and less stressful ways to do that.

The forward P/E ratio is indeed calculated using the current price of the stock. It's a projection of the company's future earnings per share (EPS) into the current price to estimate what the P/E ratio would be if the projected EPS were to materialize. It's a forward-looking metric that investors use to gauge the current market valuation of a company in relation to its projected future performance. The forward P/E ratio is calculated as follows: Forward P/E = Current Stock Price / Projected EPS For example, if a company's stock is currently trading at $50 per share and the projected EPS for the next fiscal year is $2.50, the forward P/E would be 20 (50/2.50). The forward P/E ratio can be useful for several reasons: 1. It helps investors understand the market's current valuation of a company in relation to its projected future performance. A lower forward P/E ratio may indicate that a stock is undervalued in comparison to its projected earnings growth. 2. It can be used to compare the valuations of different companies in the same industry or across industries. For example, if Company A has a significantly lower forward P/E ratio than Company B, it may suggest that Company A is a better value based on its projected earnings growth. 3. It can help investors set realistic expectations for their investments. A forward P/E ratio that is much lower than the historical average for a company may indicate that the market expects the company to perform better in the future. However, it's important to note that the forward P/E ratio is based on projections, which may not materialize as expected. Therefore, it should be used in conjunction with other financial metrics and analysis to make informed investment decisions. Additionally, the reliability of the forward P/E ratio depends on the accuracy of the projected EPS, which can be influenced by various factors such as management's assumptions, market conditions, and economic projections.

If you work for two companies in the same year, each company will make a deposit to your Social Security account. Normally, the total of these two deposits will be slightly more than your annual salary, because each company deposits a little bit extra to cover its own share of your benefits. In the case of an over-contribution, what happens is that the extra deposit from Company B will push you over the limit, even though Company A deposited the correct amount. In this case, Company B will get a credit for the excess amount deposited, and will not be required to make another deposit for you in the future. (This credit can be applied to a future year, if the company wishes.) Is this fair? Well, from Company B's point of view, they are paying their share of your benefits. From your point of view, you are entitled to the benefits for the work you performed. So it would be unfair if Company B were required to pay twice for the same work. (And if you had only worked for Company B, then Company B would be within its rights to fire you for causing them to over-pay their taxes!) What if you quit Company A before the over-contribution was discovered? Then the situation is different, and Company A is under no obligation to make another deposit for you. (They may choose to do so as a good-faith gesture, but they are not required to.) In any case, the amount of the over-contribution will be reflected in your Social Security statement, and will be subtracted from the amount of benefits you receive. (This is another reason that it is important to perform this kind of audit every year.)

If the main reason for refinancing is to avoid dealing with the current lender, then yes, that is a crazy idea. It is not unusual to have a lender that is difficult to deal with, but that is no reason to refinance. Refinancing an auto loan is not a small transaction. There are costs involved, and these costs may be passed on to you in the form of a higher interest rate. It is possible to refinance at a lower rate, but that depends on your creditworthiness, among other things. If you have the ability to pay off the existing loan, then do so, and avoid the refinancing altogether. Paying off an existing loan and getting a new one is not a bad idea, if you can get a significantly lower rate on the new loan. The question is what is the difference between the two rates, and what are the costs of the new loan? If the new loan has a lower rate, great. Refinance. If not, then don't. It is not unusual to have a lender that is difficult to deal with. If this is the case, then it is worth shopping around to find a lender that is a better match for you. There is no need to stay with a lender that is causing you problems. It is important to remember that the main reason for refinancing should not be to avoid dealing with the current lender. If that is the reason, then it is a crazy idea. The main reason for refinancing should be to reduce your monthly payment, or to reduce the total interest paid over the life of the loan. If you are willing to pay a higher rate to avoid dealing with the current lender, then it is likely that you will end up paying more in interest over the life of the loan. This is because the difference in rates between the two loans will likely be more than the savings from not having to pay the current lender. Paying off an investment property mortgage can have tax benefits, as you mentioned. But you should also consider the fact that a mortgage is an interest expense, and interest expenses reduce your net income. Therefore, keeping the mortgage means that you will pay less tax. Reducing your net income also reduces the amount of superannuation (retirement savings) that you can contribute to, further reducing your tax bill. This is particularly important if you have a mortgage offset account, where the interest rate on the offset account is higher than the interest rate on the mortgage. In your situation, with a high income and a small mortgage, the tax benefits of the mortgage are probably outweighed by the interest expense. But if your income was lower, and you had a bigger mortgage, it could be a different story. Note that this only applies while you are renting the property out. Once it is occupied, the interest expense will be reduced significantly, and you should make plans to pay off the mortgage then.

Yes, in my opinion it is a good idea to hire a tax professional, yes. I think the reason people hire tax professionals is because they either don't have the time, or don't have the expertise to do their own. If you feel comfortable doing your own, then you don't need to hire someone. But if you're like most people who hire professionals, then you probably will benefit from hiring someone to do your taxes. ;) As far as cost goes, I think it depends on how complex your tax situation is. My wife and I do our own, and the main reason is cost. If we had a lot of deductions, or investments, or whatever, then we might consider hiring someone. But for us, the savings of doing our own vs hiring someone adds up to hundreds of dollars per year. And that's a lot of money to us. So I think it depends on your situation. If you have a simple situation, then do your own. If you have a complex situation, then you might benefit from a professional.

You can use the following Ticker for NYMEX Crude Oil Front Month on Yahoo Finance: OIL.NYM. The ".NYM" tells Yahoo Finance to pull the data from the NYMEX. The "OIL" is the underlying asset, which appears to be Crude Oil. The NYMEX uses "CL" as the underlying asset for Crude Oil, so you could also use "CL.NYM" as the ticker.

There isn't necessarily any benefit, unless the subsidiary has different business than the parent company, in which case it may have different risks and rewards. Owning stock in a wholly-owned subsidiary is the same as owning an asset of the parent company. If the subsidiary does the same thing as the parent company, then it's redundant to have it as a separate entity, and it exists mostly for legal reasons (limiting liability, etc). In that case, you'd have the exact same benefits and risks as owning the parent company in the first place. If the subsidiary has a different business, then it has different risks and rewards, and you may be able to diversify your risks by investing in the subsidiary. You would need to evaluate if the risks of the subsidiary are acceptable to you, and if the potential rewards are worth the risks. Investing in a subsidiary may also give you more control over the company, depending on the exact structure of the ownership and control.

We had a similar situation a few years ago when the UK was considering leaving the EU. There was a lot of talk about how leaving the EU would cause the value of the pound to drop, and that was a factor in the decision not to leave. So, we bought a house in 2016, and the pound did drop in value after the referendum, but not as much as people had predicted. I don't think there's any way to know whether the value of the pound will go up or down in the future, but if you do decide to buy a house, I think you should definitely get a mortgage in pounds, not dollars. If you can't get a mortgage in pounds, then I think you should wait and try again later. Getting a mortgage is such a huge part of buying a house that I think it's worth waiting for the right opportunity.

Low interest rates on savings accounts are a result of the low rates set by the respective central banks, the Federal Reserve in the US and the European Central Bank in the EU. These banks set the benchmark rates which influence the rates offered by commercial banks on savings accounts. There are several factors that contribute to the low rates set by these central banks, including: * Low inflation rates: With low inflation, there is less pressure on central banks to keep rates high to prevent prices from rising too quickly. * Weak economic growth: When economic growth is weak, there is less need for central banks to keep rates high to encourage borrowing and investment. * Global competition: Central banks also consider the rates set by other major economies, and may lower rates in response to global competition. It's worth noting that interest rates on savings accounts are just one aspect of the overall interest rate environment. While rates may be low on savings accounts, they may be higher on other types of loans, such as mortgages or business loans. Additionally, even with low rates on savings accounts, the money in those accounts may still be earning more than it would if it were just sitting in a mattress.

There are a number of ways to make money with low volume stocks, some of which you may not have considered. Trade frequently. While it's true that low volume stocks have lower liquidity, and thus it may be harder to buy and sell them quickly in large quantities, this also means that you can often make a significant amount of money on small trades. Because there are fewer traders in the market, your trades will have a bigger impact, and you can often move the market quite substantially. This is both a blessing and a curse, of course - it's easier to make money, but also easier to lose it. You just need to be careful to manage your money properly. Go long. Low volume stocks often mean higher risk, but also higher reward. If you believe in the fundamentals of a company, and you think that at some point in the future their low volume will be a thing of the past, then a long position in that company could be very profitable. Think Tesla, for example - when they first went public, their volume was extremely low, but their growth has been exponential. A $100 investment in Tesla when it went public would be worth over $60,000 today, a return of over 60,000%. Of course, not all stocks will perform anywhere near that well, but if you can find even a few that do, your returns will be excellent. Short squeeze. This is a bit of a risky strategy, but if you can time it right, you can make a lot of money. Basically, you want to find a stock that has a lot of short sellers, borrow a lot of shares (this is the risky part), and then sell them into the market. The short sellers will see their bets against you fail, they'll buy their shares back from you, and you'll pocket the difference. It's risky because if the price is going to go down instead of up, you're going to lose a lot of money. This is why it's important to only do this with stocks that you can afford to lose, and that have a good chance of increasing in value. Arbitrage. This is a bit of a misnomer, because it's not really arbitrage if there's only one stock involved. But if there are multiple companies in the same industry, and one has significantly higher volume than the others, it may be a sign that there's an arbitrage opportunity. For example, if you're in the market for biotech stocks, you might find that one particular stock has significantly higher volume than the others. This could be a sign that there's more information available about that stock, or that it's easier to trade with lower risk. In any case, if you can make a good story for yourself about why that stock will outperform, you can make money by buying the low volume stocks and selling the high volume one. Of course, you need to be careful to only do this with stocks that you can afford to lose, and that have a good fundamental story behind them. Be patient. This is a bit of a cliche, but it's worth repeating. Low volume stocks will often move in response to news more than high volume stocks, simply because there aren't as many traders in the market to absorb the news quickly. So if you find a stock that you think is undervalued, be patient and wait for the right opportunity to buy. Don't get discouraged if it doesn't move the way you expect - there may be other traders in the market who see more information than you do, or who have more resources. But if you have a good fundamental story for yourself, and you're willing to wait, you can often make a lot of money on low volume stocks.

Microsoft's explanation of MIRR in Excel is mostly correct, but it's a bit unclear and could be more detailed. Let's break it down step-by-step to understand the Multi-period interest rate (MIRR) function in Excel. ### What is MIRR? The MIRR function in Excel is used to calculate the modified internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. The modified internal rate of return (MIRR) is a financial measure of an investment's profitability. It is the rate of return that equates the present value of cash outflows (negative cash flows) with the future value of cash inflows (positive cash flows), assuming that all cash flows are reinvested at the same rate. ### The Formula for MIRR The formula for MIRR is: \[ \text{MIRR} = \left( \frac{\text{FV of positive cash flows}}{\text{PV of negative cash flows}} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \] Where: - \(\text{FV of positive cash flows}\) is the future value of the positive cash flows. - \(\text{PV of negative cash flows}\) is the present value of the negative cash flows. - \(n\) is the number of periods. ### How to Use MIRR in Excel In Excel, the MIRR function is used as follows: \[ \text{MIRR}( \text{cash flows}, \text{finance rate}, \text{reinvest rate} ) \] Where: - \(\text{cash flows}\) is the range of cells containing the cash flows. - \(\text{finance rate}\) is the interest rate used to discount the negative cash flows. - \(\text{reinvest rate}\) is the interest rate used to compound the positive cash flows. ### Example Let's say you have the following cash flows over 5 years: | Year | Cash Flow | |------|----------| | 0 | -1000 | | 1 | 300 | | 2 | 400 | | 3 | 500 | | 4 | 600 | | 5 | 700 | And you want to calculate the MIRR with a finance rate of 10% and a reinvestment rate of 5%. In Excel, you would use the following formula: \[ \text{MIRR}( \text{A2:A7}, 0.1, 0.05 ) \] Where: - A2:A7 contains the cash flows. - 0.1 is the finance rate (10%). - 0.05 is the reinvestment rate (5%). ### What Microsoft's Explanation Means When Microsoft says "With MIRR, you can more accurately calculate the return on an investment by taking into account the cost of financing and the reinvestment rate of the cash flows." it means that MIRR provides a more realistic measure of an investment's profitability by considering the time value of money for both the initial outlay and the final cash flows. - The "finance rate" accounts for the cost of the initial investment (financing). - The "reinvest rate" accounts for the growth of the investment's returns. ### Why MIRR is Useful MIRR is useful because it provides a more accurate picture of an investment's true rate of return when there are multiple cash flows and a significant difference between the financing rate and the reinvestment rate. It helps to avoid the issue of reinvesting positive cash flows at the same rate as the negative cash flows, which can lead to an overestimation of the investment's return. ### Conclusion In summary, the MIRR function in Excel is a powerful tool for calculating the modified internal rate of return, which takes into account the cost of financing and the reinvestment rate of cash flows. This makes it a more accurate measure of an investment's profitability, especially when there are multiple cash flows and a significant difference between the financing and reinvestment rates.

You could do worse than $2000. Depending on the exact benefits you get, how much you pay for them, and how long you expect to live. $2000 is roughly $350 a month. So if you expected to live to age 60, you'd need to earn 5.5% on 60 years of $350 a month to break even with the cost of the benefits. Many people would consider that to be too low a return on investment. So it depends on how good an employee you are, how much they value your position, and how many years you have before your employment ends. فريبيس. Sure, $2000 is a nice start. But if you're a star employee, they'll likely pay you more than that just to entice you to join them. And if you're a family man, they'll likely pay you less. So there's that. Plus, you get what you pay for. If you pay $100 a month for your benefits, you get $100 a month of value. Is that $100 a month of happiness? Well, only you and your tax attorney will be able to answer that question. I.e. It's really up to you to decide what benefits are worth to you. What would you do if they offered you a 401K plan, but no health insurance? What would you do if they offered you a company car, but no gas? How would you feel about a 5% discount on all purchases for company employees? Would that be a valuable benefit or just a nice to have? The former is harder to quantify, but also more valuable to some people. The latter is easy to quantify, but also less valuable to many people. What are you likely to purchase? A $5000 401K contribution limit isn't as valuable to someone who expects to retire in 30 years as it is to someone else. But a $500 monthly payment to a 401K plan might be just what someone needs. Note that these are just two examples of how a benefit can be valued. Health insurance can be valued at $100 a month, or $2000 total cost. A 401K plan can be valued at $500 a month, or $6000 total cost. So there's that. $2000 is a nice starting point, but it can go up or down from there. In general, benefits are valued at what they actually cost the employee, not what they're worth to the employee. So if a benefit is worth $100 a month to an employee, but costs $50 a month, then it's really worth $50 a month. Why? Because if an employee were to leave and then return to the job in a year, they shouldn't have to pay $100 a month for the same benefits. Note that this isn't to say that a benefit can't be worth more or less over time. A $50 a month discount on a $100 a month service isn't really a 50% discount. It's more like a 10% discount. Why did I mention this? Because it's likely that over time, the value of the benefits will increase. So in 10 years, a $50 a month discount on a $100 a month service might be a 20% discount, and so on. This is why I think $2000 is too high to value all benefits. Most people won't stay in their jobs for 20 years. And if they do, they'll likely be making more money in the later years of employment. So it doesn't make sense to value a $2000 total benefit package equally over a 20 year period. Note that this isn't to say that you should necessarily avoid jobs that offer a $2000 benefit package if you only expect to stay on for 5 years. What I'm saying is that you should probably avoid jobs that offer a $2000 benefit package and other non-negotiable terms for 20 years if you only expect to stay on for 5 years. Don't do this. Just pay off the credit card debt and get the $800 a month back on your loans. That's the best investment you can make. No risk and guaranteed reward. College can invite all sorts of unexpected expenses and opportunities, so stay liquid, protect working capital. If you're going to do this on a regular basis, you should probably just pay off the cards and get the money on the loans now. As @keshlam noted, this is basically what you're doing with the $800 each month. Might as well make the change now so you can enjoy the interest savings and debt reduction benefits. If you're doing this once or twice a year, you should probably just pay off the cards and get the money on the loans now, or when you next have a large chunk of debt paid off. As @keshlam noted, this is basically what you're doing with the $800 each month. Might as well make the change now so you can enjoy the interest savings and debt reduction benefits. If you're cognizant of the risks and willing to accept them, and you do this often enough, you should probably just pay off the cards and get the money on the loans now. As @keshlam noted, this is basically what you're doing with the $800 each month. Might as well make the change now so you can enjoy the interest savings and debt reduction benefits. In general, if you have a large amount of debt, it's best to pay it off as soon as you can. Not only will it save you interest, but it will also remove the risk of having to pay more interest or having to make a payment at a time when you might not have the cash available (because you're spending so much on interest). In your case, if you pay 3% interest on the $800 each month, it will cost you $2400 in interest per year. So even at 2%, it will save you $480 per year. Is it worth it? Only you can answer that. One last thing, be careful of the impact on your credit score. If you're doing this regularly, it will impact your credit score. Specifically, it will reduce your available credit to debt ratio, which could be a problem if you have a thin credit file. Just be sure to pay the bills on time and in full, and to avoid getting into debt again. I hope this helps. Good luck! P.S. If you're interested in improving your credit score, there are a few things you can do. They say that having a credit score of 680 is the sweet spot, so if you can get there, it will open up a lot of opportunities for you. Here are a few things you can do to improve your score: Pay your bills on time and in full. This is the most important factor in your credit score. If you do this, you can skip buying expensive credit insurance. Just be sure to have enough credit available to avoid having to pay interest on it. If you build up a good downpayment (20%), a number of banks would do manual underwriting for you. 20% is the minimum they will do now, due to the risk of having to pay interest on the mortgage. If you're married, your wife can refinance her mortgage to include you, and you can refinance your student loans to consolidate them. There are a number of good reasons not to do this, but if you do it, it will help your credit score. Just be careful, it can trap you in debt if you're not careful. Also, don't ever pay more than you can afford to lose on interest. I.e. if you have a $50,000 mortgage and student loans, and you make payments of $800 a month, it will take you 6 years to pay off the student loans, and during that time you will pay $9600 more due to the student loans. So, it will save you $480 a year. Is it worth it? Only you can answer that. If you think you can make more than 2% on 50,000, then it might be worth it. There are, but they don't have the trading volume or velocity to be considered truly liquid. In Australia, the REITs are much more liquid than the equivalent US product. In the US, the REITs are more like the European product - lots of different asset classes, not just residential. Australian REITs are based on the underlying asset class - be it office, retail, industrial or residential. The occupancy rate and rental income of the asset class is used to estimate the monthly cash flow of the REIT, which is then used to estimate the monthly unit price. Hence, they are more like a futures contract than a true index. This is in contrast to the US, where the REITs are more like the European product - they are based on a basket of shares/units of real estate. The price of the REITs is based on the market price of the underlying shares/units. As the REITs are not based on a futures contract, they are not as liquid - there is no market except for the creation/redemption mechanism on the REIT. REITs are a relatively new way of investing in real estate. They are attractive for investors who want to get into the real estate market but don't want to buy and manage a property. REITs also offer more liquidity than buying a property, as they are based on a market index rather than the price of a specific property. There are a number of different REITs available, each with a different index. Some of the most popular REITs include: ARX (All Real Estate Index), which tracks the monthly cash flow of the Australian REIT market. It is based on the total of the monthly cash flows of all the REITs on the Australia Stock Exchange. As there are no transaction costs, it means that the REITs are being bought and sold at their net tangible asset value. STW (Street State W), which tracks the monthly cash flow of the US REIT market. It is based on the total of the monthly cash flows of all the REITs listed on the NYSE, Nasdaq and other US stock exchanges. Again, no transaction costs are included. ERUS, which tracks the monthly cash flow of the European REIT market. It is based on the total of the monthly cash flows of all the REITs listed in Europe. Transaction costs are included in the index. HTH (Home Trust), which tracks the monthly cash flow of the Australian home building industry. It is based on the total of the monthly cash flows of all the companies in the S&P 200 that are involved in home building. Transaction costs are included in the index.edipus, which is a price index for listed companies in the S&P 200 that are not in the top 400 companies by market capitalisation. It is used to calculate the S&P 200 less the top 400 companies by market capitalisation, which is then used to calculate the monthly cash flow of the S&P 200 less the top 400 companies by market capitalisation. This is a very indirect way of calculating the monthly cash flow of the S&P 200 less the top 400 companies by market capitalisation and is only really used to calculate the monthly index. There are also a number of other REITs that track other parts of the market, such as industrial property, retail property and so on. As the REITs are based on the market index, they are more liquid than buying a property in that sector of the market. However, they are not as liquid as buying an index fund, as there is no market except for the creation/redemption mechanism on the REIT. REITs are a flexible way of investing in real estate. They are attractive for investors who want to get into the real estate market but don't want to buy and manage a property. REITs also offer more liquidity than buying a property, as they are based on a market index rather than the price of a specific property. However, REITs do have some costs. As they are based on the market index, they can only be traded at the pace of the market index. For example, if the market index rises by 10% in a month, then all the REITs will rise by 10% in that month. This means that if you buy a REIT, you are locked into its rise and fall. In the US, there are some over-the-counter REITs that trade at their net tangible asset value, but this is not true for listed REITs. Listed REITs must register with the SEC and have their financial statements audited by a registered accounting firm in the US. There are fees

I'd say, in general, you want to pay down your mortgage and live within your means. If you do that, you don't need to think about risk neutrality. Take the $400K loan, for instance. If you pay 4% down, you have a $400K loan. If you pay 20% down, you have a $320K loan. And so on. So if you just pay down your mortgage, you can live with any of those. But if you're six months out of work, you'd better hope you get a good severance package, because you'll need it. Same thing for any other risks that may occur. Note that I'm not saying here that you should pay down your mortgage with the $200K in your bank account. I'm saying that if you're willing to borrow a little more refinancing your mortgage, you can make a good deal. And if you do that, you don't need to worry about risk neutrality. Sure, if you could avoid the risk of unemployment or other risks, great. But if you accept those risks, and only because you're cognizant of them, you can still have a good outcome. And if you're young (say, under age 30), you can wait to pay down your mortgage, and enjoy the benefits of homeownership in the meantime. And if you're lucky, you may be able to refinance your mortgage later for 10-15 years, so even if you start out with a 30 year loan, you can refinance it later for 10-15 years, and that will save you money. So in my opinion, risk neutrality is achieved by living within your means, and not accepting unnecessary risks. If you do that, you don't need to think about it. I'd add: Don't ever spend money on the mortgage that you don't have. If you're six months out of work, or your job pays you $50K/year, you need to make sure that you have $50K/year available before you buy a $100K house. Don't ever buy a house and then try to make it work. If you can't pay for it, it's going to have to be a short sale, and that's going to ruin for a while. So make sure you have the cash flow to service your mortgage before you buy a house. Having the cash flow is different than having the assets. You may have $100K in assets, but if it's tied up in investments, it's not liquid. You need to have cash in your hand, not assets that may or may not be liquid when you need them. And if you do manage to save up $200K to buy a house, and then lose your job, you're going to need that $200K to pay for your living expenses until you find new employment. So don't ever spend money on the mortgage that you don't have. Finally, you should always have an emergency fund. I don't care if you have a $50K emergency fund, or a $100K emergency fund, or even a $200K emergency fund. Just have an emergency fund. And if you're paying $900/month on your mortgage, you should be able to put $100 a month aside for your emergency fund. Take the $100K loan, for instance. If you pay 4% down, you have a $400K loan. If you pay 20% down, you have a $320K loan. So if you just pay down your mortgage, you can live with any of those. I think it is worth pointing out explicitly that the benefits of a credit card, in general, are entirely dependent upon the fees charged. For a small business, the fee of a credit card transaction is likely to be a far bigger concern than the reward points or cash back. I mean, if your business is small enough, then the $50 or so that you "save" by using a credit card instead of a debit card is likely to be of no value at all. That combined with the fact that you are likely to be paying 2% or so on that $50 to get the reward points, and you aren't getting anything of real value... well, you can do the math on that. You're looking for a really wierd rule. Most of the time, if you're not making enough money in your side business to cover the $1000/year cost of the LLC, you're better off just dumping the $1000/year into the hobby. That's because the alternative is to treat the LLC as a corporation, and that has all sorts of complications if you're not making enough money to justify the cost. In this case, you're already at the mercy of the IRS, so why make it any more complicated than it needs to be? I.e. If you're not making enough money in your side business to cover the $1000/year cost of the LLC, you should probably just go to the IRS and ask them. They will likely tell you to dissolve the LLC. If you're not able to pay the $1000/year for the LLC, you should probably just dissolve the LLC. This is really weird, but the reason it's so is that you're the one who called the LLC into existence. You can't expect the IRS to just take money from you and leave you red handed. You should expect to be able to justify the expense of the LLC or dissolve it. If you're not able to pay for it, that's really your fault. Don't blame the IRS for not letting you deduct the expense of maintaining a LLC that you called into existence. If you're making enough money in your side business to cover the $1000/year cost of the LLC, you can follow the instructions on page 23 of the inputs for how to proceed. In short, you need to treat the LLC as a corporation and file the necessary forms to claim the expenses as business expenses. Note that you're still on the hook for the $1000/year cost of the LLC even if you don't take income from it, so you need to make sure that you have that money available each year. In general, LLC's are not worth it unless you're making enough money from them to justify the cost. In your case, you're already spending $1000/year on the LLC, so you need to make sure that you're getting something for that money. What you should have done when you discovered this was use the $1000/year to offset the cost of the LLC. As noted above, you can't deduct the expense of maintaining a LLC that you called into existence, so you should have dissolved the LLC a long time ago. If you're unable to pay for the LLC, you should have dissolved it a long time ago. Don't expect the IRS to do this for you. When the LLC is dissolved, you should take the opportunity to review your tax situation with a professional tax adviser (EA/CPA licensed in your State). They can help you to make sure that you're claiming the proper exemptions etc. to avoid excessive withholding. Please note that this is not a recommendation to hire a tax professional or to provide professional tax advice. I'm just a random guy on the Internet who occasionally looks through IRS inputs for fun. --- Additional notes: If you're not familiar with LLC's, they're basically a partnership. The "members" of the LLC are the people who own it. They could be your friends, family, or even people you don't know. The LLC is separate from the people who own it, but it's still connected to them in that the members are ultimately responsible for the actions of the LLC. If you're the only member of the LLC, you can dissolve it at any time you want. You can also dissolve the LLC if you add other members to the LLC. Adding members to a LLC is like adding partners to a partnership. They share in the profits and losses of the LLC. In your case, you probably should have added other members to the LLC so that you could dissolve it and not have to pay the $1000/year for it. You can dissolve the LLC by filing a Form 2553 with the IRS. You should always consult with a professional tax adviser before filing any forms with the IRS. They can help you to make sure that you're claiming the proper exemptions etc. to avoid excessive withholding. Please note that this is not a recommendation to hire a tax professional or to provide professional tax advice. I'm just a random guy on the Internet who occasionally looks through IRS inputs for fun. --- Final notes: I know this is an old question, but given that the OP is still active on this site

Short Answer: Length of Time invested and risk should be correlated. From what I am hearing this is pretty good game plan for your age. Minutia: Once you get closer to retirement lets say in 20 years. You might want to treat two lumps of money with different risk. For me at 49 I have a lump of money for 55-70 that carries a lot less risk then another lump of money for when I hit 80. This way I can wait and take Social Security at 70 when it pays the most per month. Then I'll have another pile of money for when my care costs start being very expensive. Or I think most people would benefit from making sure you have the funds you need for the next 5 years in items with extremely low risk and funds you need 6 years out or more you can have some risk tolerance there. Best laid plans though. Había You sound like you have a good plan to me. Conservative investments will probably not bring in as much return but also will probably not fluctuate as much either. I would personally avoid putting money into anything other than cash or bonds or very short term CDs. If you get a good spread in your CDs you can probably make more than a conservative strategy but also will take on more risk. I would also avoid putting money into stocks as well. If you do manage to save enough for retirement you can probably coast on just cash and bonds the rest of your life and never have to work for income. Most people aren't able to save enough to retire so they will have to work for income for the rest of their lives. If you do manage to save enough you will probably want to take some time off to enjoy life and figure out what you really want to do in retirement. Many, many people, including me, have made the mistake of starting to work for income right after retiring. I would also avoid buying expensive insurance with a high premium. I would shop around for a good used car, reliable transportation is a good investment in my opinion. Avoid getting in debt, that's the biggest risk of all. If you do manage to pay off all your debt and have the $50k in investments and expenses $2k/year on it, you can probably make it last for 20 years without ever having to sell the investments. Many, many people have made the mistake of running up debt and getting in over their head. I would also avoid using an expensive mutual fund as your retirement account. The high fees they charge are just to eat up capital gains. I would also avoid investing in anything other than cash, bonds, or short term CDs. You can make a lot of mistakes and still have enough time to recover. Don't worry about making a lot of money, just don't lose it all. If you can just break even, you'll do alright. I would also avoid using a financial advisor that charges a lot of money for advice. You can get good advice for free on the internet. Don't let a financial advisor talk you into investing in expensive mutual funds, or in expensive insurance, or in a 401(k) that has high fees. Do your own research, read the fine print, understand what you're getting into. Don't let emotions or fear tactics influence your decision making. Be a consumer, get quotes, compare, read reviews, get a second mortgage if you can, pay off the highest interest debt first, don't get in over your head, protect your capital gains from the IRS and from inflation. Don't take my advice, I'm just a random guy on the internet. But I do have a lot of experience reading financial literature and paying attention to the market. And I also have a lot of experience doing financial math. So that combined with my common sense approach to money management, that's what I would do. Remember, the most important number is the one that's on your bank account at the end of the day. Don't let the market fluctuations affect you, what's on your bank account is what you can use to pay for your retirement, or to pay for when you need to go to the doctor, or to pay for when you want to take that vacation you've been dreaming about. Just don't ever spend money that you don't have. If you don't have it, you don't have it. Don't get in debt to buy something you don't need. If you do manage to save up enough, in my opinion the best strategy is to pay off all your debt and then start investing for retirement. It is probably true that you don't need to have as much money in an emergency fund if you live in a country with nationalized healthcare. However, the money that you do need in an emergency fund increases the longer you live in the country. In a sense, nationalized healthcare is a big insurance policy that everyone pays into. However, everyone pays into it at different times. So, while it is there to help you when you need it, it is unlikely to help you at the exact time you need it. Nationalized healthcare does not mean that you don't need to think about money when you are sick. It just means that you don't need to pay for it. Personally, I would still have an emergency fund, just in case. In your comment in response to this answer, you ask about the importance of an emergency fund in a country with nationalized healthcare. You say that you don't need as much money in an emergency fund because you live in a country with nationalized healthcare. That is (almost) certainly true. However, as I may have mentioned, nationalized healthcare is a big insurance policy that everyone pays into. So, while it is there to help you when you need it, it is unlikely to help you at the exact time you need it. That is, it is unlikely that you will get sick at the exact time you need to pay for something else that is not covered by nationalized healthcare. I hope that helped. The question is what are you using the emergency fund for? If it is for things that are not covered by nationalized healthcare, then you still need an emergency fund. If it is to pay for things that are covered by nationalized healthcare, then you don't need as much money in an emergency fund. Either way, you should still have an emergency fund. I understand your concern. You say that you don't want to pay for something that is covered by nationalized healthcare. However, the way that nationalized healthcare is funded is through general taxation. So, while it is true that you don't need to pay for something that is covered by nationalized healthcare, you do need to pay taxes to fund it. I hope that helped. Hmm... Well, you could do worse. I'd say $200k is a pretty good start. Especially if you can get $1000/mo gross rent. In general, though, you should be able to charge $1000/mo for a $200k house. Even if you have to pay $120k/yr for your mortgage, you should still be able to charge $1000/mo because the market rent for a $200k house is probably at least $1000/mo. So, to answer your question: In general, you should be able to charge $1000/mo for a $200k house. This should be plenty to pay for your $120k/yr mortgage. And leave you some nice profit. I'd encourage you to check Zillow and/or Trulia for the actual rental market data in your area. Zillow is probably more accurate, but Trulia is free. Zillow will show you the median rent for a $200k house. I'd use that as your guide, but maybe adjust it up a bit to account for your higher property tax. Don't worry about adjusting it up too much and losing tenants. It's better to have a slight overestimation of your rental income than to have to pay your mortgage and leave you red. Good luck! In general, when I run the numbers, I come up with a $1200/mo gross rent for a $200k house. That's with a $320k/yr mortgage at 30%. So, you should be able to charge $1000/mo and make plenty. I'd encourage you to get a spreadsheet and do the calculations yourself. Using the future value (FV) of the annuity (A) formula is the best way to do the calculations IMO. Here's a link to a good website: http://www.futurevalue.com/ If you're not comfortable with spreadsheets, you should probably stick to managing your own money. And renting out your property. In general, when you're dealing with personal finance, you should always have a cushion of cash on hand. In your case, that would be $16,000 (3 months of your $1000/mo rent). This is so that if you have a run of bad luck, or make a few mistakes, you don't lose your shirt. And your down payment. And your ability to live. It happened to a lot of people. Don't make the mistake of thinking that because you're renting out your property you don't need to think about managing your money. You do. Cash is king. Always have enough cash on hand to pay for your expenses. And a good rule of thumb is to keep your cash readily available for 3 months of your highest possible monthly expense. Keep in mind though, in most cases, if you do manage to lose everything, you can always go to work for a living. And if you're willing to borrow a few thousand dollars, you can rent out your property for a few months while you get back on your feet. And if you're willing to sell your property, you can use the equity to purchase a lesser sized house for a lower monthly payment. There are always options. Keep your cash readily available, but don't let it scare you away from investing in real estate. Real estate is still one of the best investments out there in my opinion. And if you're willing to learn about property management and willing to take on the risks and rewards of owning a rental property, you can make a good living at it. And owning a rental property is a good way to build wealth and equity. But you should do it because you're passionate about it, and willing to put in the hours of study and work to make it a success. And if you can in turn inspire and motivate others to take on the challenge and rewards of owning a rental property, you can leave a positive mark on the world. And that's something to feel good about. gabriel In general, when you're looking to purchase an investment property, you should always have a partner. Someone else who's willing to put in the hours of study and work to make it a success. And preferably, someone who's willing to partner with you on the emotional rollercoaster ride that is owning a rental property. And if you can find a property that's a good fit, and at a price you can afford, and you have the cash to purchase it

Unfortunately I believe you are out of luck. While I am sure that a contractor in your area would be able to give you a ball park percentage that is likely applicable to your situation, without knowing the exact details of how you would phrase your question it would be impossible to give you a definitive answer. As @keshlam noted in a comment above, this is likely to be an assumptive answer to a question that is asked. While I am sure that the person asking the question is intelligent enough to figure out the tax implications of his side business, he may be required to do more than just provide a simple formula to get an answer that is both accurate and complete. I would advise the person asking the question to consult a CPA or tax attorney for more details. They will be able to take into account the full scope of his side business to determine the tax implications. It will probably involve more than just filling out a 1040. Depending on the results of the tax attorney's work, the person asking the question may need to adjust the way he does his side business or the amounts he receives from it. For example, if a significant portion of the income from the side business comes from expenses that are not ordinary and necessary, then the person asking the question may need to change the way he does business to be more of a cash business. Or, he may need to keep better records and do more accounting work to support the expenses as business related. There may also be implications for how the side business is structured. For example, if the side business is a sole proprietorship, then there are probably limits on how much income it can generate before the person asking the question is required to file a Form 2210. There may also be state income tax implications. If the side business is a corporation, then there are probably more compliance issues, and possibly even criminal charges if there is an accident. Again, a tax attorney can help to sort out the details. Disclaimer: I know very little about taxes. I know more about the implications of not paying them. So my advice on this matter should be taken with a grain of salt. In general, side businesses are not well-loved by the IRS. If you do enough work, they may not even need to issue you a 1099. So you should definitely not expect to see one coming in the mail any time soon. If you want to know how much income you are generating, I would advise you to keep track of expenses and do your own accounting work. This will also help you to determine the tax implications of the side business. As @keshlam noted in a comment above, this answer is probably too broad. It is really about how the IRS will tax a side business. It is not really about how much of a headache it will cause the person asking the question. If that person is looking to maximize his ability to make money with the side business, then he will want to do it in a way that minimizes his tax liability. This may involve keeping good records, doing accounting work, and possibly even consulting a CPA or tax attorney. It may also involve changing the way the side business is done to be more cash-like, or to issue less 1099's. There are many many many financial institutions that can help a person to do more business, but the tax implications need to be carefully considered. If someone is interested in opening a side business, I would advise them to do research on how to do it legally and properly. There are many good books and articles on this topic. If you are already in business, you can probably just do it as a sole proprietor. There are forms to file with the IRS to do a sole proprietorship, but you may have to do more work to comply with state laws. Again, there are resources available to help people do this. If you want to limit your legal liability, or work with a partner, you may want to look into opening a corporation. This is probably beyond the scope of this question, but many people do this to limit liability. You can probably open a corporation with just a few hundred dollars, but to do it properly and legally you will probably need to hire a CPA or tax attorney. In general, side businesses are a lot like regular businesses. They have the potential to generate a lot of income, but they also create a lot of work and expense. If you are not able to do the work yourself, you will need to hire someone else to do it, which will cost you more money. This is the basic concept surrounding all businesses. So, once you get past the initial setup costs, the income statement for a side business will probably resemble that of a regular business. That is, it will likely be a lot of busy work for

This is a complex question that has been thoroughly picked apart on this site. I will only attempt to summarize the main points. ************************************* The good reasons are: ************************************* 1) It is hard to control inflation: You may be able to control the printing of money, but it is hard to control where that money goes and what it is used for. 2) It is hard to control who gets the money: You may be able to control the printing of money, but it is hard to control who gets the money and what they do with it. 3) Externally-imposed controls: Many countries have signed treaties that limit their ability to print money. They may have promised to keep inflation under control, or to only print money for essential needs. 4) Domestic political instability: If there is a lot of corruption, or if the government is not able to collect taxes, it may be hard to keep control of the situation. 5) The ".": Many countries have a lot of external debt, and so are not able to print money without causing default on that debt. 6) The risk of default: If a government prints money, it increases the risk of default on that money. It may be hard to attract foreign investment if you have high levels of inflation or if there is a lot of corruption. 7) Alternative uses for money: There are alternative uses for money that do not involve spending it, such as investing it or exporting goods from your country. If your country has a lot of natural resources, it may be able to earn income by exporting goods from your country. There are many ways to structure an economy to maximize exports. One of the reasons that the United States dollar is still used in so many transactions is that it is a "safe" currency, and using it for exports is a low-cost way to get your goods to market. Many Japanese movies and TV shows depict Western countries as being very corrupt, and so they may be reluctant to adopt the Western practice of using dollars for exports. There are also religious objections to using money for exports, since it is seen as taking money out of the hands of the poor. These are all factors that may go into the decision of whether or not to use dollars for exports. If a country does not have a lot of natural resources, it may not be able to export goods and so will likely have to rely on foreign aid to pay for imports. Foreign aid may also be used to pay for things like road construction, dams, and so on. These are all things that can help a country to develop. However, there is often corruption in the way that foreign aid is distributed, and so it is not always the best investment. It is important to note that foreign aid is not a gift. It is money that is given to a country to help it develop, but which does not have to be paid back. Some countries may disagree with the statement that foreign aid is not a gift, but the vast majority of economists would agree. Foreign aid is an investment in a country's future. It is hoped that by investing in a country now, you are helping to create a more prosperous future for that country. There are many ways to structure a foreign aid program. One of the simplest ways is to just give the money to the recipient country and tell them to use it as they see fit. This is called "budget support". Many countries provide some social programs that are funded by foreign aid. A common example is a program that provides free or low-cost access to health care for pregnant women. These programs are often called "vertical programs" because they only benefit a small part of the population. There are also programs that provide access to education for children, and so on. These programs are usually funded by a combination of foreign aid and money that is raised by the private sector. Many charities waste most of a donation trying to get it to the intended recipient. A lot of money that is given to charities ends up being used to pay executive bonuses. Charities that are involved in delivering aid programs in a developing country may waste a lot of money getting aid to the people that need it. There are also a lot of middlemen who will take a percentage "discount" on every dollar that passes through their hands. Many governments also take a percentage "discount" on every dollar that is given to the private sector. This is called "corruption". There are many ways to fight corruption. One way is to simply refuse to give money to governments that are not transparent about how they will use the money. Another way is to demand that governments put all financial transactions on a bank account that can be audited. Auditing a bank account is like having a financial statement of every transaction that goes into or out of the account. It is a very effective way to catch

I think it's worth pointing out explicitly that while PayPal does a good job at protecting you from fraud, it's based on the information you give them. In other words, if you're not careful, they can't do much to help you later. For example, if you give them your bank account number, they can't do much to help you if someone else gets ahold of it. They can't reverse the charges, and they can't guarantee that your account will be safe from fraud. Your IRA custodian will be able to take care of most of this for you. You can usually have the IRA account designated as such that the account will distribute the assets according to the terms of a will. This will save you the cost of having to open a new account. In any case, you should consult with a lawyer who specializes in estate law. They will be able to advise you on the best course of action in your specific situation. Please note that this advice is given in good faith, but without any knowledge of your specific situation. It is always best to consult with a qualified professional. If you have a will, and you want the IRA to be distributed according to its terms, then you should definitely have the IRA account set up as a revocable living trust. The IRA custodian can do this for you. You would need to provide the IRA custodian with a copy of the will. WARNING: if you do this, then the IRA assets are no longer considered to be in your IRA account, and will not count towards your IRA balance. This should not really be an issue, since the assets are still yours, and you can contribute them to your IRA at any time you wish. If you don't have a will, then you can still have the IRA assets distributed according to your wishes. You would need to work with a lawyer to draft a new will, and then the IRA custodian can execute it. Again, this would cause the assets to no longer be considered to be in your IRA account. If you are married, and your wife also has a IRA, then she has the ability to make a joint account that will distribute the assets according to your joint will. This would allow you to keep the assets in your IRA account, but still have them be distributed according to your wishes. However, if you are not married, then you will probably not be able to keep all of the assets in your IRA account. In any case, you should always consult with a lawyer who specializes in estate law. They will be able to advise you on the best course of action in your specific situation. Please note that this advice is given in good faith, but without any knowledge of your specific situation. It is always best to consult with a qualified professional. In addition to the excellent advice you received, there is another point to consider. In the US, there is no need to have a will to distribute assets. Intestate assets (i.e. assets that do not have a will) are distributed according to state law. In most of the US, this will be 50% to your spouse, and 50% to your children. This is called "intestate distribution". Even if you are not married, and do not have a will, your assets will still be distributed according to some state law. However, it is possible that if you are not married, then the intestate distribution rules will not apply, and you might have to go to court to determine the distribution of your assets. This is another example of the complexities of US intestate law. In any case, as I mentioned above, it is always best to consult with a lawyer who specializes in estate law. They will be able to advise you on the best course of action in your specific situation. Please note that this advice is given in good faith, but without any knowledge of your specific situation. It is always best to consult with a qualified professional. In addition to the excellent advice you received, there is another point to consider. IRS Publication 590 (which is titled "How to Apply for and Receive an IRA") states the following: If you are married and live with your spouse in a community property state, half of any income defined by state law as community income may be considered yours. Community income includes income from property, including pensions and annuities, and income from self-employment too, as well as commissions, alimony etc. If you are not married, then you are considered a single taxpayer, and your income is all considered yours. However, if you are in a community property state and not married, then the state laws on community property may override the IRS rules, and half of the IRA assets may be considered yours. This is another example of the complexities of IRA rules. In any case, you should always read IRS Publication 590 (or the equivalent publication for your IRA provider) and review the sections on contributions, deductions, and distributions. You should also be sure that you are claiming the proper exemptions etc. In your case, it is possible that if you are not married, then the IRA rules will require you to redesignate the IRA account to be a Roth IRA account. This is because the rules for Roth IRAs are more restrictive than the rules for traditional IRAs. Specifically, you are required to have $550,000 or more in the Roth IRA account, and you are forbidden from making any contributions to the Roth

You'd be paying two mortgages. First, you'd pay the remaining balance on your current mortgage. Then, you'd pay the full amount of your new mortgage. If you have the income to service two mortgages, great. But, if you're maxed out on your current mortgage, then you may have a problem. One option is to refinance your current mortgage for 15 years. Then, you can refinance your new mortgage for 30 years. The problem is that you'll pay two mortgage companies to make your payments. I'd shop around to find the best deal. Sure, you could pay off your mortgage, but why would you do that? It does not make sense to pay off a 30 year mortgage with a 15 year mortgage. You'd be better off paying off the 15 year mortgage with the 30 year mortgage. Also, you'd get a lower payment each month if you paid off the 15 year mortgage first. I hope this helps. Good luck! P.S. If you're six months out of unemployment, you should probably wait before buying a new house. It's not that you can't do it, but it is wise to wait. If you lose your job, you'll have to pay two mortgages and a car payment. It would be wise to wait until you've been employed for a year or two before buying a new house. That way, if you do lose your job, you only have to pay one mortgage and a car payment. And, you'll probably be able to refinance your mortgage later for 30 years at a low rate. But, if you buy a house right away, you'll have to pay two mortgages and a car payment, and it will be harder to refinance later for 30 years. P.P.S. If you do manage to pay off your mortgage and buy a new house, it will be a lot harder to make the payments if you lose your job. It will be a lot harder to make the payments if you lose your job. It will be a lot harder to make the payments if you lose your job. P.P.P.S. If you want to buy a new house, save up a good down payment. Don't get talked into paying 20% or 30% or more of your mortgage to start. If you can't raise that money, then you shouldn't buy a new house. There are no shortcuts here. You will have to pay interest on your mortgage for 30 years if you don't pay it off. And, you will have to pay property tax on your mortgage for 30 years if you don't pay it off. Don't even think about it. If you want to buy a new house, start saving money now. And, get a good life insurance policy while you're at it. Term life insurance is cheap and will save your family a lot of money in the event of your death. And, it will help a little bit with getting a new mortgage if you have a good life insurance policy. I hope this helps. Good luck! Disclaimer: I know this is an old question, but given the dead link in the above answer, this seemed like the best place to ask this question. Please feel free to move it to the appropriate forum if you like. How would I go about paying two mortgages? It would not be a good idea to do this. You'd be better off paying off the smaller mortgage first, then paying off the larger mortgage. That way, you'd save money on interest and you'd be able to refinance the larger mortgage later for 30 years at a low rate. Also, you'd save money on property tax. Don't even think about doing this. I generally write the date on the back of the check when I deposit it, for my own record keeping purposes. Check numbers are generally not very meaningful - they're assigned by the bank where they're issued, and may be easily re-written or Photoshopped later. So, while it's certainly true that someone may have a use for the check number later, it's not something that you need to think about now. More important is that you keep good records - which is why I also write the date on the check itself (in addition to putting it in a spreadsheet or accounting program). If there's ever a question about the check, having the date on it is going to be very helpful. In general, whenever you're given a check, whether that's from a stranger or from your spouse, it's a good idea to treat it as though it could be fake. That is, don't cash it right away, and make sure that you have a good record of the transaction. In your case, you say that the check was for $1500. So, in addition to not cashing it right away, you should probably also avoid using it for any purchases. I would suggest depositing it into your bank account, and then using that money for your regular expenses. If you do manage to lose or misplace the check, or if someone else cashed it off your account, you'd be out $1500. So, it's not really a big deal - provided you keep good records. (And, uh, don't let your spouse cash it off your account!) (Note: this is not the same as saying that you should deposit checks you receive into your bank account - some people do this, and some people don't. I'm not going to tell you whether or not you should do that. Do what makes sense to you.) Hope this helps. Good luck! P.S. When you do finally cash the check, be sure to save the receipt (in hard copy and electronic form forever). You never know when you might need it. P.P.S. Avoid using check numbers in any way in your records or accounting program. Check numbers are only for your convenience, and may change without notice. So, using them in your records may cause problems later. (Ask me how I know this, if you like). In general, when you're given a check, the best thing you can do is to treat it as though it could be fake, and to make a good record of the transaction. P.P.P.S. If you ever find yourself in trouble because of a check you deposited, or because you cashed it off your account, you should sue the bank that issued the check. They are ultimately responsible for what they put in your account. (This is assuming that you keep good records, of course). P.P.P.P.S. May your checks always be greeted with a nice, cold beer. P.P.P.P.P.S. And may you always have enough beer to share with your friends. P.P.P.P.P.P.S. (I know, I know, I know - another beer pitch. But hey, it's a good one). Have a nice day! (Note: I know this is an old question, but given that the OP is still active on this site, I thought this might be of interest to them. Plus, it's a fun little story.) So, what happened was that I had a full accounting outfit, with all the checks and deposits and balances and accounts and stuff. It was a real pain to have to figure out what checks belonged to which account, and vice versa. So, I wrote a little Excel spreadsheet to help me out. It was a bit tedious to enter all the check numbers and dates and stuff, but it only took me an hour or so to get it all entered. Then I ran the spreadsheet and it spat out a list of accounts and balances and stuff. I was going to use that as a basis to reconcile my records, but then I had a beer and a half and forgot about it. The end result is that my records are still a mess. I guess I could use a bit more organization. Maybe if I had a database and a bit of time and effort, I could get it all under control. But I don't have that time or effort to put into it right now. Plus, the amount of money in my various accounts doesn't really warrant it

If you're looking to purchase employee stock options, there's a good reason they're not standardized. The value of the option at any given time is based on the price of the underlying share at that time. As the price of the share can fluctuate wildly, the value of the option can too. If you're not an expert in judging the value of stocks, this can be a bit tricky. How much should you pay for the option? How much should you sell it for? How long should you hold it? What happens if the stock does well, and then poorly? How liquid will your trade be? For example, if you were allowed to purchase a call option on a public company, but only because you're an employee of a partner company, that option would only be worth something if the public company's stock price rose above the strike price. Given that the public company's stock is currently trading at $100,000,000, it would take a lot for the stock to rise enough to make your option valuable. I've been using Upwork for a few years, and I've also used Fiverr and Thumbtack. Upwork has the largest pool of freelance professionals, and the fees are generally lower than Fiverr or Thumbtack. I've also found the quality of work to be higher on Upwork. The one exception is for web-related work, where Fiverr and Thumbtack may be better. Upwork uses a different payment processor than Fiverr and Thumbtack, so you have the option of paying either with a credit card or by check. I prefer credit card, of course. Upwork does not charge sales tax on its fees, Fiverr and Thumbtack do. So, depending on your state sales tax rate, Upwork may be slightly more expensive. In general, I would say that Upwork is the best site for most people. The one exception is if you're looking for work in the web-related field. I think it's fair to say that a $20 bonus share does not improve shareholder value. After all, it's just giving away $20 per share that could otherwise be used to buy more shares. In a world where all shareholders are equal, this would cause the price of the last share to drop by the same amount (assuming no change in the value of the company). Since most people aren't clairvoyant enough to see how a $20 bonus share might be valued, they'll likely view it as being a bit better off than they could otherwise. This is, however, completely offset by the fact that the company is now worth more. After all, if the company is going to distribute $20 per share, then it's a $20 per share company. This is why I didn't simply have the company buy back shares at $20 each - because it's not worth $20 per share. It's a bit odd that they would do this, but maybe it makes sense to do this to remain competitive in a market where everyone else is selling their shares. I hope this helps. Good luck! I'd like to add on a bit: In a world where all shareholders are equal, selling a block of shares doesn't necessarily cause the price of the remaining shares to drop. In fact, in some cases this may even be true. However, in the case of a $20 per share company distributing $20 per share, it's hard to see how this could be true. I.e. if the company is going to give you $20 for your shares, then it's going to have to come from somewhere - either cash in hand, or more debt, or higher profits next year, etc. In a world where the company is not allowed to do either of those things, then you have a monopoly on the company and can name your price for the shares. Okay, well maybe a bit dramatic. I'd also like to mention that this isn't exactly too different from what Apple is doing. Apple is a $100 per share company that consistently trades at $110-120. So it's not exactly a $100 per share company anymore. In fact, if Apple were to distribute $100 per share, the price of the last share would drop to $100. So it's a bit odd that they would do this. However, it may make sense to do this to remain competitive in a market where everyone else is selling their shares. In other words, if your employer paid you $1000 each month, and then reimbursed you $500 each month, you would only be able to deduct the $500 each month, because the $1000 is considered reimbursement. I assume this is because you could easily play games with the numbers to deceive someone else into thinking you are entitled to more money. I.e. They will likely pay you $1000 each month without question. Then, when you no longer need the $1099-MISC, you can slowly increase your requests for reimbursement, up to the $1500 per month. Your employer will probably not notice the difference and will continue paying you $1000 per month. That combined with the $500 per month that you are now entitled to means that you are receiving $2500 more per year, but your employer is probably not going to care enough to question it.
