If you were working for a corporation, you would expect to see a K-1. The numbers on the K-1 would be the same as those on the 1099-MISC. The difference is that the K-1 will have your employer's Federal and FICA W/H numbers on it, while the 1099-MISC will not. You are in luck, I am a fellow hobbyist (I love to play video games, watch movies, and read books). I just pulled out my old spreadsheet of deductions and found that I could deduct $1500 of my $4500 of hobby income (33.3% tax rate). I also found that I could deduct $500 of my $1,000 of home office expenses (50% tax rate). Home office expenses include any expenses incurred for the exclusive use of a home office (i.e. not for a business). In your case, you can deduct $100 of your $300 of hobby income (33.3% tax rate). You can also deduct $25 of your $100 of home office expenses (25% tax rate). Unfortunately, I think that is the only way you will be able to deduct your hobby expenses. I.e. Home office expenses and hobby income are the only expenses that the average person can deduct. EDIT: You can also donate money to a registered charity and deduct the donation amount (up to $1000) in your tax return. Many charities will match dollar for dollar the donations they receive. Second Edit: As I was saying in the comments, in order to claim the $25 home office expense deduction, you need to have an office in your home that is used exclusively for your hobby. I.e. you cannot deduct $25 of your $100 of home office expenses if you use that money to pay for your business expenses. It has to be for your personal use. In your case, you can probably skip the home office expense deduction (I would guess that you don't have $100,000 of stuff in your house). Final Edit: As I was saying in the comments, there is no need to rush to spend money on your hobby. Take your time and enjoy the process. I.e. Don't feel like you have to buy the latest gadget or software to have a good time. gabriel I would like to add something else to @Chris Rea's excellent answer. In the section about home office expenses, it is worth clarifying that the expense must be for the exclusive use of a home office for the individual. In other words, if you use a home office exclusively for your hobby, then you can deduct home office expenses. If you use a home office for both your hobby and your business, then it is likely that the $25 deduction limit will not be enough to cover your expenses. This is an unfortunate situation as you cannot deduct your hobby expenses (other than the $100 of $300 that you are allowed to deduct) or home office expenses (other than the $50 of $100 that you are allowed to deduct) separately. The only way to maximize your deduction is to ensure that you keep good records that justify your expenses as being for your hobby. You would do well to keep track of the hours you spend on your hobby and the expenses you incur. If you can justify $10,000 of expenses as being for your hobby, then you should be able to deduct $3000 of that amount. Unfortunately, there is no way to deduct the full $10,000 of expenses. Chris Rea is correct in that you should enjoy the process and take your time. I would add that you should also be cognizant of your overall tax situation. I.e. Don't let the potential for a deduction discourage you from pursuing your hobby. At the same time, you should ensure that you are not overstating your case. Be honest with yourself about what you can and cannot deduct. In summary, there are no get-out-of-debt schemes or short cuts. You have to earn your deductions. You can't expect to be able to deduct $10,000 of expenses or to have a $4000 tax bill. You also must remember that if you are not able to deduct your expenses, you should still enjoy your hobby. Life is too short to not participate in activities that bring you joy. gabriel In response to your comment, Chris Rea, you are certainly correct that you should enjoy the process and take your time. I think it's worth pointing out explicitly that the US government cannot default on its loans, or more specifically, cannot repudiate the debt. What can happen, and does happen, is that the US government negotiates lower payments with creditors, and uses the money thus saved to continue to operate the government. This is a very simplified explanation, and doesn't take into account the many ways in which the US government can borrow money, or the impact of not paying creditors. But the bottom line is that the US government remains solvent, and can continue to borrow money to pay for itself. Creditors will probably demand higher payments, and the US government will probably not be able to borrow as low an interest rate, but the debt will still get paid. So what will happen to your bank money? Nothing, really. The bank will probably not be able to offer you as good a return on your savings, but otherwise the bank will still be there to take your money and run your credit card transactions. I hope this helps. This is a complex issue, and I don't think there's a good parallel in real life. The US government is not a very transparent outfit, and it's hard to know what the real impact will be. I think the worst that will happen is that you'll get a lower return on your savings, and you'll have to pay more. But I don't think the US government will ever repudiate its debt, and so you don't have to worry about that. In fact, you probably don't even need to pay your credit cards or bank loans any more. The government will probably not be able to collect enough money to pay for everything it owes, so there will probably be some kind of a debt overhang. In the long term, this will probably not be a major issue, as the US government will probably not be able to borrow money at a sufficiently low interest rate to care about the difference. But in the short term, there may be some unpleasantness as the US government negotiates lower payments with creditors. Again, I don't know much about the US government, but it seems to me that it's not really about numbers, but more about political posturing. The US government may be forced to choose between paying for popular programs, or paying its creditors. The US government may even choose to repudiate some debt, and use the money to pay for more popular programs. It's hard to know what the US government will do in this situation. What I do know is that the US government is not a very responsible outfit, and it's always skirting the edge of default. The US government is borrowing money at a rate that's not sustainable, and it's not clear that the US government will be able to pay for all of its programs, including military spending, social welfare programs, and interest payments on debt. Again, I don't know much about the US government, but it seems to me that it's not really about numbers, but more about political posturing. The US government may be forced to choose between paying for popular programs, or paying its creditors. The US government may even choose to repudiate some debt, and use the money to pay for more popular programs. It's hard to know what the US government will do in this situation. What I do know is that the US government is not a very responsible outfit, and it's always skirting the edge of default. In the long term, I think the US government will probably not be able to borrow money at a sufficiently low interest rate to care about the difference, and so it doesn't really matter how long the debt overhang is. But in the short term, there may be some unpleasantness as the US government negotiates lower payments with creditors. I hope this helps. Good luck! If you are new to options, I would start by defining what you really want to invest in. For example, if you want to hedge your downside, you would want to invest in a put. If you want to speculate on the upside, you would want to invest in a call. If you want both, you can get both. But, you should probably stick to the basics for a while. There is no shame in not understanding the intricacies of option pricing. In fact, if you are not pricing the options correctly, you are at the mercy of the market. A Black Swan event (something that is unpredictable but will almost certainly happen at some point) will likely wipe out any gains you make. Avoid the Black Swan events by not over-trading. Trading is a zero-sum game. You will lose money if you trade too much. You will also lose money if you trade at the wrong time. A good rule of thumb is to only trade 1/2 of what you can afford to lose. Of course, you should never trade with money that you need back soon. Plan for the worst, hope for the best. Pricing is difficult because of the time value. An option has two values associated with it: time value and exercise value. The time value is based on how long of a time period you have to exercise the option. The exercise value is based on how much money it will cost you to buy the underlying asset and how much you will make in interest on that money. Knowing the price of the underlying asset and the strike price on the option, you can calculate the time value, but not the exercise value. Thus, you can't really know what you are going to make on the option because you don't know what the market will pay for it when you want to sell it (and you don't want to sell it too early). A sane approach to pricing options is to assume that you will only be able to exercise the option at the end of the trading day and that you will only be able to sell it to a friend (so you need to find a friend who wants to buy it). Under these assumptions, you can calculate the present value (time discounted price) of the option. If you are selling a put, you will only be able to exercise it if the market price of the underlying asset moves above the strike price. The time value of the option will decrease day by day. The present value of the option will fluctuate up and down (and possibly even go negative) over the day. If you buy a call, you will only be able to exercise it if the market price of the underlying asset moves below the strike price. The time value of the call will increase day by day. The present value of the call will fluctuate up and down (and possibly even go negative) over the day. If the price of the underlying asset moves above the strike price, you will need to exercise the call. If the price of the underlying asset moves below the strike price, you will need to sell the put. If the price of the underlying asset doesn't move, you will need to buy more options to hedge your downside and speculate on the upside. Note that you will only be able to sell the put or exercise the call at the end of the trading day. Also, you should generally avoid exercising options early (there is usually a fee to do so). Putting all of this together, you can see that option pricing is really difficult and that you should only attempt to do it if you really know what you are doing. If you want to learn more about options, I recommend that you start by reading a book about options. A good book will explain the basics of option pricing and how to develop trading strategies using options. There are many good books on options; do your research to find one that is right for you. After you have read a book or two about options, practice what you have learned by trading options on the market. Start out small and gradually increase the size of your trades over time. Always have a stop-loss strategy in place in case you get a Black Swan event. EDIT: added information about how to calculate the time value and exercise value of an option EDIT: clarified that you should only trade options if you really know what you are doing and warned about the risks of over-trading and trading at the wrong time EDIT: added information about how to price a portfolio of options EDIT: warned about the risks of exercising options early EDIT: provided a suggestion for where to start reading about options EDIT: clarified that you should start out small when trading options and gradually increase the size of your trades over time EDIT: emphasized that you should always have a stop-loss strategy in place when trading options EDIT: provided a suggestion for what to do

It is super easy to open an account, but it is another thing to actually get good returns out of it. I think that your best option is to actually put the money in an indexed ETF, rather than trying to time the market. The reason is that you are investing for the long term, and so you can take a bit of risk. The worst that happens is that you underperform the market, but you seem happy to accept that risk. To keep things simple, I will assume that your only tax vehicle is a super fund, and you will contribute $50,000 to it in total, and you will draw down $2000 per year from it. I will also assume that your super fund will return 6% above the CPI, which I think is a reasonable assumption for the medium to long term. With these assumptions, I think you can get a good outcome, and the simplicity of the strategy is appealing. I don't think you are in trouble. Your boss is probably going to be alright. He probably doesn't even owe you money. You may have misunderstood something. It is possible that the company that your boss works for is having financial issues. So, it is possible that your boss doesn't have the money that he owes you. There is no easy way to find out if this is true. You could threaten to go to the labor court, but that may not be worth it if it only costs you an hour or two of your time. You could also try the conciliation service, but that may not be available in your area. Another possible route is to try to get a receipt for the money that he owes you. Sometimes debt collection agencies will help you out in this way. It is worth bearing in mind that the debt collection agency is there to make money. They will probably not help you out in any way. You are going to have to decide what you want to do in this situation. Do you want to go after the debt collection agency? Do you want to go after your boss? Do you want to work out a settlement? There is no right or wrong answer here. Do what ever you feel is right in this situation. If you do decide to go after the debt collection agency, there are a few things that you need to keep in mind. First, you are going to need to gather some paperwork together. Namely, you are going to need to show how much you are owed and why. It may be worth getting some help with this from a union representative or an attorney. Second, you can contact the debt collection agency and demand a refund of your money. They are legally obligated to respond to you. If they are not cooperative, you can escalate the issue with the Information Commissioner's Office (ICO). They will be able to provide you with a sample form letter to request a refund of your money. You can use this letter as a starting point and customize it as you see fit. There is no need to use a solicitor at this stage. If the debt collection agency is not cooperative, the next step is to contact the solicitor that is representing them and demand a refund of your money. They will be able to provide you with a list of all the options that you have. If the debt collection agency has already sold your debt off to a third party, then you are going to have to go after the third party. This can get very messy very quickly. That is why I would advise you to get as much money up front as you can. You can make this as simple as possible. If you are owed £100,000, then ask the debt collection agency to send you a cheque for that amount. They don't have to send you a cheque if they don't have the money. You could set up a brokerage account and have people sell their short positions to you. This would insulate you from some of the risks of lending your stock out, but it's going to create some administrative overhead for your. You'd have to open an account, figure out how to handle the money, and so on. It might be worth it, or it might not. You could also try to find a willing counter-party to buy your stock back at a set price (say, $100/share). This would allow you to recoup your losses, but it's going to be harder to unload your stock this way. Mesmo if you find a willing counter-party, you're still out $100/share on the sale. It might take some time to sell the stock, and you're not making any money on the sale. You could do a reverse stock split (20:1) and use the money to buy back the stock, but you're still out 10% on the sale. And you've now diluted your shares by 20%. It might take some time to recover from the dilution, and you're not making any money on the sale. You could also just hold the stock and wait for some sucker to buy it, but that's not exactly what I'd call "getting rid of your losses". You're not making any money on the sale, and you've now tied up your shares in a long-term loss. It might take some time to recover from the loss, and you're not making any money on the sale. So I don't think there's any real upside here. You're not making any money on the sale, and you're taking on some risk to do it. You might be better off just holding the stock and waiting for some sucker to buy it, or selling it to a stranger. If you're really interested in unloading your losses, you should probably do a proper accounting analysis of the situation. I.e. figure out exactly what you're allowed to deduct and how much it will cost you to do so. You might also want to look into whether there are any tax benefits to doing this. I.e. can you deduct the loss of the shares, or is it only a capital gain? There might also be some value to the tax deductions (depending on your income, etc.). Do an analysis of the situation from a tax perspective as well. You might also want to consult with a CPA or tax attorney to get their professional opinion on the matter. They might charge you a fee for this, of course. Most importantly, you should probably just hold the shares and wait for some sucker to buy them. Unless you're absolutely convinced that the market will go down in the near future (and you're willing to take the risk of being wrong), you're likely better off holding the shares and waiting for some sucker to buy them than selling them to a stranger. After all, you're not making any money on the sale, and you're taking on some risk to do it. It might take some time to recover from the loss, and you're not making any money on the sale. And who knows, maybe in a few years you'll be able to sell them at a gain. But if you just hold the shares, you know exactly what you're going to get for them (nothing) and you don't have to pay any money to sell them. And you might also be able to use the tax deductions to offset some of the loss. So it might be worth it, depending on your situation. But if you do it, you should definitely consult with a CPA or tax attorney to make sure that you're not running afoul of any tax laws. They might charge you a fee for this, of course. It might be worth it, or it might not. You'd have to do an analysis of your situation to figure out if it would work for you or not. Again, most people would probably be better off just holding the shares and waiting for some sucker to buy them. The Federal Reserve Bank publishes exchange rate data in their H.10 release. It is daily, not hourly. The Fed says this about their data: About the Release The H.10 weekly release contains daily rates of exchange of major currencies against the U.S. dollar. The data are noon buying rates in New York for cable transfers payable in the listed currencies. The rates have been certified by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York for customs purposes as required by section 522 of the amended Tariff Act of 1930. The historical EURUSD rates for the value of 1 EURO in US$ are at: http://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/hist/dat00_eu.htm If you need to know the exchange rates the value of 1 US$ in EUROS is at: http://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/hist/dat00_usd.htm Other sources: I don't know of any hourly data sources, but there are plenty of companies that will sell you data. One such company is Worldcap, which is a data provider for currency values. Their website is http://www.worldcap.com/ And there is also the European Central Bank which will provide you with exchange rate data for the value of 1 EURO in US$ and vice versa. You can find their data at: http://www.ecb.org/pub/data/historical/daily/index.htm If you are looking for a free data source, I would recommend checking out WorldCap or the ECB. I'll echo: many of the other answers. In addition, Yahoo! Finance uses a different data provider for its index prices than it does for its stock prices. The index prices are provided by Nobel Prize winner, Robert Shiller's site. The stock prices are provided by the National Best Bid and Offer. In this case, the difference between the two is a matter of how you interpret the data. The index price is a price calculated by taking a basket of representative stocks in an attempt to track the performance of a particular market segment. In the case of the S&P 500, it is literally a price calculated from the 500 member stocks. To be clear - it is a price calculated by Yahoo! Finance. The National Best Bid and Offer will set their own prices based on the activity in the market. In this case, the difference between the two is a matter of how you interpret the data. The index price is a price calculated by taking a basket of representative stocks in an attempt to track the performance of a particular market segment. In the case of the S&P 500, it is literally a price calculated from the 500 member stocks. To be clear - it is a price calculated by Yahoo! Finance. I hope this helps. EDIT: As requested, here is how to calculate the S&P 500 index price. I'll leave it open for others to debate the best way to do so. Given that the S&P 500 is a price index, I think it is fair to say that the best way to calculate it is to simply sum the product of the index weight and price for each stock in the index. In Excel, this was derived as shown To calculate the S&P 500 index price, you need to calculate the weighted total of the member stocks. The weights are based on the market value of the stocks in the index. To calculate the weighted total, you need to multiply the price of each stock by its weight and then sum the results for all the stocks in the index. The index price is then reported by Yahoo! Finance. It is worth pointing out that the S&P 500 index price is not the same as the S&P 500 ETF. The S&P 500 ETF is a passively managed ETF that tracks the performance of the S&P 500 index. The S&P 500 index is a price index that is calculated from the market prices of the member stocks. As a consequence, the S&P 500 index can be thought of as a price index of the S&P 500 ETF. This should not be taken as a recommendation to buy or sell the S&P 500 ETF or any other ETF or stock. The purpose of this exercise is to explain how the S&P 500 index is calculated and to point out some of the differences between the S&P 500 index and the S&P 500 ETF. EDIT: As requested, here are some additional resources that explain how to calculate index prices. I'll start with a simple example, the Dow Jones Industrial Average. The DJIA is a price index of 30 U.S. stocks. To calculate the DJIA, you need to calculate the weighted total of the member stocks. The weights are based on the market value of the stocks in the index. To calculate the weighted total, you need to multiply the price of each stock by its weight and then sum the results for all the stocks in the index. The index price is then reported by Dow Jones & Company. Calculating an index price is not the same as investing in an index. The DJIA is a price index that is calculated from the market prices of the member stocks. As a consequence, the DJIA can be thought of as a price index of the DJIA ETF. The DJIA ETF is a passively managed ETF that tracks the performance of the DJIA. Buying the DJIA is buying a basket of stocks. Owning a stock that is included in the DJIA will not allow you to claim that you are owning the DJIA. In order to own the DJIA, you must own the stocks that make up the index. This should be taken as a warning to investors who may be considering buying the DJIA or other indexes. This is just a pedestrian (my) opinion: Yes, if you have a low risk tolerance, this is a good investment. Why? Because, you know, bonds. They're safe, and this one's as safe as it gets. It's not like there's some big risk of default on these, so it's a good emergency fund, or a bond index fund, or whatever. > If you do manage to lock into this, please do not hold it to maturity. You know, in case you actually need it back soon. > Also, don't let this stop you from contributing to your retirement, or buying a house, or starting a business. If you'd otherwise be better off, and you have the discipline to pay the bills, I think it's better to go all out and live. I.e. Don't let this cr@p get in the way of living your life. > And if you do manage to lock into this, please do not hold it to maturity. I'd be surprised if there was ever a good reason to hold a bond to maturity, but if there was, you wouldn't be reading this comic. > So, in my opinion, this is a good investment for someone with a low risk tolerance. Not the best, but good. > Disclaimer: I know very little about personal finance. I'm mostly self-taught, and I've only been at it for a few years. So, take my advice with a grain of salt. > And if you do manage to lock into this, please do not hold it to maturity. > P.S.: If you're reading this, you should probably skip the part about investing in bonds. I mean, if you're going to do it, do it right. Get some good quality bonds, and maybe some junk bonds, and make some money. But if you just hold them to maturity, you're not really going to make anything. And you can't really lose anything either. So, it's up to you if you want to make money with them or not. If you do manage to lock into this, please do not hold it to maturity. > P.P.S.: If you want to learn about personal finance, I recommend you read "The Total Market Maker" by Anthony Bourdain. It's not exactly a "how to" book, but it is an interesting read. And it will make you question a lot of things you think you know about money and investing. Questioning is the first step to taking control of your finances. Taking control of your finances is the best investment in the long run. I.e. It's up to you to take control of your finances and make the best of them. > P.P.P.S.: If you want to learn about how to invest, I recommend you read "A Random Walk Down Wall Street" by Burton Malkiel. Malkiel is one of the best investors in the world, and he has the results to back it up. He also has a lot of good advice on how to build an investment portfolio. I would advise you to read a lot of books about investing and learn as much as you can about the market. The more you learn, the better equipped you will be to take control of your finances and make the best of them. > And if you do manage to lock into this, please do not hold it to maturity. > And if you ever get the chance to buy a zero percent bond, don't. Unless you're planning on holding it to maturity, in which case you should probably stick to cash or money market funds. > And if you ever get the chance to buy a negative yield bond, do it. Negative yield bonds are used to describe bonds that pay out a negative yield. They're basically giving you money to hold the bond, and you're expected to hold it for a long time. Some investors believe that this is a good strategy for managing risk. I.e. They're not expected to make money with the bond, they're just expected to hold it for a long time. This is another example of how there's more than one way to invest in bonds. There's a lot of different strategies that investors use. You should research what strategy are you wanting to use, and then invest accordingly. Don't just buy bonds blindly just because you think bonds are "safe". Bonds can be a great part of your portfolio, and they can help you hedge against risk, but you should invest

Pre-edit, Pete mentioned that you can just apply and find out. Well, yes. But there's no need to go through the whole application process just to find out your pre-qualification status. In general, if you're willing to pay 4-5% interest rates, you'll do fine. If you're willing to pay 3% or less, you'll do even better. Keep in mind that these are just ballpark figures, and they may change without notice. Also, since the market is currently experiencing a boom, rates may drop even lower. Don't take my word on it -- ask a local bank or mortgage broker. They'll give you a better idea of what you can really afford. Also, don't think that you have to stick your entire 401(k) or IRA into the mortgage. You can make a low payment each month that's more feasible. Talk to a local bank or mortgage broker to find out what your monthly payment would be. They'll take into account your income, the value of your collateral, and the total amount you want to borrow. Remember, however, that you'll pay interest on the difference between what you borrow and what you repay each month. So, if you're paying 4%, and you borrow 100K, you'll pay 4000/yr, or $320/mo. Is that sustainable? Depends on your total yearly income, of course. If you're cognizant of that, and you still think you can afford it, great. If you're not sure, or you think the market will tank and you'll lose your job, or you have a lot of debt already, you might want to hold off. And if you're six months out of college and ready to start your career, or just plain scared, you might also want to hold off. There's nothing wrong with wanting to buy a house, but there is something wrong with buying a house and immediately maxing out your credit cards. Don't do that. If you can't raise the cash to pay for the down payment, or you're six months out of college and your only credit card is a Visa with a $2000 limit, you need to re-evaluate your plan. You can still buy a house, but you need to start thinking long-term about how you'll pay the bills if you have a run of bad luck, or if you decide to change jobs or move for work, or if you just plain hate your job and want to start over again, or if you have a family member or significant other who depends on you, or you are in an accident and can't work for a significant period of time. If you're married, or seriously considering it, make sure that your spouse helps with the shopping, and is in agreement on the priorities and choices. If you're not married, or don't have a significant other, you might want to consider whether you really want to buy a house, or if you just want to feel like you have a home. If you buy to rent, or buy a fixer upper, or use the money for an investment property, you need to have a plan for how long you'll hold it, and what you'll do if/when you lose money, or the market goes down, or you simply change your mind about where you want to live. Having a baby, or adopting a child, or buying a house -- in that order -- can change your mind about a lot of things. And if you're religious, or believe in reincarnation, or simply want to avoid the risk of having "all your eggs in one basket", you might want to buy a house that's in a different city, or at least 30 miles from your nearest city. Plan for where you'll live, and work, in 5 years, 10 years, 20 years. If you're lucky enough to have family nearby, or you're willing to commute to your job, or you have a lot of friends in your network, you can probably skip this advice. But if you're six months out of college and your only job leads are through your network, or you're making a lot of contacts but they all want to hire you for a different job, or you're stuck in a dead-end job that your network could easily connect you with a better one, you might want to hold off on buying a house. And if you're making more than $100K/yr, or working for a company that offers good benefits and a 401(k) match, you can probably skip this advice too. But if you're

Some of the benefits of having credit available but not used: Some of the costs of having credit available but not used: Overall, not too bad. But, if you do it regularly, you will incur some cost. In particular, credit agencies will charge you for each credit check. They will likely be minimal, but it will depend on how long it has been since you last used credit. Some tips for reducing the cost of credit checks: In general, we don't need to tell you how to use credit. After all, you're not exactly hurting for credit. So, just use credit normally and pay your bills on time. That will build a better credit history - and score. Some additional tips: When you pay the bills, make sure to pay them all at once. Don't pay them when you get a credit card statement, because that will likely mean that you have already incurred interest on the charges. Pay them when you get a check statement, or in full when you get a cash statement. That will save you money on interest. It will also help to avoid fees from the credit card company. Some more tips: If you ever find yourself in trouble, don't panic and don't pay the bills. Take your time to pay the bills, and only pay them when you can afford to pay them. Don't get yourself in trouble by trying to pay too fast or too low. Take your time, and pay the bills when you can afford them. That will save you money in the long run. It will also help to avoid negative marks on your credit report. Negative marks can accumulate on your report and can cause problems later, with getting credit cards or mortgages, or can even be a factor in determining your credit score. Avoiding them will save you money and time in the long run. It's really about managing your money and your credit. If you do that, you don't need to think about it. Credit cards can be a big help in managing your money, by providing a cushion in case you ever overspend or make a mistake. But, you need to pay the bills and you need to pay them on time. Once you do that, you can use credit cards for whatever you want. Just remember to pay the bills on time. Good luck! Note: This is not about your original question, but it's related to the topic. I think that including your home region in your investment portfolio is a strategy worth considering. The argument for doing so is that it is an extension of your risk management strategy. If you are not able to manage the risk of your investments, then you should consider whether you are able to manage the risk of including your home region in your investments. What is the worst that could happen if you do so? If you decide to include your home region in your investments, there are a few things you need to consider. First, you need to make sure that you have a good emergency fund. This is important for two reasons. First, in case you ever need to pay to replace your home, it will be a lot cheaper to do so if you have a good emergency fund. Second, a good emergency fund will give you the peace of mind to know that you can replace your home if necessary. Second, you need to make sure that you have enough insurance on your home. Once again, this is for two reasons. First, in case you ever need to rebuild your home, it will be a lot cheaper to do so if you have enough insurance. Second, insuring your home will give you the peace of mind to know that you can rebuild your home if necessary. Third, you need to consider whether you want to live in your investment property. If you plan on retiring in a different city, then this is a non-issue. However, if you plan on retiring in your current city, then you need to make sure that you have enough money to support your lifestyle in your current city before you start investing money that could be used to support your retirement. If you do manage to save up enough money to support your retirement, there are a few things you need to consider to make the transition easier. First, you need to make sure that you have a plan for where to live in retirement. If you don't have family or friends in your current city, then you might want to consider moving closer to family or friends in your retirement. However, if you do have family or friends in your current city, then you might want to consider keeping your current residence as close to your retirement residence as possible. In any case, you need to make sure that you have a plan for where to live in retirement before you start investing money that could be used to support it. Finally, you need to remember that retiring is a process. It is possible to retire from work, but it is not possible to retire from life. There will be ups and downs along the way, and you need to make sure that you have the emotional strength to deal with them. If you can manage your money well and make the necessary sacrifices, then retiring will be a lot easier. If you feel that you are in better position to deal with the challenges of retiring, then you can start planning for it sooner. If you feel that you are in worse position to deal with the challenges of retiring, then you might want to wait even longer before starting to invest for retirement. However, regardless of your financial situation, if you start feeling overwhelmed by the challenges of retiring, there are always options for you. You can move, sell your investments, and buy a smaller house for a lower mortgage payment. You can work for a while longer before retiring. You can use the remaining money in your retirement accounts to supplement your income after you retire. And if you have children, they can help you out in a number of ways. With a lot of hard work and dedication, you can achieve financial independence and retire at any time you choose. In my opinion, the best way to invest for retirement is to do what is necessary to make that happen. If that means keeping your current residence as close to your retirement residence as possible, then do that. If it means selling your investments and moving to a lower cost of living, then do that. If it means working for a while longer before retiring, then do that. However, if you are unable or unwilling to make the necessary sacrifices to fund your retirement, then you can still achieve financial independence by following the steps I listed above. With regard to the challenges of retiring, there are a number of things that can go wrong. However, if you have a good plan and a positive attitude, you can overcome any challenges that arise. If you are living in your investment property, it will make it a lot easier to retire since you can rent it out to help support your retirement. If you aren't living in your investment property, then you need to make sure that you have a good plan for where to live next to support your retirement. Once again, if you are willing to make the necessary sacrifices to fund your retirement, you can do so without compromising your ability to sleep at night. If you are unwilling to make the necessary sacrifices, then you need to develop a plan B. What will you do if you are unable to work for a living? What will you do if you are injured on the job or develop a debilitating illness

No, there is no linkage to the value of precious metals and the liquidity of assets. While it is possible to use them as a hedge against currency fluctuations, there is no guarantee that they will perform as desired. Also, there is no way to ensure that you will be able to sell them at a reasonable price in an emergency. In general, using precious metals as a hedge against currency fluctuations is a very long-term strategy with no short-term benefits. While it is possible to make a lot, it is also possible to lose a lot of money and take a big hit to your emergency fund. I would not recommend it. In general, emergency funds should be as liquid as possible. Cash is the most liquid asset, so if you have a lot of cash in your emergency fund, you should be able to access it easily in an emergency. If you don't have a lot of cash, you should still be able to access enough to pay for an emergency. If you can't access enough cash, you might have to use credit cards or borrow from friends or family. If you do manage to save up enough cash, you should still keep it safe in an emergency fund. Don't spend it on vacations or other non-emergency expenses. If you spend it on an emergency, you'll just have to rebuild it later. If you use it on vacations or other non-emergency expenses, you'll have to come up with a plan to pay for those expenses in an emergency. The most important thing to remember about emergency funds is that they are a hedge against the risk of having to pay for an emergency. If you have a high risk of having to pay for an emergency (i.e. you don't have a lot of cash), you should make your emergency fund as liquid as possible. If you have a low risk of having to pay for an emergency, you can still benefit from having a relatively liquid emergency fund. In general, if you have to pay for an emergency, it will disrupt your plans for the future. If you rebuild your emergency fund later, it will take time to recover your original position. During this time, you may have to make do with less money than you had before the emergency occurred. If you plan on having a lot of expenses in the future (e.g. college, a new mortgage, or a baby), you should try to make your emergency fund as liquid as possible so that you can access the money you need for those expenses. In conclusion, there is no one-size-fits-all answer to this question. You need to decide what makes the most sense for your own situation. If you want to learn more about emergency funds, I would recommend reading up on the topic. There are a lot of good books and articles on the subject. If you have any doubts about whether you should have an emergency fund or not, talk to a financial advisor. They can help you to evaluate your situation in more detail and determine what the best course of action is for you. Here are some additional tips for managing emergency funds: - Pay yourself first. If you pay yourself first, you don't have to worry about making a payment to your emergency fund each month. This is especially important if you are tight to make the payment. It happens all the time that people will make a payment to their emergency fund, then have an unexpected expense and have to go into debt. If you pay yourself first, you avoid that risk. It is, however, still a good idea to have a relatively liquid emergency fund in case you do have a run of bad luck. In general, if you have a lot of expenses in the future, you should try to make your emergency fund as liquid as possible so that you can access the money you need for those expenses. If you don't have a lot of expenses in the future, you can still benefit from having a relatively liquid emergency fund. In any case, you should avoid putting yourself in debt to pay for an emergency. If you do find yourself in debt, you should make a plan for how you are going to pay it off. If you have to pay a lot of interest on the debt, you should make a plan for how you are going to refinance it later for 10-15 years when the balance is low. This will help to avoid the risk of having to pay two mortgages or rent two apartments, etc. - Don't keep unnecessary items in your emergency fund. If you keep track of your expenses and only withdraw money that you need, you can make your emergency fund last much longer. The ticker symbol for the mini Google stock is NLY. The "mini Google" refers to the fact that NLY is a small cap stock that is included in the S&P 500 index. NYT quotes NLY as saying "500" which indicates its inclusion in that index. Another way to think about it is that NLY is a "single" stock that can be traded as part of the S&P 500 index. Single stocks that are part of an index are referred to as "index members". Other index members of the S&P 500 include IBM (another blue chip stock), GE, and many others. Index members can be bought or sold as part of the index, which means that the price of NLY is determined by the market as a whole. However, there is nothing preventing someone from trading NLY separately and outside of the S&P 500 index. Other stock markets may have different rules and conventions for how to get to the index member level. For example, the Nasdaq index, which is the basis for the S&P 500 index, is a price-weighted index. So, if someone were to buy up a lot of NLY stock and started trading it on the Nasdaq, it is possible that the price of NLY on the S&P 500 index could go up or down independently of the Nasdaq price. This would be a lot less true for an ETF, which would be expected to be much more "liquid" -- i.e. the price of the ETF will be much more in line with the underlying asset(s). Hope this helps. Good luck! Edit: As to why they do it this way? It is a service. NYT, and other newspapers, provide a lot of information to help people buy and sell stocks. For example, the NYT will often highlight stocks that are included in common indexes, such as the S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, etc. They do this because these stocks are of interest to a lot of people. If NYT were to list all 500 stocks in the S&P 500,000 people who read the paper each day would be better informed about the market. However, most people aren't that interested in reading about the market every day, so instead, they index stocks by their inclusion in common indexes. This is a service. NYT is providing a way for people to understand the market more easily. In a sense, it is almost like NYT is saying "we know how important it is for people to understand the market, so we are going to make it easier for you to understand the market. Please note that this is a simplified explanation of how to get to the index member level. In reality, it is more complicated. For example, the S&P 500 index is a price-weighted index. So, if a stock that is included in the S&P 500 index were to be bought up by someone who doesn't currently own it, it is possible that the price of the stock on the S&P 500 index could go up or down. In a sense, the S&P 500 index is "sticky". It is based on the principle that "familiarity breeds contempt". In other words, because everyone pays attention to the S&P 500 index, it is harder for a stock to be included in the S&P 500 index. It is easier to include a stock in an obscure index. For this reason, there are a lot more obscure indexes than there are S&P 500 indexes in the world. However, if you wanted to include a stock in an S&P 500 index, you would have to buy up a lot of stock to get it there. This is complicated by the fact that the S&P 500 index is a price-weighted index. So, if you wanted to include a stock in the S&P 500 index, you would have to buy up a lot of stock to get it there, and it would need to be bought at a price that would be acceptable to the owners of the stock. This is why it is possible that a stock that is included in the S&P 500 index could be bought or sold on the S&P 500 index, but it is harder and slower to get a stock into the S&P 500 index. Hope this helps. Good luck! Second edit: As to why Google isn't included in the S&P 500 index: There are a lot of reasons why

Kid, you need to start thinking ahead. Not in the sense of planning for your funeral, but just planning ahead for when you get your first apartment, your first job, your first encounter with a real estate agent, etc. Because it's not about timing the market, it's about time in the market. So get in as early as you can. And don't worry about the cost of the first few months of your apartment. Just get in somewhere, anything, and start building your credit up. And if you can manage to save up a few hundred thousand dollars, you can buy a house and start building equity in that. But if you just spend the next few months blowing your $3000/mo. on going out to eat, you'll never save up enough to buy a house, and you'll just have to pay rent forevermore. And if you do manage to save up enough to buy a house, the $3000/mo. will no longer be available to you for going out to eat, because you'll need to use it all to pay the mortgage. And if you're six months out of college, no real estate agent is going to give you a serious look unless you have a lot of cash in your hand. Because they don't care about your potential, they care about your liquidity. And if you're not married, then you are essentially on your own to find a partner, buy a house, and make a go of it. So get out and find a good real estate agent that will show you how to invest your money properly. And if they charge you a lot, find another real estate agent. There's no need to pay $500/mo. or more for a real estate agent if you're not making enough money. Do it yourself, or find a partner, but don't pay more than you can afford to lose. And when they tell you to "buy the cheapest house you can", they mean it. Don't even try to buy a house 25% bigger than you need, or 25% cheaper, or you'll pay the mortgage for it and it will be a lot longer before you're whole. And don't even think about squirreling money away to "invest" -- if you're not married, you need to put all the money you can into the mortgage, and into building up your equity. It's not about how long it takes to pay off the mortgage, it's about how long you live in the house. If you live in the house for 30 years, and pay the mortgage for 30 years, you'll do a lot better than if you rent it out. And if you're investing, and the market goes down, you'll do even better. So don't even think about it -- if you're not married, you need to put all the money you can into the mortgage, and into building up your equity. It's the best investment in the world, the best hedge against the risk of being single. No other investment can compete with it. So don't even try it. If you're investing, and the market goes down, you'll do better than if you rented. So don't even think about it -- if you're not married, you need to put all the money you can into the mortgage, and into building up your equity. Generally, no. Unless there is some sort of fraud or illegal activity, lawyers don't get paid. In some cases, like someone making a claim of abuse, the lawyer's fees may be paid by the insurance company. In a settlement, all the money paid by one party to another in exchange for goods or services. So if your client paid $1099 for the settlement, that would not be considered income to you. Even if the client didn't attend the settlement conference, and you had to go to court to get the settlement, the court would likely not consider the fees to be your income. Why? Because the losing party in a lawsuit is generally not required to pay the winning party's fees. (There is an exception if the losing party does not attend the settlement conference.) So if your client didn't attend the settlement conference, you would probably not be able to consider the $1099 as income. If you did attend the settlement conference, the $1099 could be considered as income to you. However, the amount of time you spent on the case, and the amount of money you earned on the case would likely offset any tax liability. (This is called "offsetting". When one item of income is eliminated, it offsets another item of income on your tax return.) So for example, if you earned $10,000 on the case, and spent $4,000 on the case, you would only be taxed on $6,000 of income. That's called "taking a deduction" of $4,000. Your client would not be able to deduct the $4,000 from his tax return because he didn't earn it. (He earned $10,000, so he would only be able to deduct $6,000.) So in any case, you should not consider the $1099 as income to you. If you did attend the settlement conference, you may have to pay some taxes, but the amount of money you earn on the case should offset any tax liability. You are in my debt. I have 24 hours to pay off this debt or be out $5000.00 plus interest. What is the meaning of this? It is that I can sell you a stock that I don't actually have. You are 3 days away from being able to withdraw your money. So I am essentially giving you a line of credit that will be very expensive. If you are going to be selling stocks that you don't own, you should probably stick to cash accounts. This is because you are going to be paying interest on the money that you borrow, unless you are borrowing from a broker that offers interest-free loans. I would also recommend that you don't sell stocks that you don't own because you are going to be paying a penalty if you have to pay interest on the loan, plus the cost of the loan itself. In general, high-frequency trading is used to buy and sell stocks that are highly liquid. This is because the high-frequency trader can get into and out of the market quickly to take advantage of price discrepancies. For example, if a stock that a high-frequency trader buys goes up in price, then the high-frequency trader can sell the stock and make a profit. Conversely, if the stock goes down in price, then the high-frequency trader can buy the stock and make a profit. Note that these profits are only possible because the high-frequency trader is paying very low prices for the stocks that they are selling, and vice versa. This is made possible by the fact that the high-frequency trader is only required to put up $50,000.00 to finance their trades, and the $50,000.00 is only a "good faith" deposit held by the clearinghouse. The actual price that the high-frequency trader pays for the stock is $100,000.00, so the $50,000.00 is simply a "good faith" deposit held by the clearinghouse in case the high-frequency trader decides to close out the loan. You, on the other hand, are going to be paying interest on the $50,000.00, plus the cost of the loan itself. This is because you are not as quick to get into or out of the market, so you don't get to take advantage of price discrepancies as a high-frequency trader does. As a result, you are going to be paying more. Specifically, you are going to be paying interest on the $50,000.00 at whatever rate the market is offering, plus the cost of the loan itself. This is why I recommended that you stick to cash accounts if you are going to be selling stocks that you don't own. Cash accounts offer a number of advantages over margin (essentially what you are using when you sell a stock that you don't own). One advantage is that you don't have to pay interest on the money that you borrow. Another advantage is that you don't have to pay the cost of the loan itself. The only disadvantage is that you don't get as good a price for the stock when you sell it. However, you should still make a profit since you will be selling for a price that is higher than the price that you bought (hopefully). __In general, high-frequency trading is used to buy and sell stocks that are highly liquid. Note that this is a different issue than the missing dividends/capital gains which you may still experience with a relatively new position in an index fund. Many of these funds will not distribute dividends/capital gains until after the close of the last trading day. In this case, EDV is not distributing dividends/capital gains at all. This may be because the fund is not yet large enough to warrant the cost of the distribution or perhaps there is some other reason. I'm not sure why Yahoo! Finance would not label this as a dividend. It may be that this is a no-load fund (i.e. no transaction costs) and so the first distribution is not labeled as a dividend. (I've seen a lot of load funds but not no-load funds that distribute dividends.) I hope this helps. Good luck! Here's another example, where the fund is distributing dividends but not capital gains: http://finance.yahoo.com/q/hp?s=O+Historical+Prices In this case, the fund is distributing dividends but not capital gains because it has not yet completed its tax-efficiency analysis. As a result, it's not clear to Yahoo! Finance that this distribution should be labeled as a dividend. Again, this may be because the fund is not yet large enough to warrant the cost of the distribution. Note that this is different from the situation where the fund is not distributing dividends/capital gains at all. In that case, the fund is not labeling any distribution as a dividend. Here, the fund is labeling a distribution as a dividend but not another. This may be because the fund doesn't want to label a distribution as a dividend if it's not tax-efficient. After all, if the distribution isn't tax-efficient, then the fund's tax-efficiency rating will drop which may not be desirable for a fund that wants to attract investors. I hope this helps. Good luck! I would sell it off. Why? Because it's the smart thing to do. I mean, you could hold it and take your chances, but why suffer the potential losses and hassles of holding a stock that is no longer listed? I would sell it to a friend or on the open market. If you're going to be holding it for a long time, then you should probably stick to the open market so that you can realize any capital gains. I mean, it's not really about timing the market, it's about time in the market. So if you buy something that gets delisted, you're pretty much stuck with it until it's listed again (and who knows if it will be listed again). I would also avoid buying stocks that are in the process of being delisted, as well as stocks that get delisted because of some sort of scandal. You know, things like accounting fraud, or failing to meet SEC or other regulatory requirements. These stocks are usually cheap for a reason. They may have to be sold for as little as a dollar or two per share. So if you're not careful, you could buy a stock that ends up being worth less than you paid for it. And that's just plain dumb. I mean, you're better off selling it to a friend for a hundred bucks and then buying it back later for a dollar if you want to test your trading skills. Terminology aside. Your question is really which type of savings account do you want? Do you want one that pays some meager interest rate, or do you want one that pays no interest and simply returns your money? The above is what I believe to be a rephrasing of your question. I think the proper question is which type of savings account do you want. And the options are pretty much what you described. (I'm not thinking of any details, like minimum deposit amounts, transaction fees, etc.) Sure, you can deposit $50k in an MMA. But the total interest rate, after all the fees, will probably be somewhere below 1%. So you're not really going to make much headway. Also, you have the risk of overdraft fees. (I'm not sure how much they would charge you, but it's another potential gotcha.) You might do better with a high yield savings account. The rates are higher, and you don't have the risk of overdraft fees. But you don't get both. Pick one or the other. (And, as I may have mentioned, read the fine print on any fees.) As to your comment on not being able to deposit $50,000 in an MMA... You can. But the total interest rate will be below 1%. So you're not really going to make much headway. But you have the risk of early withdrawal penalties. (I don't know how long this would last, but it's another potential gotcha.) As to your comment on not being able to deposit $50,000 in an MMA... How can I find out what factors are making a stock's price rise? This is a question that can be asked in many ways. Initially, you need to define what you are looking for. Are you looking for the causative factors (what made the stock price rise in the first place)? Or are you looking for the current price drivers (what is keeping the stock price up)? Once you have defined what you are looking for, you can start to look for answers. For the causative factors, you will want to look at the fundamentals of the business. What is the business model? How does the business make money? What are the barriers to entry? Are there any external factors that could cause the stock to rise or fall? For the current price drivers, you will want to look at the technical analysis of the stock. What is the current trend direction? Are there any support or resistance levels that could cause the stock to reverse direction? How is the volume of the stock traded? Are there any dividends or stock splits that could cause the stock to move erratically? In general, the price of a stock will rise when people are buying the stock and fall when people are selling the stock. But there can be many reasons why people are buying or selling a stock, so it is important to look at the fundamentals and technical analysis to get a better understanding of what is causing the stock to do. In the case of Tesla, people are buying the stock because they believe that the company is going to do well in the future. They are basing their decision on the fundamentals of the company - what is the company's business model, how is it making money, what are its goals, etc. If people believe that Tesla is going to do well in the future, they will continue to buy the stock and its price will probably continue to rise. There can also be external factors that cause a stock to rise or fall. For example, if Tesla were to build a factory in China, or open an office in India, or even just announce that they are going to do one of those things, the stock price could rise on hope alone. Or conversely, they could announce that they are closing a factory in China, or laying off staff in India, and the stock price would probably fall. Note that these are just a few of the many factors that could cause a stock to rise or fall. The list of potentially relevant factors is probably endless. So what use are these concepts to you? Well, they could help you to understand the current price of a stock, and more importantly, they could help you to estimate where the stock might trade in the future. If you are looking to buy or sell a stock, understanding the fundamentals and technical analysis is an important part of the process. You might not be able to move fast enough to take advantage of all the information available to you, but you can at least educate yourself enough to know what you are looking for. In the case of Tesla, it is still a small company, so it is likely that the people who are buying and selling them have a lot longer term goals than just making a few bucks. For example, many people believe that Tesla is going to revolutionize the way we get around, or that they are going to make electric cars accessible to everyone. These people are probably going to hold Tesla stock for a long time, and they are probably going to continue to buy it no matter what it does in the short term. For people who are only interested in making a few bucks, well, they might not be so interested in Tesla. So what use are these concepts to you? I hope this has helped. Basically, understanding the fundamentals and technical analysis of a stock is an important part of understanding the stock's price. It is useful for people who are interested in buying or selling a stock, and it is especially useful for people who are interested in buying or selling a stock that is not yet publicly traded. ftill What are the current price drivers for Tesla? In Hong Kong, you can find a lot of companies whose shares are listed, but there are very few who are actively traded. In fact, I can't name more than a handful of stocks that are frequently traded enough to be of any interest to an investor. This is because the bulk of the Hong Kong stock market is made up of small-cap stocks that are only traded by institutional investors and/or insiders. The "Central" and "South" are the two primary exchange venues in Hong Kong, and they are both operated by the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. Most of the large companies are located in the Central, while the South is home to a lot of smaller companies. There are also a number of other exchange venues that are used for trading in Hong Kong, but they are all much smaller and are used primarily by institutional investors. For a fixed income ETF, the price of the ETF will be determined by the market, just like any other ETF or stock in the world. The market will look at the net assets of the ETF (i.e. the value of the bonds it owns) and will price it accordingly. If the net assets of the ETF are $100,000 and the market thinks they are worth $105,000, then the ETF will trade at $105.00. If the market thinks they are worth $110,000, then the ETF will trade at $110.00. And so on. Of course, there may be some slight discrepancies between the value of the bonds and the price at which the ETF trades, but that will be true for any kind of bond or stock in the world. It is possible that a fixed income ETF will trade at a premium or discount to the value of its net assets, but that will be true for any kind of ETF or stock in the world. If investors think that a bond or stock will appreciate in value, they will buy it at a premium. If they think it will depreciate in value, they will sell it at a discount. And so on. The fact that the bond or stock is illiquid enough to be included in a ETF doesn't matter here. It only matters for the purpose of determining the value of the ETF as a whole. So, what determines the price of a fixed income ETF? The market. And what does the market look at to determine the price of a ETF? The net assets of the ETF. And what are the net assets of a fixed income ETF? Bonds. So, in a sense, the price of a fixed income ETF is determined by the price of the bonds it owns. However, the price of the bonds is determined by the market, so ultimately, the price of the ETF is determined by the market. In a more technical sense, the price of a fixed income ETF can be thought of as the present value (time discounted price) of the cash flows it will generate in the future. If $100,000 of the ETF's assets are in the form of 100,000 bonds paying 10% interest, then the ETF is generating $10,000/year. The present value of $10,000/year is $100,000. This is the value of the bonds to the holder of the ETF. It is important to note that this is not the same as saying that the bond is worth $100,000. The bond is still worth whatever the market decides it is worth, which may be $105,000 or $110,000 or somewhere in between. So, to summarize: The price of a fixed income ETF is determined by the market. The market looks at the net assets of the ETF (i.e. the value of the bonds it owns) and prices it accordingly. If $100,000 of the ETF's assets are in the form of 100,000 bonds paying 10% interest, then the ETF is generating $10,000/year and is currently trading at $100,000. EDIT: As to why the author of the question may have been so vague in his question: It is because the answer to the question as it was originally written could be interpreted in more than one way. The author may have been asking about the present value of the bond's cash flows, in which case the answer would be $100,000. Or, he may have been asking about the price of the bond in a secondary market, in which case the answer would be $105,000 or $110,000 or somewhere in between. Or, he may have been asking about both, in which case the answer would be $100,000 + (some adjustment). There are multiple valid interpretations of what the author of the question is asking for, which is why I feel it is important to have the additional information about the bond's cash flows in order to give a clear answer. This question is likely to be voted closed as it's not really about personal finance. That said - In general terms the market cap of a company is the value of all the shares that are outstanding. It's calculated by taking the total number of shares, multiplying it by the price per share, and then adding up the total. In theory it should be possible to look at the books of a company and determine its market cap, but in practice it may not be possible to get a true picture as the books may not be entirely accurate. For example - A company may have bought back some shares, but not sold any, so the number of shares is still the same. Or, a company may have sold some shares, but not bought any, so the number of shares has decreased. There are various ways that companies can treat their shares, so it's not possible to make any assumptions about what should be in the market cap. In the case of an acquisition, the price paid may be very different from the market price. For example - A company may pay $100 per share for a target, but the market price of the target may be $200 per share. So - the company has now bought 100,000 shares at $100 per share, but the market still thinks they are worth $200 per share. This may have various reasons - the company may have to pay some premium to acquire the target, or the target may have some liabilities that the company now has to assume responsibility for. In any case - the company's board thinks that $100 per share is the best price to have paid for the target. So - there may be various reasons why the market cap of a company doesn't match its acquisition price. It's not really about personal finance, so I won't go into any more details, but there are various reasons why the numbers may differ. courtier In addition to Chris Rea's excellent answer, these points may also cause the numbers to differ: In some cases, there may be stock split or some other corporate action that causes the stock to be split up or down, which may change the relationship between different classes of shares. There may also be a dilution of earnings if a company uses some of its cash to buy back shares, and so the remaining shares are worth less. There are various ways to value a company, and so if you're not careful, you may end up paying more for a company than it's really worth. I'm not sure why they would be touting a $200 per share valuation of the company - perhaps the company thinks that is what the market will bear, or perhaps there are some special circumstances which aren't immediately obvious. There may be some premium paid over the market price to acquire the company, as it's not uncommon for there to be some disruption to the market price when a company makes a big acquisition. In general, the market cap should be taken as the total value of all the shares out there. It's possible that some shares may be held by parties who aren't selling them, so the market cap may not be the same as the total value of shares that are for sale. In theory, someone could buy up a lot of shares and then refuse to sell them, so the market cap may increase without any change in the value of the shares. There are various ways to manipulate the market cap, so it's not really a meaningful number in and of itself. It may be useful to look at the books of a company and determine what the true value of the shares is, but in practice this may not be possible. In some cases, a company may have to be taken to court to settle a price for its shares. So - there are various reasons why the market cap of a company may not be the same as the total value of the shares. It's a complicated issue, and it's best to just accept that these numbers may differ. Chris Rea makes some good points, and these are worth bearing in mind. In the US, when a bank account is closed, all the money in it, including any investments, are supposed to be transferred to the government. This is a rather drastic measure intended to discourage people from keeping money in banks that are not their "primary" bank. In your case, since you never opened a bank account in the US, you never actually performed any transactions with it, and you closed it after a few months, I doubt that the US government now owns all your investments. But, as I may have mentioned above, it is worth checking with a lawyer or CPA in your home state. In Australia, I think the situation is different. I'm not sure what would happen if you closed a bank account, but I do know that if you simply left money in a closed bank account for a long time, eventually the bank would have to sell it to the government. In the US, banks are allowed to hold onto your money for 7 business days, at which time they must either return it to you, or send it to the government. If they choose the latter option, you can be certain that the money is gone from your account. There is no going to court to get it back. You should have been more careful to read the fine print when closing the account. There is usually contact information for the bank where you can talk to a human being to possibly negotiate a settlement for a closed account. Don't ever give out money to settle a closed account without talking to a lawyer or CPA first. You might have to pay taxes on the money they give you back. Also, you should consider whether or not you really should have closed the account in the first place. As I may have mentioned above, in the US this is a rather drastic measure intended to discourage people from keeping money in banks that are not their "primary" bank. So, if you maintain a positive balance in your US bank account, and you close a second bank account, I doubt that the US government now owns all your investments. But, as I mentioned above, it is worth checking with a lawyer or CPA in your home state. In closing, I'd like to suggest that you contact the bank where you have your money and ask them for a list of all the investments in the account. They should be able to provide you with a list of securities and their current value. This will save you the trouble of having to re-purchase your investments at the current market rate. Good luck! P.S. I'd also like to suggest that you change your bank account to a different bank. As I may have mentioned above, banks can hold onto your money for 7 business days before they are forced to return it to you or send it to the government. So, if you were to close your bank account today, they could hold onto your money for 7 business days without any legal consequence. I'd also like to suggest that you change your bank account to a different bank because the likelihood of government seizure is so low that it wouldn't be a wise move to keep large amounts of money in a US bank account. Especially if you keep track of the money in your account and make sure to withdraw it every 6 months or a year, I doubt the government will ever go after you for the money in your US bank account. But, as I mentioned above, it is worth checking with a lawyer or CPA in your home state. P.P.S. I'd also like to suggest that you learn about the tax laws in your home state. As I may have mentioned above, there could be tax consequences to closing a bank account or changing banks. It might be worth it to at least initial a conversation with a CPA in your home state. P.P.P.S. I'd also like to suggest that you keep good records and do projections. Keeping good records is always a good idea, and doing projections will give you hope to recover your money in the unlikely event that you ever need to. Good luck! This is just a pedestrian (my) opinion: Yes, a bond fund can be too good to be true. Read carefully what you are investing in. A high yield bond fund is a high yield bond fund, not a high dividend bond fund. Some may disagree, but that's what I call a high dividend bond fund: PAYs out a high dividend and has a low price. An example of a (to my mind) well-managed high yield bond fund is one that owns assets like: AAA bonds, BBB bonds, junk bonds, etc. You can get exposure to those assets easily with ETFs, such as XLB. Talvez I'm not going to say that a high yield bond fund can't be too good to be true, but if you are not comfortable with the risks of owning junk bonds, then you probably shouldn't. Junk bonds (aka high yield bonds) are more volatile, and in a world of rising interest rates, they are likely to underperform. If you don't trust me, ask your broker. EDIT: By "it" I mean the bond fund. You can buy individual bonds, but if you are not an expert in that area, you should probably stick to bond funds. Bond funds are a more accessible way to invest in bonds, and there are many good ones to choose from. I would avoid buying individual bonds because it is harder to buy them, and harder to get a good price. EDIT2: Some may disagree, but I do not consider high yield bonds to be an asset class. I consider bonds to be a bond, and there are many different types of bonds. High yield bonds are simply a type of bond that pays out a high dividend. So, no, high yield bonds are not an asset class. Final note: I am not a financial advisor. I am just a random guy on the Internet who occasionally looks through bond funds. I know enough about bonds to be dangerous, but not enough to be a true expert. I can't tell you whether or not you should buy a particular bond fund, but I can point out things to consider if you are looking for a bond fund to invest in. Please do your own research before investing in anything. I am here to help, but I can't tell you whether or not you should buy a bond fund.

The ETR is the world's third largest stock exchange, after NYSE and Nasdaq in the US. It is a very liquid exchange, with low fees for market makers. The FRA is the fifth most liquid exchange in the world, after NYSE, Nasdaq, ETR, London Metals Exchange and OTC. It is also a very liquid exchange, with low fees for market makers. The main difference between the ETR and the FRA is that the ETR is a cash equity exchange, while the FRA is a derivatives exchange. The ETR lists companies like Apple, Google, Microsoft, etc. The FRA lists derivatives products like futures, options, etc. The FRA also has a spot market, where you can trade cash equities. The ETR does not have a spot market. You can only trade derivatives on the FRA. You cannot trade cash equities on the FRA. The FRA is a very liquid exchange, but the liquidity varies depending on the time of day. The ETR is open 24 hours a day, 5 days a week. The FRA is open from 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM on business days. In general, the ETR is the better exchange for someone who wants to trade cash equities. The FRA is the better exchange for someone who wants to trade derivatives.
