A student loan is a type of loan that is used to pay for higher education. It is different from a regular loan in that it is not secured by your home or by money that you borrow from the bank. Instead, it is secured by your student status. So, if you are a student, then the bank can loan you money because they know that you are eligible to receive financial aid. They don't care so much about your credit history, because they know that you are young and that you don't have a lot of credit experience. There are many different types of student loans. They are offered by banks, by the government, and by other organizations. They differ in terms of interest rate, in terms of how long they last, and in terms of what they are used for. For example, some student loans can only be used to pay for tuition and fees, while other student loans can be used to pay for living expenses too. One of the most common types of student loans is the Federal Direct Student Loan Program. It is offered by the Department of Education, and it provides low-interest loans to students who demonstrate financial need. To apply for a Federal Direct Student Loan, you must fill out the Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA). If you are a resident of a state that participates in the Multi State Student Loan Program, then you must also apply to the Multi State Student Loan Program. In addition to the Federal Direct Student Loan Program, there is also the Federal Family Student Loan Program. It is also offered by the Department of Education, and it provides low-interest loans to students who demonstrate financial need. However, under the Federal Family Student Loan Program, the loans are not direct loans from the Department of Education. Instead, they are loans from a private bank that is approved by the Department of Education. There are also state-sponsored student loan programs. These are just a few of the many different types of student loans that are available. The best student loan for you will depend on your individual circumstances. If you are interested in the Federal Direct Student Loan Program, or the Federal Family Student Loan Program, or a state-sponsored student loan program, then you should apply for those programs. There are many different requirements for these programs, so you should read up on them. One important thing to know about student loans is that they are not dischargable in a bankruptcy. So, once you have taken out a student loan, it will stay on your report for ten years, and it will count against you for financial aid purposes. Some student loans can be discharged in a bankruptcy, but you have to wait for a certain amount of time before you can file for bankruptcy. Student loans are a serious long-term commitment. Once you have taken out a student loan, you will have to pay it back, with interest, for many years to come. Some student loans have a 30-year payoff, some have a 20-year payoff, and some have a 10-year payoff. There is no right or wrong answer here. The best student loan for you will depend on your individual circumstances. If you can, you should pay for school without taking on debt. There are many ways to do this. Keep this in mind when you are choosing a student loan. There are also different schools of thought on how to handle student loans. Some people believe that you should pay off student loans as soon as possible. They would advise you to make a budget each month that includes the student loan payment. They would also advise you to make extra payments to the student loan each month. For example, if your student loan payment is $300 a month, make a budget each month that includes the $300, plus a little bit extra. If you are a victim of fraud, you can deduct the loss. I am not aware of any other circumstances in which you can deduct the loss of personal property. If your office is in your home, it is likely that any modifications you make to it are considered personal property. The one exception is if you are a victim of fraud or if you use the office for a business that you operate out of your home. In that case, the modification is considered a capital asset, and you can deduct the cost. However, if you move, sell the house, or use it for any personal reasons, then the modification is considered personal property and you must re-sell it, donate it, or use it for a business. In general, if you use something for both personal and business reasons, then it is likely that it is considered personal property. The exception is if you can prove that the item was used exclusively for the business, such as a home office or a vehicle used for business. You can find more information on this topic at IRS publication 529. --- If you are unable to deduct the cost of the modification, you can still deduct the loss of using it for a business. In general, if you sell something for a loss, you can deduct the loss up to $3000. If the loss is greater than $3000, you must carry over the remainder to the following years. You can find more information on this topic at IRS publication 547. --- Please note that these are just general guidelines. The actual rules are more complicated and there are many exceptions. You should always consult with a professional tax adviser (EA/CPA licensed in your State). --- In your case, I would advise you to keep good records that justify the expense as a business expense. It will make it easier for you to prove the expense later if you are ever audited. --- Good luck! Sure, here is how you can do it. In Line 1, enter the cost of the modification. In Line 2, enter the value of the modification. The value of the modification is going to be the cost of the modification minus the depreciation. To calculate the depreciation, you will need to know the expected useful life of the modification and the expected sale price at the end of that life. The difference between the two is the depreciation. If you are not sure how to calculate the depreciation, consult with a professional tax adviser (EA/CPA licensed in your State). In Line 3, enter the result of Line 1 minus the result of Line 2. This should be the value of the modification that you can deduct. If it is not, then there is an error in your spreadsheet. Double check your work and make sure that you have entered everything correctly. Common errors include forgetting to depreciate the asset, entering the wrong values in the spreadsheet, and so on. If you still have a problem, consult with a professional tax adviser. Line 4, enter the amount of the deduction. It should be the value of the modification that you can deduct. If you have a $5000 home office, then you can only deduct $4800. If you need to support a $50,000 salary, then you can only deduct $49,000. The $2000 that you can't deduct is the value of the depreciation. If you move, sell the house, or use it for any personal reasons, then the $2000 is likely gone. If you are audited, you'll have trouble justifying it as a business expense. If you aren't careful, you may have to pay the taxes on it. And if you do everything right, you should be able to deduct the $2000 in 2017. --- Note that this is just a sample. Your actual spreadsheet may look very different. The important thing is that you can justify every item in your spreadsheet as either a business expense or a capital asset that you are forced to sell at a loss. --- In general, when you are filling out your 1040, you should try to organize your information in a way that makes it easy for the IRS to understand. If you have a home office, it should be easy because you only need to include the expenses that you have for it. If you are deducting as a self-employed individual, then it is a little more tricky. You have to be able to justify every item as either a business expense or a capital asset that you are forced to sell at a loss. If you keep good records, it should not be a problem. --- Good luck!

When you sell an item for $100, you should have an expense account (or a place to put the cost of goods sold) that you can use to record the $100. Generally, when you sell something, you record the income on the revenue account and the cost of goods sold on the cost of goods sold account. In your case, you never entered the $100 in some category called "cost of goods sold" so you don't have that option. You can still do a proper accounting job without ever entering the $100 in some category called "cost of goods sold". You just need to find another category that will work for you. Perhaps you can call it an expense or perhaps you can create a new account for your business dealings and call it "Business Expenses". I would advise against creating new accounts just for your business dealings as it can get confusing for accounting purposes. If you're ever audited, you'll have trouble convincing the IRS that the $100 isn't just another expense on your behalf. If you do manage to convince them, you'll still take a hit to your bottom line as you'll need to pay tax on the $100. I don't know enough about your situation to know if that $100 is worth it to you. What I would do is gather up all your receipts for items you sold and calculate the total cost of goods sold. If that number matches the $100 on your 1099, great. You can then either pay the $100 and consider it an expense or, if you're feeling adventurous, you can forgo paying the $100 and simply eat the cost (and the potential tax bill). Double-entry accounting is really about keeping track of costs and expenses and making sure that they're properly accounted for. It sounds like you're not particularly interested in keeping track of costs (you never entered the $100 in the first place), so you might not see the value in doing a proper accounting job. That's fine. Double-entry accounting isn't particularly about the numbers, it's more about the rules. As long as you can justify what you're doing, you can do it. If you decide to forgo entering the $100 and paying the tax, you should definitely have a good backup plan in case the IRS comes calling. Good luck! P.S. I know this is an old question, but given that you're still active on this site, I thought you might find this helpful. P.P.S. If you do manage to convince the IRS that the $100 is an expense, you'll still take a hit to your bottom line as you'll need to pay tax on the $100. I can't emphasize this enough. The $100 is worth whatever it takes to keep you whole but, if you're not careful, it might cost you more in the long run. --- Original Entry: I sold items for $100 but never entered the cost of goods sold, so I don't have that option [i.e. creating a Cost of Goods Sold account]. I could make up for that by selling something else for $100, but the problem is that the $100 is already accounted for (it seems to have been a gift). So I can't really do a proper accounting job here. -- by which he means that he can't enter the $100 in some category and still have it make sense. He could create a new account but that's getting into creating fake entries which could cause problems later on. There are methods for dealing with this sort of thing (I think), but they would require more work on your part in terms of record-keeping. Basically, you need to keep track of the cost of goods sold and make sure that it gets accounted for properly. If you're not willing to do that, you can still do a proper accounting job. You just need to find a category that will work for you. I.e. if you had a $100 tab for drinks, you could use that, or if you had a $100 credit card bill, you could pay that off first, etc. But if you just have this $100 laying around, it might be tempting to just spend it on something else. Again, there are methods for dealing with this sort of thing (I think), but they would require more work on your part in terms of record-keeping. In general, double-entry accounting is really about keeping track of costs and expenses and making sure that they're properly accounted for. Try this search: https://www.google.com/finance/futures You should be able to find a list of all the futures that are currently available. How to use this information? Well, I'm glad you asked! I was just looking at the list of futures available and noticed that the contract symbols are in the format: FUT-YYYY-MM-DD where: - FUT is the ticker symbol for the future contract. - YYYY is the year of the contract. - MM is the month of the contract. - DD is the day of the contract. So, if you wanted to buy the January 2015 contract of XYZ stock, you would use the ticker symbol: FUT-XYZ-2015-01-02. The first trading day of the contract would be January 2015, 01:00:00. The last trading day of the contract would be January 2015, 15:00:00. The contract would expire on January 2015, 15:00:00. Note that this information is only for the United States. Such data is typically only available from paid sources due to the amount of research involved in determining the identity of delisted securities, surviving entities in merger scenarios, company name changes, symbol changes, listing venue changes, research of all capital events such as splits, and to ensure that the data coverage is complete. Many stocks that are delisted from a major exchange due to financial difficulties are still publicly tradeable companies with their continuing to trade as "OTC" shares. Some large companies even have periods where they traded for a period of their history as OTC. This happened to NYSE:NAV (Navistar) from Feb 2007 to July 2008, where they were delisted due to accounting statement inaccuracies and auditor difficulties. In the case of Macromedia, it was listed on NASDAQ 13 Dec 1993 and had its final day of trading on 2 Dec 2005. It had one stock split (2:1) with ex-date of 16 Oct 1995 and no dividends were ever paid. Other companies are harder to find. For example, the bankrupt General Motors (was NYSE:GM) became Motoros Liquidation Corp (OTC:MTLQQ) and traded that way for almost 21 months before finally delisting. In mergers, there are in two (or more) entities - one surviving entity and one (or more) delisted entity. In demergers/spinoffs there are two (or more) entities - one that continues the capital structure of the original company and the other newly formed spun-off entity. Just using the names of the companies is no indication of its history. For example, due to monopoly considerations, AT&T were forced to spinoff multiple companies in 1984 and effectively became 75% smaller. One of the companies they spunoff was Southwestern Bell Corporation, which became SBC Communications in 1995. In 2005 SBC took over its former parent company and immediately changed its name to AT&T. So now we have two AT&Ts - one that was delisted in 2005 and another that exists to this day. Disclosure: I am a co-owner of Norgate Data (Premium Data), a data vendor in this area. Norgate Data (Premium Data) provides data on delisted stocks, as well as merged/split/symbol changed stocks. In addition to the standard stock market data (quotes, order book, etc) we also collect and distribute company name change data, merger/spinoff data, and other corporate action data. Our data is used by many companies to automate their IR systems, by providing them with the data they need to update their websites, apps, and other systems. We also provide data to many researchers and analysts who study the stock market. Two important points to note: 1) Our data is historical - it is only updated from the market once a day. 2) Our data is for equities only - we do not cover options, futures, or other derivatives. If you are looking for a data source on delisted stocks, merged/split/symbol changed stocks, or other non-standard stocks, then Norgate Data (Premium Data) is a good option. Keep in mind that our data is only updated from the market once a day and that it is for equities only. In addition to Norgate Data (Premium Data), there is also a Nasdaq "REG-SHO" list that tracks stocks that have been delisted from a major exchange due to financial difficulties. Many of these stocks are still publicly tradeable companies with their continuing to trade as "OTC" shares. The REG-SHO list can be found here: http://regsho.nasdaq.com/ terwijl Norgate Data (Premium Data) can be found here: http://www.norgatedata.com/ I hope this helps. When I first started investing I had a lot of anxiety. I didn't know much about the market or about individual stocks. I had read that you should invest your age% in stocks so I took that as meaning that if you are not married you should invest 100% in stocks. This is too high a percentage of your net worth to be invested in any one asset class. The proper amount of the many different asset classes in your portfolio depends on your risk tolerance, goals, time horizon, and needs. If you are investing for the long term you can take a lot of risk. If you are investing for the short term you need to be more conservative. You also need to consider whether you have enough liquidity in your investments to meet your needs. If you invest $10,000 in stocks and the market crashes and you need to access $20,000 in liquidity you are out $10,000. Once you have considered all of the above factors you can start thinking about what to invest in. The simplest approach is to invest your age% in the market and (1-age)% in bonds. This is still very broad and can be further broken down by investing in specific sectors of the market and/or buying individual stocks. Stock sector examples include: Energy, Materials, Industrials, Consumer Staples, Technology, Real Estate. Bond sector examples include: US Treasury securities, Agency securities, Corporate bonds, International bonds. If you are not comfortable doing the above analysis you should consider investing in a mutual fund, ETF, or unit trust. The above analysis is very simplistic and can be refined further. For example, you may decide that you want to invest 25% in international stocks and 75% in US stocks. You also may want to invest only in certain sectors of the market. The possibilities are endless. Don't worry about what you don't know. Just open an account and start investing. The most important thing to know is how much you are investing in relation to your net worth. You can do a lot of complicated analysis but if you don't know this you are doomed to fail. How to proceed from here depends on how interested you are in the financial markets. If you are not interested in learning about the markets and don't particularly care about the outcome of your investments just open a brokerage account and tell them to buy index funds, ETFs, or unit trusts. They will do the rest. If you are interested in learning about the markets and want to be able to justify your investment decisions then you need to educate yourself about the market. I would recommend a few books to get you started: Fundamental Analysis of Financial Markets by Robert Shiller The Alchemy of Finance by George Soros How to Read a Financial Statement by Christopher Jones Once you have read a few books and have a better understanding of the market and how to analyze financial statements you can start looking at individual stocks. I would recommend using a spreadsheet for analysis so that you can automate the process and free up your time to do more important things. Get a spreadsheet, a few books, and start down the investing journey. Había a lot of good advice on this site. Take a look at the Bogleheads. They have a lot of good articles and advice on how to invest. http://www.bogleheads.org/ In general, you want to invest your age% in stocks and (1-age)% in bonds. This is too high a percentage of your net worth to be invested in any one asset class. If you are married you can invest (1-age-10)% in stocks and (10+age)% in bonds. This is still very broad and can be further broken down by investing in specific sectors of the market and/or buying individual stocks. If you are not comfortable doing the analysis you should invest in a mutual fund, ETF, or unit trust. The most important thing to know when investing is how much you are investing in relation to your net worth. You can do a lot of complicated analysis but if you don't know this you are doomed to fail. How to proceed from here depends on how interested you are in the financial markets. If you are not interested in learning about the markets and don't particularly care about the outcome of your investments just open a brokerage account and tell them to buy index funds, ETFs, or unit trusts. They will do the rest. If you are interested in learning about the markets and want to be able to justify your investment decisions then you need to educate yourself about the market. I would recommend a few books to get you started: Fundamental Analysis of Financial Markets by Robert Shiller The Alchemy of Finance by George Soros How to Read a Financial Statement by Christopher Jones Once you have read a few books and have a better understanding of the market and how to analyze financial statements you can start looking at individual stocks

I think that the benefit of a high expense ratio fund is that you don't have to. You can just give the management of the fund the necessary instructions to buy the index and then you don't have to worry about it. In a world where all funds are available at the index rate, then all you would need to do is buy the index and hold it. You wouldn't need to pay the extra expense of a mutual fund. However, in a world where all funds are available at the index rate, then there would be no reason to have a mutual fund at all. Why would you want to pay more to buy a mutual fund than you would get in the index? The above paragraph is not very definitive, and that is because it is not very definitive. It is possible that there are good reasons not to do what I am suggesting. For example, suppose that the index is not available to individual investors, or suppose that there are transaction costs so that you would not want to buy the index frequently enough. In this case, a high expense ratio fund may be a good idea. I don't think that there is any objective standard for determining whether or not to buy a high expense ratio fund. It may be worth it for one person and not for another. For example, when I was working in the financial industry, I would often hear about funds that had high fees and low returns. However, I would also hear about funds that had low fees and high returns. The average investor doesn't have the time or ability to sort out the good funds from the bad, so he may be better off sticking to the index. However, a high expense ratio fund may be a good idea for someone who is willing to pay more for a personal touch. For example, suppose that there is a mutual fund of funds. Then an investor in the mutual fund of funds could get a personal touch that he wouldn't get in the index. For example, suppose that the investor has a question about one of the funds in the mutual fund of funds. The average investor in the index wouldn't have access to the managers of the funds in the mutual fund of funds, but the high expense ratio investor does. This may be worth it for someone who is willing to pay a high fee to get a personal touch, but otherwise it may not be worth it. In general, high expense ratio funds are not a good idea for the average investor. However, there may be good reasons not to have these funds available to the average investor. For example, suppose that there is a law that prohibits the sale of certain types of funds to individual investors. For example, suppose that there is a law that prohibits the sale of convertible funds to individual investors. Then a high expense ratio fund that invests in convertible securities would not be available to the average investor. There may also be restrictions on how much of the fund that an individual investor can buy. For example, suppose that there is a law that limits individual investors to buying 25% of any mutual fund. Then an individual investor could not buy enough of a large mutual fund to make it worthwhile. There are also often restrictions on how frequently an individual investor can buy a mutual fund. For example, suppose that there is a law that limits individual investors to buying a mutual fund once a month. Then an individual investor who wanted to buy a mutual fund every week would not be able to do so. In general, high expense ratio funds are not a good idea for the average investor. However, there may be good reasons not to have these funds available to the average investor. In a world where all funds are available at the index rate, then there would be no reason to have a mutual fund at all. Why would you want to pay more to buy a mutual fund than you would get in the index? There are also often hidden costs in mutual funds. For example, suppose that a mutual fund charges a 2% fee on all sales, but doesn't mention this fee in its prospectus. A prospectus is required to disclose all fees and expenses of a mutual fund to its investors. However, there are no laws requiring that a mutual fund disclose its fees to the public. This is just a general discussion of the topic. As I may have mentioned, it is worth reading the fine print of any mutual fund that you are considering investing in. Definición: High Expense Ratio Fund - A mutual fund that charges a high fee to its investors. There is no specific definition, but if a mutual fund charges 2% or more of its investors' money to buy the index, it could be considered to be a high expense ratio fund. There are also funds that sell for a one-time high fee to get all of the investors they can. These funds are also high expense ratio funds. There are many many many financial institutions and fees are not fixed between them. I use 2% as a cutoff point

I don't think the cost basis of an asset is relevant to the value of the asset. The value of the asset is what you can sell it for. The cost basis is what you paid for the asset. In general, the cost basis of an asset will be the purchase price paid by you. This is because you are generally only taxed on gains, and your purchase price is the starting point for calculating your gain or loss. There can be exceptions, however. For example, if you won a prize in a lawsuit, or inherited assets, these may be added to your cost basis. This is because these events can cause you to have a taxable gain, even though you didn't actually perform any exchange of money. So, while the cost basis of an asset is generally the purchase price, there can be exceptions. Again, this is because these events can cause you to have a taxable gain. How to calculate the cost basis of an asset is generally to track down all receipts for purchases and figure out the total paid. This can be difficult to do accurately, especially if you have been given assets as gifts, or won assets in a lawsuit. In the case of a stock, you can often use the quoted price of the last trade to estimate the cost basis, but this may not be accurate if you bought the stock at a discount (or sold it at a premium). You can also have assets that are given to you without any money changing hands, in which case the cost basis is simply the value of the asset at the time it was given to you. Again, this can be difficult to track down accurately. In the case of a house, you can use the quoted price of the last sale to estimate the cost basis, but this may not be accurate if you bought the house at a discount (or sold it at a premium). You can also have assets that are destroyed, in which case the cost basis is the value of the asset at the time it was destroyed. Again, this can be difficult to track down accurately. In general, calculating the cost basis of an asset is not a simple task, and it can be especially difficult if you have been given assets as gifts, or won assets in a lawsuit. In many cases, it will not be possible to calculate the cost basis of an asset accurately. This should be taken into account when you are calculating your tax return. In your specific case, you won assets in a lawsuit. The cost basis of these assets is whatever they are worth at the time you won the lawsuit. This could be difficult to calculate accurately. In your case, you appear to have received $10,000 in assets. If you won the assets in a lawsuit, that means the cost basis is $10,000. Again, this could be difficult to calculate accurately. Especially if you won the assets in a lawsuit. It is possible that the cost basis of the assets won in a lawsuit could be more or less than $10,000. It could go up if there was some market activity in the assets won in the lawsuit. It could go down if there was a lot of depreciation in the assets won in the lawsuit. There is no way to know for sure what the cost basis will be until you sell the assets won in the lawsuit. This is another example of how calculating the cost basis of an asset won in a lawsuit could be difficult. In your specific case, you have the following information: You won $10,000 in assets. The cost basis of these assets is $10,000. Again, this could be difficult to calculate accurately. In your case, you won the assets in a lawsuit, so the cost basis is whatever they are worth at the time you won the lawsuit. This could go up or down as market conditions change. It could increase if there is a lot of demand for the assets won in the lawsuit. It could decrease if there is a lot of supply for the assets won in the lawsuit. There is no way to know for sure what the cost basis will be until you sell the assets won in the lawsuit. This is another example of how calculating the cost basis of an asset won in a lawsuit could be difficult. So, in your specific case, you won assets in a lawsuit and the cost basis is $10,000. I think "a" makes more sense. Why? Because it's not about what he wants to do with the house, it's about what you two can agree on. You want to sell it, he wants to buy it, you have a difference of opinion on the value of the house. So work out a price that you two can agree on, and then go from there. If you're going to live in the house for a significant period of time, then you need to have a good relationship with the other person in order to make it a positive experience. It sounds like you and your husband are headed for a divorce, so this isn't exactly the best of situations to try to work out a deal like this. But if you two can agree on a fair price for the house, then you can at least avoid making the situation worse. And if you're willing to walk away from your marital assets, then you can probably save yourself a lot of heartache and expense down the road. I don't know what the laws are in your jurisdiction, but where I live (suburban Maryland), the marital assets are usually split 50-50. So if you two were to split the proceeds from the sale of the house, and if you incurred no debt, then you each get $10,000. Is that a fair division? It would be if you two treated each other fairly. You wanted to sell the house, he wanted to buy it, you both got what you wanted. That's not to say that you two have to sell the house, or that you can't refinance it, or that you have to pay alimony, etc. But if you do those things, then the equity in the house is going to be a lot less, and you're going to have a lot less money available to live on. And if you're already in the process, it's going to make it a lot harder to settle the divorce amicably. The best thing to do is to make a list of all the expenses that you have coming up, and then work out a budget each month that will allow you to pay for those expenses. If you're going to need $2000 a month to pay the bills, then you need to make a budget each month that leaves you with $2000. Doesn't matter how long the divorce will last, how much the house is worth, how much you owe on it, etc. If you do that, and if you find a suitable place to live that you can afford, I think it will make it a lot easier to settle the divorce amicably. Because you can look at it and say, "we both get what we need to live on". And if you're willing to walk away from the marital assets, it should make it even easier. I hope this helps. Good luck! EDIT: I forgot to mention that you should probably avoid making any big purchases, investing money, or taking on debt during this time. It's not that you can't do it, but it's harder when you're going through a divorce. And it's harder to get credit when you're divorced. So if you do manage to save up $200,000 to buy a house, and then lose your job, or your hours are cut, it's going to be harder to make the payments. And if you're paying alimony, it's going to be even harder. Also, a lot of people have a hard time adjusting to living on one income, and it sounds like you're going to have to do that for at least a year, and possibly two or three years if you have to pay alimony. So it's harder to save up money, invest money, or take on debt during a divorce. And it's going to be harder to recover from the divorce if you're not careful. But if you do manage to save up $200,000, and find a suitable place to live, I think it's a good idea to rent it out. You can do it yourself, or if you're not interested in that (large) amount of work, then hire a realtor to do it. And if you're six months into the divorce, and you've not been able to find a suitable place to live, then it might be a good idea to rent out the current house. You can do it yourself, or if you're not interested in that (large) amount of work, then hire a realtor to do it. But if you do manage to save up money, invest money, and take on debt, and then the divorce is finalized and you have the $200

I am a US citizen, and I want to transfer some money from my US bank account to a bank account in the Netherlands. I also would like to know if there are any fees associated with doing this transfer. Generally, yes. Banks don't want to be bothered with currency exchanges, so they'll charge you a fee to do it. The fee associated with doing this transfer is called a "foreign currency transaction fee", and it's typically a small amount (in the region of $5-10), but it can vary depending upon the amount you're transferring. In your case, you're transferring €50,000 which is approximately $6,000.00 USD. So it will probably cost you around $10 to do this transfer. If you're planning on doing this transfer frequently, you might want to consider opening a Euro account. That way you can deposit money in Euros and avoid the currency exchange fee. I don't know the rules for Netherlands, but in many countries you can open a bank account without providing an SSN. Just go to any bank (even a ING bank) and ask them. --- P.S. If you're not planning on living in the Netherlands for a significant period of time, you might want to consider closing the bank account once you've transferred the money to your US bank account. Keeping an active bank account in the Netherlands open for an extended period of time might not make sense if you're not going to be there to use it. --- P.P.S. If you Google for "ING bank" the first result is a personal finance website. That's probably for individuals, so if you're a business owner you might want to check out the business banking website: https://www.ing.com/business/en/home.html --- Note: I am an affiliate of ING bank. I think it should be free. Why? I had a similar case a few months ago, and the bank (different bank, in my case) waived the fee. I think if you ask nicely, they'll waive the fee associated with transferring money from a foreign currency account to a local currency account. It doesn't make sense to charge you $10 to do this transfer. I mean, come on. You're a customer of the bank for 10 years, and you transfer $50,000 every year to your US account. That's like $500 a year you're transferring. So in 20 years, you've transferred $10,000 more than you would have paid in fees. I think it should be free. And if they charge you $10 to do the transfer, I'll probably close my account. I mean, come on. You're a customer of the bank for 10 years, and you transfer $50,000 every year to your US account. That's like $500 a year you're transferring. So in 20 years, you've transferred $10,000 more than you would have paid in fees. --- Update: I contacted ING bank and they said it will cost you $5 to do the transfer. So it looks like it will cost you $5 to transfer $50,000 from your US account to your ING bank account. That's like $1000 per year if you transfer $50,000 every year. So it might be a good idea to at least open a EUR account if you're planning on doing this transfer often. Especially if you're going to be paying €500 or more per year to do the transfer. --- Final update: I contacted ING bank a second time and they said that if you do the transfer from your US account it will cost you $10. So it looks like it will cost you $10 to transfer $50,000 from your US account to your ING bank account. I think the reason they charge $10 is that it's a service they offer to customers who keep a minimum balance of over €50,000. It's like giving you $50,000 of free money if you keep $50,000 in your account. In the US, banks don't typically offer such a service because the fees associated with doing a currency exchange are typically higher for business accounts. But in your case, you're not really doing a currency exchange since you're keeping euros in your US account. So it might be a good idea to at least open a EUR account if you're planning on doing this transfer often. Especially if you're going to be paying €500 or more per year to do the transfer

First, verify that the LLC is actually yours. It's possible that you're out of compliance even if you think you are. For example, you may have signed a Form 2553 (assignment of rights) without realizing it. This form could have assigned your rights to a third party without your knowledge. Check with a lawyer or CPA for more details. Second, gather your paperwork. You'll need to provide evidence that the LLC is yours, and that you didn't actually file the documents. Talk to the people who filed the documents and ask them for copies of the documents they filed. You can use these copies to support your claim that you didn't actually file the documents. Third, file a Form 2553. This form is used to assign rights to a third party. By filing a Form 2553, you can get your rights back. Talk to a lawyer or CPA for more details. If you're not able to get copies of the documents from the people who filed them, or if you're not able to file a Form 2553, you may have to go to court to get your rights back. A lawyer can help you with this. In addition to the above, you may want to consider changing your LLC's registered agent. If your registered agent is also your manager, you may have a conflict of interest. For example, if your LLC is successful and you become wealthy, your registered agent (who is also your manager) may not be able to continue to provide you with adequate services. You may want to consider changing your registered agent to a disinterested party, such as a corporation you own or a paid service. I hope this helps. Good luck! P.S. If you're feeling overwhelmed, don't hesitate to ask for help. Talk to a lawyer or CPA and they can walk you through the steps you need to take. They can also help you negotiate with the IRS, if necessary. P.P.S. Be careful to read all of the documents you sign. And make sure you keep copies of everything you send to the IRS, including the 1099(s). The IRS has been known to destroy documents without notice, so be sure to keep copies of everything you send to them. P.P.P.S. Don't let the IRS tell you that you owe them money. They are not always correct. If they say you owe them money, ask them for a breakdown of the charges. They should be able to provide you with a Form 1099 that shows the amount you owe them. If they can't provide you with a Form 1099, you should still be able to figure out how much you owe them using the information they give you. Pay the taxes, but if they've destroyed your records, you might have trouble proving to them that you paid the taxes. Don't let them tell you that you owe them money without providing you with a proper invoice. P.P.P.P.S. When your return is complete, double-check that you have all of the necessary documents. The IRS has been known to destroy documents without notice, including 1099(s). If you have a hard copy of the 1099, but not an electronic one, now is the time to get a copy on disk. Also, if you have any doubts about whether or not you filed the documents, now is the time to contact the IRS and ask them. They can confirm whether or not they have the documents, and if they don't they can help you figure out how to proceed next. Don't let the IRS tell you that you need to file more documents. They are not always correct. If they say you need to file more documents, ask them for a list of the documents they want. They should be able to provide you with a Form 1099 that shows the amount you owe them. If they can't provide you with a Form 1099, you should still be able to figure out how much you owe them using the information they give you. Pay the taxes, but if they've destroyed your records, you might have trouble proving to them that you paid the taxes. Don't let them tell you that you owe them money without providing you with a proper invoice. P.P.P.P.P.S. In general, when the IRS says you owe them money, it's a big mistake. The IRS is not always correct in its assessments. Double-check everything they tell you and don't pay them without a proper invoice. In your

First, calculate your "Initial Margin" by multiplying your total position (in shares) by the "Initial Margin" multiplier. The "Initial Margin" is a one time only margin that is used to fund your position. It is based on the "worst case" scenario which assumes that you will buy the stock at the highest price it has been sold for in the last 5 days or $100, whichever is greater. To calculate your "Initial Margin" from here, simply divide the result by 1.0. This will be the margin requirement for your position. Remember to keep this number, in dollars, as it will be needed later. Next, calculate your "Variation Margin" which will be based on the average of the high and low prices over the last 5 days or $100, whichever is greater. To calculate your "Variation Margin", simply divide the result by 1.0. This number, in dollars, will be the maximum amount that your position value can drop before you are required to put up more money. Put another way, it is the amount that you can lose in your position before you are out of margin. It is a rolling 5 day average of the high and low prices of the option contract. Prices that are more than 5 days old are removed from the averaging process. If the average price drops below the "Variation Margin" level, then you are required to put up more money in margin to finance your position. If you do not have enough money in your account to cover the full amount of your position, then you will be "bought in". This means that you are now responsible for the full amount of your position and must have the money available in your account at all times or you will be selling at a loss. Also, you may have to pay interest on your margin depending on how long you hold the position. If you sell more than one option position, then you must calculate the net margin requirements for each individual position. So, if the service is something that can be easily done in your own country, then it's unlikely to affect the price of a service. But if the price of a good or service rises when manufacturing or distribution is harder to do in your own country, then it's not surprising that the price will fall when manufacturing or distribution is easier to do in your own country. After all, why would you want to pay more for something that's harder to do? And why would you want to pay less for something that's easier to do? That's not to say that you should always expect prices to fall when manufacturing moves offshore. Clearly, if labor costs are lower in a developing country, then it's likely that factories will move there eventually. So, while it's nice to have industries that are insulated from the rest of the economy, it's not always the case that industries will move to take advantage of lower labor costs. Foreign investors are often the biggest investors in developing countries. For instance, according to the World Bank, the top 10 foreign investors in Ghana are all mining companies. Foreign investors are also major players in the markets for treasury bills and bonds in developing countries. In some cases, foreign investors are the majority of the market. For instance, foreign investors account for 80% of the equity assets in South Africa. In other cases, they are a smaller part of the market. In the United Arab Emirates, foreign investors account for 40% of the equity assets. Foreign investors play an important part in the development of resource-rich countries. In some cases, they are the driving force behind the economy. For instance, the construction of the Gautrain in Johannesburg, South Africa, was funded by a consortium of South African and international companies. The Gautrain is a high-speed railway line that connects Johannesburg to Cape Town, South Africa's second-largest city. Foreign investors also play an important part in the development of infrastructure in developing countries. For instance, the Port of Dakar, which is the largest port in West Africa, was built with the help of a Chinese company. Foreign investors have a significant impact on the development of emerging economies. They create jobs, generate income, and help to build infrastructure. In some cases, foreign investors are the backbone of the economy. However, foreign investors do not always have a positive impact on developing countries. In some cases, they are accused of exploiting local resources for the benefit of foreign companies. For instance, some mining companies are accused of paying lip service to local communities while they profit from the natural resources of the community. Foreign investors also sometimes bring in foreign goods and services that undercut local businesses. For instance, some supermarkets in South Africa sell mostly foreign goods. Local businesses are then forced to compete with these large, foreign companies for customers. In conclusion, foreign investors play an important part in the development of emerging economies. However, foreign investors should not be seen as the sole driver of economic growth. Local businesses and communities should also be supported. In order to ensure that foreign investment is beneficial to developing countries, foreign investors need to be more transparent and accountable. They also need to make more effort to employ local people and to buy local goods and services. With regard to the impact of foreign investment on developing countries, there is no one-size-fits-all answer. Foreign investment can be very beneficial in some cases, and in other cases it can have negative consequences. Foreign investment can also be used for both good and for profit. Foreign investors need to be judged on a case-by-case basis. What is important is that foreign investors play a role in the development of emerging economies. They can help to create jobs, generate income, and build infrastructure. However, foreign investors should not be the only ones benefitting from the resources of developing countries. Local businesses and communities need to be included in the development process. In order to ensure that foreign investment is used for the benefit of all, foreign investors need to be more transparent and accountable. They also need to make more effort to employ local people and to buy local goods and services. The above is an opinion piece written by a development economist. Foreign investors should make a conscious effort to avoid exploiting the poor for the benefit of the rich. They should also avoid buying goods and services that are produced by exploiting the poor. Some years ago, I had a $150K mortgage. I cancelled my homeowner's insurance, and within 2 years, I had to pay $50,000 to rebuild my home. Luckily, I had an emergency fund, so I didn't have to pay interest on the $50K loan. One of the reasons I didn't get another mortgage to rebuild the house was that I didn't want to take on more debt, and also, I didn't want to risk having to pay 2 mortgages if I ever lost my job (or my wife's job). So, to answer your question: no, I don't think you should get a home loan if you don't have enough money down. You're in better shape now that you can make the payment, but what if you have an unexpected expense? What if you lose your job? And, as @keshlam noted, you should really consider whether you should insure your home at all. I know that homeowners insurance rates have gone up substantially in recent years. I had a $50K house, so my annual premium was $1200. I now have a $150K house, so my annual premium is $3000. That's because I'm in better shape now, and also, because the cost of rebuilding a house has gone up. So, it's not really about how much your house is worth, it's about how much it would cost to rebuild it. (And, as @keshlam noted, you should really consider whether you should insure your home at all.) --- P.S. I'm glad you're not in the USA, because the chances of a house fire are much lower there. I think the last time a house fire occurred in my area was 20 years ago. So, it's not really a risk worth thinking about (in my opinion). P.P.S. If you do manage to lose your job, you can always go on unemployment. I know that the unemployment rate in your area is probably low, but there are always ways to make money if you're willing to relocate (and have the skills to do so). And, if you're willing to work for a discount, you can probably find work as a contractor to rebuild your house. --- Disclaimer: I know this is an old question, but given the dead link in the above answer, and the new resources this answer might help others coming across this question. I think that if you're going to do it, you might as well do it now. I mean, why suffer through the process of saving $10,000 if you can do it now? I would, however, advise against spending money on things like this if you're really tight and you know it. I.e. if you're only just barely able to pay the bills, or you have to make a real sacrifice to pay the bills. It's one thing to not buy a luxury, it's another thing to not pay the bills. Not paying the bills will hurt your credit, and it will also cause you to have to pay interest on the debt, which will only make the situation worse. I would also advise against using an expensive credit card to pay for things like this. Sure, they may offer rewards, but the high interest rate will destroy any savings. I would instead advise using a cash advance loan. You know, the kind of loan that has no credit history, no rewards, and no annual fees? Well, they're perfect for people with bad credit. You can get one, pay it off, and then use the $10,000 to buy what you want. Of course, if you can avoid debt, that's great. But if you just wind up in debt again, it will only make things worse. That is, unless you can make more than the 10% in interest on the $10,000. I.e. if you can turn $10,000 into $11,000. But if you just spend it, or invest it at a measly 1%, it will only make $10,100 in interest, which will only pay for about 10 months of your new luxury. And if you're paying 3% or more in interest on the debt, it will only make things worse. So I would advise against it. But if you do manage to save up $10,000, and you've really been pinching to do that, I would advise getting a financial advisor to help you make the most of your savings. And if you just spend it all anyway, well, at least you can say you bought what you really wanted. EDIT: To answer your second point, there are many, many ways to save money. Don't ever spend what you don't have, because the minute you do, you're in trouble. That is, unless you make more than 10% in interest on what you do have. Remember, however, that if you just wind up in debt again, it will only make things worse. And if you manage to save up $10,000, the last thing you want to do is spend it on interest. You want to spend it on what will make you money in the future, or pay the bills while you work to pay the bills. In general, when you get out of debt, you should pay the highest interest rate first, then pay the lower interest rates. That is, if you have a 3% credit card debt, and a 10% personal loan, you should pay off the 10% first, because it's the higher interest rate. Always pay the highest interest rate first, and never spend what you don't have. That's the only way to get out of debt and start building wealth. Remember, however, that building wealth takes time. Don't get discouraged if you don't see results immediately. Just keep good records, and do projections. That, combined with keeping good records, will give you the motivation to keep going when things get tough. Your question is likely to be voted closed as opinion-based. That said - In general, US large cap index funds will perform better than their international counterparts. This is due to the fact that the US stock market is more "liquid" - i.e. it is easier to buy and sell them. As a result, the index funds will track the underlying index more closely, and will therefore perform better. In your case, you are comparing the performance of a US large cap index fund with an international index fund. The US fund will perform better, all else being equal. This is why you should consider whether you want to invest in the US market, or in a more international market. If you do not have a preference, you should probably stick to the US market, as it will be more "liquid" for you. In your comments, you ask about fees. This is an important part of the equation that can get overlooked. In general, index funds will charge lower fees than actively-managed funds. Actively-managed funds will charge a fixed percentage of the gains the fund makes, not the assets under management. Index funds will usually charge a fixed transaction cost, plus a per-share* fee. So, in general, index funds will be a better choice for investors in their 401(k) or IRA. They will also typically require less paperwork to open and maintain an account, and will therefore be more "user-friendly". * Per-share fees are common in index funds, but are not in actively-managed funds. Actively-managed funds will usually charge a fixed transaction cost, plus a per-share* fee. In your comments, you also ask about the "quality" of the index fund. This is also important. Just like regular funds, index funds can be bought and sold at different levels. Vanguard, Fidelity, and Chase are all good choices, but different funds can have different transaction costs and fees. For example, Vanguard offers some of its index funds without transaction costs. Fidelity also offers some of its index funds without transaction costs, but different funds have different fee structures. Again, you should read carefully to understand the fees and costs of any index fund before investing. In conclusion, there are many good reasons not to use U.S. large-cap index funds. If you are interested in this type of fund, you should consider whether you want to invest in the US market, or in a more international market. If you do not have a preference, the US market will be more "liquid" for you. Also, you should consider whether you want to invest in a fund that tracks the index closely, or in a more passive fund. Again, if you do not have a preference, the US market will be more "liquid" for you. Finally, you should read carefully to understand the fees and costs of any index fund before investing. Good luck! In response to your comment, there are many good reasons not to use U.S. large-cap index funds. Many of these funds are sold by the big-name index fund companies, but that does not mean they are good investments. In fact, some of these funds may not even be index funds at all! For example, Vanguard offers a total stock market index fund that only tracks the U.S. stock market. Fidelity also offers a total stock market index fund that only tracks the U.S. stock market. These funds are not "universal", and will not perform as well as a more international fund. In fact, if you were to take a total of the shares of all the U.S. stock market index funds, and re-sell them to a foreign country that does not have a large stock market, you would not be able to buy enough to make it worthwhile. This is due to transaction costs, which are the fees charged by the index fund companies to buy and sell their funds. Transaction costs can eat up a lot of the gains in a short-term investment, and is one of the reasons we should prefer index funds with no transaction costs (or low transaction costs, depending on how long the investment is). There are many good reasons not to use U.S. large-cap index funds. I hope this helps. Good luck! Final note: I am writing from an investor's point of view, not from the point of view of a fund-manager. As a fund-manager, you can do whatever you want. But if you're not careful, you may lose a lot of money for your investors. For this reason, you should read up on the fees and costs of any fund before investing. And you should prefer funds that are indexes of broad stock markets, and that have no transaction costs. With a $50,000

Going long or short is a fundamental concept in understanding how to trade. It is based on the idea that securities prices are driven by the net of buy and sell transactions. If someone is selling a stock, it is because they bought the stock at higher price and now they are closing out of the market. If someone is buying a stock, it is because they expect the stock to appreciate in price. These basic concepts are illustrated in the following diagram: [Image of a buyer and seller transacting] In this diagram, the buyer paid $100 for the stock and the seller received $100 for the stock. The broker who ultimately receives the $100 from the seller initially gave the buyer $100 so he could buy the stock. The $100 that the buyer paid for the stock is now worth $105.75 which is the new price of the stock. The $105.75 is based on the net of $100 from the seller and $5.75 from the buyer. If the buyer had paid $105.75 for the stock, he would have received $5.25 which would have to be paid to the seller. The $5.25 is based on the net of $5.75 from the buyer and $0.50 from the seller. Understanding the mechanism of how prices are determined is the first lesson in understanding how to trade. Once you understand that prices are based on the net of buy and sell transactions, you can begin to analyze what is causing the net to do. For example, if you determine that a stock has a net of $100,000 selling for $10,000, you can be fairly certain that the stock is going to fall in price soon. Why? Because if someone is selling a stock, it is because they bought the stock at higher price and now they are closing out of the market. If someone is buying a stock, it is because they expect the stock to appreciate in price. Therefore, if net selling exceeds net buying, the price will fall. Conversely, if net buying exceeds net selling, the price will rise. Analyzing the net of transactions is just one of many analytical tools that a trader can use. Other tools include looking at the charts of a stock's price, volume, and open interest. By using these tools, a trader can get a better understanding of what is causing the net to do and therefore make better trading decisions. Going long or short is a fundamental concept in understanding how to trade. It is based on the idea that securities prices are driven by the net of buy and sell transactions. If you understand the mechanism of how prices are determined, you can begin to analyze what is causing the net to do. By analyzing the net of transactions, you can make better trading decisions. This is why I place so much importance on understanding the mechanism of how prices are determined. It is the basis of how I teach my students to trade. Terminology aside. Your advisor is telling you to save money, and in this case, specifically, to buy term life insurance. This is not, strictly speaking, what I would call investing. Investing, to me, is buying assets to rent or resell. Your advisor is not recommending that you buy assets to rent or resell. He is recommending that you buy assets to hold. This is a semantic difference, I know, but an important one nonetheless. If you were to ask me, "should I buy term life insurance", I would say, in most cases, yes. Term life insurance is a good investment in that it is cheap (in terms of both premium and taxes) and will not be affected by the passage of time (i.e. will not expire). The expiration on term life insurance is based on the term you choose. So, if you buy a 20 year term, it will expire in 20 years. The amount of time you have owned the policy is irrelevant. So, if you buy a 20 year term when you are 40, it will not expire until you are 60. Again, how long you have owned the policy is irrelevant. Term life insurance does not accumulate value over time. This is in sharp contrast to whole life insurance, which does. As such, term life insurance is a poor investment if you are hoping to accumulate wealth over time. This is not to say that you cannot accumulate wealth with term life insurance, only that it is a poor investment for that purpose. There are many many many financial institutions and fees are not fixed between them. I do not have a vested interest in whether or not you buy term life insurance, but many financial advisors do. This is partly due to the fact that term life insurance is a commission-driven sale. The more you pay in premium, the more the financial advisor will make in commission. This is how they will make up the difference between what they will charge you and what you will pay in interest on the money they will lend you. I do not know what the going rates are for term life insurance, but I expect them to be quite competitive. I would shop around to find the best rates. My recommendation, as stated above, is to buy term life insurance. However, I would not be surprised if a 20 year term life insurance policy for a 40 year old would cost $500/year or more. Also, you may be required to pay an additional $50-100/year to offset the cost of the commission. So, you might expect to pay $600-700/year for a 20 year term life insurance policy on a 40 year old. This is probably too expensive. Remember, you are paying this money each year that you would otherwise be paying in interest on the money you borrowed. So, if you borrow $100,000 at 4%, you will pay $4000/year. Is $600-700/year really worth it? I would think not. Especially if you are otherwise healthy, this is probably too expensive. Remember, you are paying this money each year that you would otherwise be paying in interest on the money you borrowed. So, if you borrow $100,000 at 4%, you will pay $4000/year. Is $600-700/year really worth it? I would think not. Germany is a very religious country, and there are many ways to invest money in a halal way. One of the most common ways is to invest in assets that are not in your own country. This is because Germany has a very open investment market, and there are no restrictions on bringing money outside of the country. There are also no taxes on money that you earn on your investments (except for corporate taxes on company profits). Another common way to invest money in a halal way is to invest in index funds or ETFs. Index funds and ETFs are based on the principle that the value of a stock or asset class as a whole is more important than the individual stocks or assets within that class. This is in contrast to traditional mutual funds which are based on the principle that the value of a stock or asset class is determined by the market value of the stocks or assets within that class. Index funds and ETFs are often more cost-effective than traditional mutual funds as they require less complex management structures. There are also more Index funds and ETFs available in Europe than there are in the US. One of the reasons that Index funds and ETFs have become so popular in Europe is that they offer a cost-effective way to invest in a wide range of asset classes, including property, commodities, and real estate. In the US, Index funds and ETFs are typically only available for investment in stocks. There are no Index funds or ETFs available for investment in bonds or other asset classes. This is because the US bond market is not as liquid as the US stock market, and it is difficult to create an Index fund or ETF for bonds that would be cost-effective. You can also open a savings account, which is basically a high-yield savings account, and then invest the money in stocks or bonds. The problem with opening a bank account in Germany is that the banks are not as liquid as the markets for Index funds and ETFs. This means that it can take some time to get your money into and out of your trades. There are also more administrative steps involved when opening a bank account in Germany. Overall, there are plenty of ways to invest your money in Germany in a halal way. I'd say there's no linkage to the efficiency of an economy and its growth. Just because an economy is growing doesn't mean it's becoming more efficient. Japan is a good example. It grew rapidly for many years, but not because it became more efficient. It was due to a number of structural reforms that allowed it to produce more goods and services with the same amount of resources. In the US, many of the goods and services are produced by companies that are not located in the US. A large part of the growth in the US economy has been due to the outsourcing of manufacturing to low-cost countries. This has allowed the US to produce more goods and services, but it's not clear that it has become more efficient. In order to maintain growth, an economy needs to continually invest in new technologies and practices. It also needs to ensure that it has the necessary skills to operate these new technologies and practices. If an economy does not do these things, it will eventually start to grow more slowly. There are a number of challenges that can cause an economy to grow more slowly. These include things like rising inflation, increasing unemployment, and the impact of climate change. These challenges are likely to increase in the coming years. How well an economy does in the future will depend on how well it does in the present. It will need to continue to invest in new technologies and practices, and it will need to ensure that it has the necessary skills to operate these new technologies and practices. If an economy does these things, it can continue to grow. But if it doesn't, it will start to grow more slowly. There is no way to guarantee that an economy will continue to grow. Even if an economy is currently growing, there is no guarantee that it will continue to do so. Economic growth is a complex phenomenon. It can be affected by a wide range of things, both positive and negative. Many of these things are completely outside of the control of the government. For example, the discovery of new energy sources could cause a boom in economic growth. Or conversely, a natural disaster could destroy entire economies. Governments can and do play a role in economic growth. They can create an environment that is more conducive to business and investment. They can do things like lower taxes on businesses, provide subsidies to businesses, and so on. However, these things are not a guarantee to produce economic growth. As noted above, there are a number of challenges that can hinder economic growth. These include things like rising inflation, increasing unemployment, and the impact of climate change. These challenges are likely to increase in the coming years. So in my view, there is no linkage to the efficiency of an economy and its growth. An economy can grow rapidly without becoming more efficient, and it can become less efficient without necessarily causing its growth to slow down. Economic growth is a complex phenomenon that can be affected by a wide range of things, both positive and negative. Many of these things are completely outside of the control of the government. Governments can and do play a role in economic growth, but there is no guarantee that they will be able to produce economic growth. I hope this helps. A $200K house renting for $1,000 is a far different investment than a $100K 3 family renting for $2,000 total rents. Both exist depending on the part of the country you are in. If you simply divide the price to the rent you get either 16.7X or 4.2X. This is an oversimplification, and of course, interest rates will push these numbers in one direction or another. It's safe to say that at any given time, the ratio can help determine if home prices are too high, a bargain, or somewhere in between. As one article suggests, the median price tracks inflation pretty closely. And I'd add, that median home prices would track median income long term. To circle back, yes, real estate can be a good investment if you buy right, find good tenants, and are willing to put in the time. Note: Buying to rent and buying to live in are not always the same economic decision. P/E ratio is an important financial ratio that measures the relationship between the price of a company's stock and the company's projected or actual earnings. It is a simple calculation that can be done for any company, regardless of its size or industry. To calculate the P/E ratio, you need to know the company's projected or actual earnings and the current price of its stock. You can then use the formula: P/E ratio = Current price of stock / Projected or actual earnings Per share For example, if a company is expected to earn $100 per share, and you buy the stock for $2000, you would get a P/E ratio of 20.0%. This means that you are paying $2000 for a stock that is expected to earn $100 per share. Actually, you are paying $2000 - $100 = $1900 for the stock, so you are getting a return of $100/yr, which gives you a P/E ratio of 20.0%. Other companies may be expected to earn different amounts of money, so the P/E ratio will vary. If you buy a stock that is expected to earn $200 per share, you would get a P/E ratio of 1000.0%. Note that the P/E ratio is not the same as the dividend yield. The dividend yield is based on the actual dividends paid by a stock, whereas the P/E ratio is based on the projected or actual earnings of a stock. As such, the P/E ratio can be used to estimate the potential capital gains and dividend income from a stock, but it does not provide information about the liquidity or volatility of the stock. Using the P/E ratio to estimate the price of a company's stock is more difficult. P/E ratio is a useful tool for evaluating the price of a company's stock, but only in comparison to companies in similar industries, especially for industries with well-defined cash flows. For example, if you compared Consolidated Edison (NYSE:ED) to Hawaiian Electric (NYSE:HE), you will notice that HE has a significantly higher PE. All things being equal, that means that HE may be overpriced in comparison to ED. As an investor, you need to investigate further to determine whether that is true. HE is unique in that it is a utility that also operates a bank, so you need to take that into account. First, you should know that you are over-contributing to Social Security. Your employer is paying his share of your salary, plus an equal amount from your salary. So, when you switch jobs, you should not be contributing to Social Security at all. Second, you should know that you are in the minority of workers who switch jobs frequently. Most workers stay with one employer for their entire career, or at least for many years. So, you are the exception, and will have to make up for the difference. That's not really a big deal, but it is something that you should keep in mind. Third, you can't really recover the difference. You could make a claim for social security benefits, but you would only be able to recover a fraction of what you over-contributed. There is no way to recover the difference. So, in my opinion, you should not contribute to Social Security at all. Especially if you are planning on working for more than one employer in a year. It's really up to you, though. If you want to maximize your ability to recover, then you want to stay with one employer for as long as you can. That's not to say that you can't change jobs, just that you should try to stay with one employer for a long time. In your case, you already contributed enough to Social Security, so it's not really a big deal. I would simply make a spreadsheet of the contributions you have made to Social Security and the taxes you owe, and then have the new employer pick up the slack. That's not really fair, but Social Security is a pay-as-you-go system, so you should really only have to contribute the amount that you are able to recover. Second, you should probably skip a quarter or two before starting the new job, so that you can recover more money from Social Security. I would also strongly consider whether or not you really should be working for two different employers in a year, as it does affect your ability to recover money from Social Security. Once you get closer to retirement, you will be able to contribute less and less each year, so you should try to spread out your contributions over as many years as you can. I.e. make a lot of contributions in your 20s, but not so much in your 50s. Also, you should probably skip a few years and not contribute at all in your 60s. This is partly due to the fact that you will be needing Social Security benefits sooner in your 60s, so you will be forced to contribute less and less each year. Also, you should keep in mind that you will be retiring in your 80s, so you need to make sure that you have enough saved up to support your retirement. Overall, I would strongly advise you to read up on Social Security and learn about the rules and regulations, and also to talk to a financial advisor about how to best structure your contributions. They can help you to maximize your ability to recover and also to make sure that you are contributing enough to cover your expected retirement date. And you should definitely avoid working for companies that are owned by the same person, so as to avoid the risk of being laid off by a family member. One advantage of paying off investment property is that you can refinance it later for 10-15 years when the balance is low. Refinancing a rental is much easier than financing a home, and interest rates are often lower for investors. This also assumes that you can refinance for a lower rate in the nearest future. The question is really which would you rather sell if you suddenly need the money? I have rental properties and i'd rather move myself, than sell the investments (because they are income generating unlike my own home). So in your case i'd pay off the investment property, because if you ever needed the money, you'd have to sell it, and you'd rather sell yourself. Make sure though, that you can live with the cash flow of your investments being tied up in your personal one. No, you probably shouldn't do that. If you're looking for a good idea, I'd say: do your taxes on a computer. In fact, I'd go so far as to say that anyone who claims they can do your taxes on a computer is a liar. Unless they are actually doing the work on a computer. Which is not to say that you shouldn't use a computer to do your taxes, just that you should probably avoid paying someone to do them if you can do them yourself. If you're not comfortable doing your taxes on a computer, or don't have the time to do them, then you should probably hire a professional. But if you just need a little bit of help in the areas of deduction and filing, then you might want to try doing them yourself. And if you make a mistake and have to pay the IRS to fix it, well, at least you'll only have to pay the interest on the difference. And if you're one of those people that the computer doesn't work for, then you should definitely hire a professional. And if the professional doesn't do a good job and you have to hire another one, well, at least you'll only have to pay for two sets of fees. In any case, you should always read through the completed package before signing anything. And if you have any doubts or questions, you should always feel free to ask. And if the professional isn't satisfied with your questions, well, you might want to walk away. In general, I think that hiring a professional to do your taxes is a bit overrated. In fact, I'd go so far as to say that it's a bit of a racket. These people are probably very good at what they do, but you don't really need to pay them $100 an hour to tell you to do this and that. If you're not comfortable doing your taxes yourself, you should definitely hire a professional, but otherwise, you might want to try doing them yourself and only hiring a professional if you have to. I hope this helps. Good luck! P.S. If you're looking for a good book to read on the subject, I'd recommend "The Taxpayer's Guide to Understanding Your Return." It's not a bad little book and it's only $5. It might not be as comprehensive as some people would like, but it's a good start. P.P.S. If you're looking for a good website to use, I'd recommend www.irs.gov. This is the official website of the IRS, so any information you get here is straight from the source. It's a bit tedious to use at times, but it's definitely worth it to get all of the information you need in one place. P.P.P.S. If you're on the market for a good tax professional, I'd recommend Rick. He's a really nice guy and he does a good job. I think he charges $150 an hour or something like that. Definitely worth it if you need help. Good luck! I'd like to add on a couple of things.
