This is a question of how you want to hold the cards. If the subsidiary is set up as such, then all you are getting is a return of your cards. This may be a wash depending on the tax implications. If you are getting a return of your cards, then you should generally be able to get a higher value for the subsidiary. This would be especially true if the subsidiary is in a better tax jurisdiction. For example, if a Canadian bank owns a US bank, then the Canadian bank will likely be paying less tax on the profits of the US bank. As such, the Canadian bank would be getting a better return on its investment. This is, however, likely to change once the US bank is sold. As such, you don't want to hold the Canadian bank stock if you are expecting to sell the US bank any time soon. Also, you should probably avoid selling the US bank to a competitor of the Canadian bank. While it is unlikely that a US bank would be a competitor of a Canadian bank, it is worth keeping in mind. Basically, you are getting what you would expect to get in a liquidation of the company. If the company is not set up as such, then you may be forced to sell the company at a discount to a third party. Again, this may be a wash depending on the tax implications. In general, if you buy stock, you should expect to hold it for a long time. If you buy a stock that you can only sell to a competitor, or that you have to sell to a competitor at a discount, then you should sell the stock immediately. Also, you should generally avoid buying stocks that are in worse tax jurisdictions. Canadian banks are in a better tax jurisdiction than US banks, so if you are looking to buy a bank, you should preferably buy a Canadian bank. Keep in mind that this may change once the US bank is sold. As always, you should do your due diligence before buying any stock. Read up on the company, and make sure that you understand what you are buying. It is especially important to understand what you are buying if you are buying a foreign company, since it is likely that you will not be able to sell it to a third party at all. In your specific case, the benefit of buying the subsidiary is that you are getting a return of your cards. If the subsidiary is set up as described, then this is the only benefit. If the subsidiary is not set up as described, then you may be forced to sell the company at a discount to a third party. As described above, this may be a wash depending on the tax implications. In general, it is not worth it to buy a stock that you cannot sell easily. If you are forced to sell it to a competitor at a discount, it is likely that the stock price will drop suddenly by a large amount. This is a somewhat common occurrence in the over-the-counter stock market, where small investors are often forced to sell stocks they bought at a higher price. This is a privilege that large investors (i.e. those who can buy 20% or more of the company) don't get. Large investors get to decide when they want to sell the stock, and to whom they want to sell it. They also are not usually required to sell it to a third party at all. In your case, you are getting a return of your cards, but if the company is sold to a third party, you may be forced to sell it to a competitor at a discount. As your stock position is likely to be a small one, it is likely that you will not be able to sell it to a third party at all in a meaningful way. You are getting what you would expect to get in a liquidation of the company, but the liquidation may not happen for a long time, and you may need to hold the stock for a long time without being able to sell it. If you don't mind holding the stock for a long time without being able to sell it, and you don't mind the risk of the stock price dropping suddenly by a large amount, then buying the stock of a subsidiary can be a good move. If you do mind those risks, then you should probably stick to buying stocks of companies that are more liquid. Liquid stocks have a lot of trading, so it is harder to have a large position in a liquid stock (it's harder to buy or sell them). For example, if there is a lot of trading in NYSE stocks, it is harder to buy or sell a large position in a NYSE stock. It is easier to buy or sell a large position in a NASDAQ stock, and NASDAQ stocks are typically less volatile (i.e. the price swings less). Nobody can predict the future value of the pound, so your question is unanswerable. What you do know is that the pound has fallen in value relative to the US dollar, and that the UK economy is doing well (as evidenced by the rise in inflation). What you need to consider is that the pound may fall in value relative to the US dollar, and that the UK economy may weaken (and possibly even crash). What you should do is hedge your bets, and not take a stance on the market. Buy a property that is priced at a level that you can afford it if the worst happens. Don't get yourself in trouble if the UK economy crashes and the pound becomes worthless. --- I've been living in the UK for 3 years, and I've seen the pound fluctuate up and down (and even go sideways for extended periods of time). I've also seen the UK economy weaken and then recover. The UK is a long way from being out of the woods yet (and may well have a hard time getting there), but it's doing better than it was a year ago. The average UK household has about £15k in debt, and the economy is growing by about 3%. That means that the UK economy could crash and the average household could find itself in trouble very quickly. But it's not all bad news. The UK is a rich country with a lot of history, and the people are generally optimistic. The UK has a young population (with lots of people under the age of 30), and so there's a lot of hope for the future. But, as I may have mentioned, it's best to hedge your bets. You never know what's going to happen. --- P.S. If you're thinking, "I'll buy a property and then the market will crash and I'll lose a lot", well, yes. But remember that the market crash of 2008/2009 was caused by a combination of factors: the sub-prime mortgage crisis, the collapse of the housing bubble, and the withdrawal of credit from the US economy. The UK economy is not currently experiencing any of those problems, and so it's not going to crash any time soon. And if it does, you'll probably be out of the property for a lot longer than you'd hoped. P.P.S. If you do manage to buy a property, and the UK economy does crash, and you lose your job, and you need to go on benefits, the new government will probably put a cap on how much you can claim (as has happened in other recessions). --- So, to summarise a bit: nobody knows what's going to happen, but it's good to have a plan in case things go wrong. And if you're going to buy a property, it's good to buy one that you can afford if you lose your job or the economy crashes. And if you're going to invest in the UK property market, it's good to invest in a mix of property types (such as student flats, or properties with a high yield). And if you're going to borrow to buy a property, it's good to keep an eye on interest rates, and to have a plan in case they go up. And if you're going to buy a property in the UK, it's good to buy one that is well below the peak price (and preferably in a cash-buying area). And it's good to buy a property that is ten years older than you think you'll live in it, in case you change your mind about where you want to live in the future. And if you do all that, and the UK economy crashes and you lose your job and your property, it's good to remember that it's not the end of the world. The UK has a long and proud history, and the people are generally optimistic. The UK has a young population (with lots of people under the age of 30), and so there's a lot of hope for the future. And if you're one of the lucky ones who manages to buy a property in the UK, it's good to get involved in the local community and start building relationships. And if you do that, and the UK economy crashes and you lose your job and your property, it's good to know that you're not alone. There are lots of people who have experience dealing with difficult situations. And if you're one of the unlucky ones who bought at the peak of the market and now the market is crashing, it's good to remember that you bought at a time when the UK economy was doing well. The UK has a lot to offer foreign investors: a young population with a lot of potential, a lot of beautiful and historic cities, and a lot of opportunities to get involved in

For federal crimes, the money collected from penalties goes into a special fund called the "Crimes Against Humanity Fund". This fund is used to pay for medical care, including mental health care, for victims of violent crimes. It is a "pass-through" fund, meaning that the money is given to the victims themselves. None money is given to the federal government. Most states have a similar fund, although they may be called something else. In some cases, such as child support, alimony, or restitution, money may be collected by the federal government but given to the states to distribute. In other cases, such as drug offenses, the money may be used by the federal government to pay for its own programs. Most of the money in the Crimes Against Humanity Fund comes from criminal fines. Some of the money may come from forfeitures, such as the sale of property that was seized from a criminal. For example, if a bank seizes $100,000 from a drug dealer, and the government seizes another $100,000 from the dealer's bank account, the government would have $200,000 more or less at its disposal. It may use that money to pay for programs for victims of crime, such as the construction of a new wing at a hospital, the purchase of new equipment for a police department, or the training of new social workers. Much of the money in the way of programs for victims of crime comes from fees collected from criminals, so it is really money that comes from criminals. That is, however, not true for all of the money in the Crimes Against Humanity Fund. As noted above, some of the money in the fund comes from other sources, such as forfeitures and restitution. Restitution is money paid by a criminal to a victim as part of a plea deal or at the end of a trial. For example, if a man is sentenced to pay $10,000 in restitution to a victim, and he pays $5,000 up front, the $5,000 will go into the Crimes Against Humanity Fund. It is important to note that the money that goes into the Crimes Against Humanity Fund is not income for the federal government. It is money that is given to victims of crime to pay for medical care, including mental health care. As such, it does not show up as income on a federal balance sheet. It is, however, an important part of the federal government's commitment to providing justice for victims of crime. It is funded entirely by fees paid by criminals, and it is used entirely for the benefit of victims of crime. Here is a link to a list of all fees that go into the Crimes Against Humanity Fund. It is called "Schedule of Fees" and it is located at 28 C.F.R. § 1704. It is important to note that this is not income to the federal government. This question is likely to be voted closed as opinion-based. That said - In general terms, the low interest rates reflect the fact that these accounts are not considered to be "liabilities" of the banks. In the US, the savings accounts are backed by the full faith and credit of the US government. In Europe, they are backed by the full faith and credit of the European Central Bank. A key difference between the US and Europe is that the US dollar is the world-leading currency, and as such the market for USD is much more liquid than the market for EUR. As a consequence, the interest rates on USD are much more "market-driven", and as such fluctuate much more based on the daily market rates. The ECB, on the other hand, sets interest rates based on the economic fundamentals of the Euro zone. As such, the interest rates on EUR are much more "controlled", and do not fluctuate as much based on the daily market rates. In general, the interest rates on savings accounts in the US and Europe are low because they are not considered to be "liabilities" of the banks. In the US, they are backed by the full faith and credit of the US government. In Europe, they are backed by the full faith and credit of the European Central Bank. The interest rates on USD are much more "market-driven", and the interest rates on EUR are much more "controlled". As a consequence, the interest rates on USD fluctuate much more based on the daily market rates, and the interest rates on EUR do not fluctuate as much. Había In the US, the savings accounts are backed by the full faith and credit of the US government. So, if the US government were to fail, then the value of the savings accounts would be lost. In Europe, the savings accounts are backed by the full faith and credit of the European Central Bank. So, if the European Central Bank were to fail, then the value of the savings accounts would be lost. Note that failing is not the same as repudiating debt. Repudiating debt is when a government refuses to pay the interest or principal on its loans. Currently, Greece is refusing to pay the interest on its loans, but it is still paying the principal. So, if Greece were to stay out of the Euro zone, then the value of the savings accounts would be lost. Había The interest rates on savings accounts in the US and Europe are controlled by the Federal Reserve in the US and the European Central Bank in Europe respectively. The Federal Reserve is the central bank of the US, and the European Central Bank is the central bank of the Euro zone. They are responsible for setting interest rates that are "commenced" with the economic fundamentals of their respective economies. As such, the interest rates on savings accounts in the US and Europe are not set day-to-day based on the market rates. They are set based on the long-term economic fundamentals of the economies. As the Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank are the central banks of their respective economies, they are also the repositories of last resort for their respective economies. If the Federal Reserve were to fail, then the US dollar would be expected to weaken, and the interest rates on savings accounts would be expected to rise. If the European Central Bank were to fail, then the Euro would be expected to weaken, and the interest rates on savings accounts would be expected to rise. Note that the US dollar and the Euro are the world-leading currency and the second-largest currency respectively. The market for USD is much more liquid than the market for EUR. As a consequence, the interest rates on USD are much more "market-driven", and the interest rates on EUR are much more "controlled". Había In the US, the Federal Reserve has been accused of "printing" money and "buying" votes. This has been a controversial issue, and there are no easy answers. The Federal Reserve denies that it is "printing" money, and points out that it is merely buying bonds from the private sector in exchange for USD. This keeps USD in high demand, and as a consequence the interest rates on USD stay low. However, it is still a controversial issue, and there are no easy answers. Había In Europe, the situation is different. The European Central Bank has not been as controversial, and there are no major scandals involving the bank buying votes. You are correct that you need to buy a lot of shares to make a significant difference in the price of a stock. However, you are incorrect in that you need to buy a lot of shares to make a lot of money. In fact, in many cases it is possible to make a lot of money in a low volume stock without buying a lot of shares. In general, what you need to do to make money in any stock is identify a pattern of behavior that consistently occurs and then exploit that pattern. For example, if people are selling a stock, then buy the stock. If people are buying a stock, then sell the stock. And if people are holding the stock, then do nothing. Of course, it is possible to get lucky and make a lot, but if you do that you will probably lose a lot of money as well. In a low volume stock, your trades will have a lot less volume than the trades of a high volume stock, so it will be harder to get a good buy or sell price. However, if you can make a lot of money in a low volume stock it will probably be a lot easier to sell it later for a good price. In general, high volume doesn't mean high price. For example, the Standard and Poors 500 is a index of the market cap of the 500 largest companies by market capitalisation listed on the Standard and Poors exchange. You could create your own spreadsheet of company market caps and use that as an index, but the S&P 500 is probably quite similar. The advantage of using an index is that you can use it to benchmark the performance of a given company against the performance of the index as a whole. The index doesn't have to be just the market cap of the top N companies. It could be the market cap of the top N companies in a given sector, or it could be the market cap of the top N companies in a given location. S&P 500 is probably the most well known index, but there are other indices as well. For example, the Total Stock Market Index (TSM) is a index of the market cap of the 15 largest companies by market capitalisation listed on the Total Stock Market Exchange.

One way to think about it is to imagine that you are buying a put with your mortgage. Puts are typically the riskiest part of a portfolio because they are exposed to "downside" risk. This is different than "upside" risk, which is typically the case for stocks. Stocks have the potential for high returns, but only if you buy the right ones. Buying the wrong stock is worse than buying no stock at all. It is possible to buy both badly, and in the case of buying no stock, there is no chance to recover. Buying a put with your mortgage means that you are paying to have the option of selling your home at a fixed price. The fixed price is typically the market price, often by definition as the market price often is the price that you can get by selling your home to a stranger. Pay close attention to the end date, because you do not want to pay more than you need to. Also, consider that you may be able to refinance your mortgage to get a better rate, or to sell your home and buy a new one, but you may not have the equity to make those moves. Having the equity to make those moves means that you can refinance your mortgage or sell your home and buy a new one without having to pay a costly private mortgage insurance (PMI). If you do not have the equity to make those moves, you are at risk of having to pay a costly private mortgage insurance (PMI). Note that there are other ways to get PMI insurance, such as paying a premium to a property tax escrow, but those are typically more expensive. Also, consider that there are few other people who deal with the risk of having to pay a costly private mortgage insurance (PMI) on a regular basis, such as banks and other financial institutions. Banks and other financial institutions deal with risk on a regular basis, and they have to charge to cover that risk. They charge for the risk of having to pay a costly private mortgage insurance (PMI) on a regular basis, just like you. If you shop around, you may find a bank or other financial institution that offers a better rate or lower cost. Remember to shop around to find the bank or other financial institution that offers a good rate or lower cost. Also, consider that there are some banks and other financial institutions that offer special deals if you keep your mortgage for a certain amount of time, or if you keep your mortgage at a certain level. Keep an eye out for those deals, especially if you plan on staying in your mortgage for a long time. There are some banks and other financial institutions that offer special deals on mortgages, so keep an eye out for those. Also, consider that there are some mortgage brokers and agents who work for the banks and other financial institutions, and they can offer you deals directly. You can save a lot of money by doing this, so keep an eye out for mortgage brokers and agents. Also, you can save a lot of money by doing it yourself. If you do it yourself, you can save money by not paying the mortgage broker and agent, and you can save even more money by not paying the bank and other financial institution. If you do it yourself, you only pay the cost of the mortgage, and you keep all of the equity in your home. This is the best way to do it, in my opinion. The only way I would do it differently is to say if you have the equity to make the moves, then make the moves. If you don't have the equity to make the moves, then pay close attention to the end date, and make the move if you can. Otherwise, you might have to pay a costly private mortgage insurance (PMI).

You need to hire a tax professional and have them sort it out for you properly and advise you on how to proceed next. Don't do it yourself, you're way past the stage when you could. You're out of compliance, and you're right - there are penalties that a professional might know how to mitigate, and maybe even negotiate a waiver with the IRS, depending on the circumstances of the case. Be careful of answers like "you don't need to pay anything" that are based on nothing of facts. Based on what you said in the question and in the comments, it actually sounds like you do have to pay something, and you're in trouble with the IRS already. It might be that you misunderstood something in the past (e.g.: you said the business had filed taxes before, but in fact that might never happened and you're confusing "business filed taxes" with "I filed schedule C") or it might be the actual factual representation of things (you did in fact filed a tax return for your business with the IRS, either form 1120 of some kind or 1065). In any case a good licensed (CPA or EA) professional will help you sort it out and educate you on what you need to do in the future.

You're trying to solve a liquidity problem with a printing press. That's like trying to solve a hunger problem with a food processor. You're not going to get very far with that. You're going to have to go find the money somewhere else. Perhaps you could sell your property to pay for it. But then you're not going to have any more property, and it's going to be harder for you to find someone else willing to pay you for it. This is why some governments issue bonds or monetize goods and services from the private sector in exchange for bonds. People colloquially call this "printing money" but it's a misnomer in this day and age where printers are not relevant tools. The good and service goes onto the government's book, and the company/entity that provided the service now has a bond on its book which can be immediately sold to someone else for cash (another reading is that the bond is as good as cash). The bond didn't previously exist until the government said it did, and likewise will cease to exist when government stops saying it does. (This is why some companies keep their surplus cash in hand, rather than banking it. Banks can fail, and then what are you going to do with all your money in the bank? It's not an exaggeration to say that a government could conceivable issue bonds for just about anything it wants, including goods and services from the private sector. The key thing is that a government that issues bonds on a whim is not a government you can take very seriously. It's one thing to issue bonds for a large public project like a highway or dam, but it's another thing to issue bonds for small receipts. If a government issues bonds for small receipts, then it's not a government you can take very seriously because it's clear that the government is just trying to make cash by any means necessary. This is why some governments are more careful about issuing bonds, and why some (like the US gov't) require bonds to go through a process of approval before issuance. This is also why some (like the US gov't) allow bonds to be immediately sold to the public, rather than having them hold the bond for a fixed period of time before selling. The US gov't also has the option of not paying interest on the bond, which is a pretty big deal. If a bond doesn't pay interest, then it's not a very attractive bond to hold, unless the bond holder can sell the bond to someone else. But, if the bond pays interest, then the bond holder doesn't have to sell the bond to someone else, and instead can just cash out by selling the bond to a third party. This is why the US gov't sometimes issues "gift" bonds to the public, which pay out a fixed small amount of interest as well as a face value that's larger than the face value of other bonds the government has issued. The end result is that the US gov't can issue bonds to the public, but it's not a very serious thing to do. For a government that issues bonds on a whim, bonds are just a convenient way to get cash from the public without having to provide anything in return.

Disclaiming an IRA is not the same as distributing an IRA. When you distribute an IRA, you distribute the entirety of the IRA, including the distributions you've already taken. When you disclaim an IRA, you distribute only the remaining balance in the IRA. You can distribute a large amount of money over a small amount of money, but you can't distribute a small amount of money over a large amount of money. This is because the small amount of money is now being distributed at a higher tax rate than the large amount of money. The end result is that the small amount of money ends up being distributed at a lower net tax rate than the large amount of money. This is why it's not a good idea to have a bequest that distributes a small amount of money over a large amount of money. The bequest will distribute the $1000 at a higher tax rate than the $5000, so that net tax rate will be higher for the $1000 than for the $5000. This is true for both of your scenarios. The scenario with the large amount of money being distributed at a higher tax rate is going to net you more total tax than if you distributed the small amount of money at a lower tax rate. This is also why it's not a good idea to have a bequest that distributes a small amount of money over a large amount of money. The small amount of money will be taxed at a higher rate than the large amount of money, so you'll net more total tax from the small amount of money being distributed than you would from the large amount of money being distributed. This is true no matter how the money is distributed, so long as the entirety of the IRA isn't distributed. If you have a bequest that distributes a small amount of money over a large amount of money, and you have no other bequests, then you've just made it so that a small amount of your money will be taxed at a higher rate than the large amount of your money. You've not done anything to reduce your tax liability, and you've increased your tax rate for no reason. This is why it's not a good idea to have a bequest that distributes a small amount of money over a large amount of money. The small amount of money will be taxed at a higher rate than the large amount of money, and as a result you'll net more total tax from the small amount of money being distributed than you would from the large amount of money being distributed. The most common and profitable investment for a good retirement in Australia is to invest in an index tracking ETF. The index tracks the market and if the market as a whole rises, then your investment will too, even though within that index individual companies will rise and fall. This limits your potential rate of return as well, and is still significantly more volatile than leaving your cash in an Aussie bank (after all, the whole market can fall), but it might strike the right balance for you. If you're getting started, HSBC, Nabtrade, Commsec and Westpac were all offering a couple of months of free trades up to a certain value. Once the free trades are done, you'll do better to move to another broker (you can migrate your shares to the others to take advantage of their free trades too) or to a cheaper broker like CMC Markets.

The Federal Reserve Bank publishes exchange rate data in their H.10 release. It is daily, not minute by minute. The Fed says this about their data: About the Release The H.10 weekly release contains daily rates of exchange of major currencies against the U.S. dollar. The data are noon buying rates in New York for cable transfers payable in the listed currencies. The rates have been certified by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York for customs purposes as required by section 522 of the amended Tariff Act of 1930. The historical EURUSD rates for the value of 1 EURO in US$ are at: http://www.federalreserve.gov/releases/h10/hist/dat00_eu.htm If you need to know USDEUR the value of 1 US$ in EUROS use division 1.0/EURUSD.
Currency fluctuation is not reflected in Google Finance. The value you see is the value you entered. Google Finance only updates the exchange rate once a day.

I'm not aware of an expense incurred in your traveling between locations, but I know many people who do similar things and they always try to deduct at least some of their mileage expenses. For example, if your employer reimburses you for mileage at a certain rate, then I would say as long as you are not reimbursed for an expense greater than your total mileage, then the difference is a deductible expense. In other words, if your employer reimburses you for 50% of your mileage, then I would say the other 50% is a deductible expense. You aren't officially reimbursed for it, but that doesn't mean there isn't an expense. Just make sure that you have good records that justify your expenses as business related. Keep in mind that just because an expense is not officially reimbursed does not mean it is not a deductible expense. For more information about deducting mileage expenses, I would refer you to this link: http://www.irs.gov/taxtopics/tc503.html

Yes, you should include the region where you live in your investment portfolio. It is called home bias. You might have heard about it with regards to putting all your eggs in one basket. The problem with a high home bias is that the investment universe in your own backyard is usually geared toward your own income. Lack of diversification: In your case, you should probably invest in other income streams outside of your own income. This is especially true if you are planning on retiring in a different country. Different countries have different tax laws. You may be able to take advantage of tax treaties to hedge against some of those taxes. But, you need to be cognizant of the fact that there are other people who deal in international tax law. They are called expats. They move to a different country to live and work. Just like you need to pay taxes on your income in your own country, they need to pay taxes on their income in their new country. Tax treaties attempt to define what income is taxable in what country. They are negotiated between the government of a country and the governments of other countries. If the demand for a commodity that is not produced in your own country is high enough, you can probably sell it a higher price than you bought it for. But, there is usually a cost of carry. The higher the cost of carry, the lower the profit you can make from selling it. And there is also the issue with storage. You may have to pay to store it in a warehouse or in your own backyard.

Precious metals are not a viable emergency fund. They have value only in a barter economy, and even then, only for the short term. A 100 year emergency fund is 100 years of living off of an investment. Precious metals do not qualify as an investment, and as the years go by, they lose more value than they gained from the previous year. This is why we recommend investing in index funds. An index fund is a portfolio of the market, and as the market as a whole rises, so does the index fund, even though within that index fund, individual companies will rise and fall. A 100 year index fund is a great way to ensure that you are getting the best return for your money, with the least amount of your money going to the financial industry.

You're going to hate my answer, but... If there was an easy way to ID stocks like FB that were going to do what FB did, then those stocks wouldn't exist and do that because they would be priced higher at the IPO. The fact is there is always some doubt, no one knows the future, and sometimes value only becomes clear with time. Everyone wants to buy a stock before it rises right? It will only be worth a rise if it makes more profit though, and once it is established as making more profit the price will be already up, because why wouldn't it be? That means to buy a real winner you have to buy before it is completely obvious to everyone that it is going to make more profit in the future, and that means stock prices trade at speculative prices, based on expected future performance, not current or past performance. Now I'm not saying past and future performance has nothing in common, but there is a reason that a thousand financially oriented websites quote a disclaimer like "past performance is not necessarily a guide to future performance". Now maybe this is sort of obvious, but looking at your image, excluding things like market capital that you've not restricted, the PE ratio is based on CURRENT price and PAST earnings, the dividend yield is based on PAST publications of what the dividend will be and CURRENT price, the price to book is based on PAST publication of the company balance sheet and CURRENT price, the EPS is based on PAST earnings and the published number of shares, and the ROI and net profit margin in based on published PAST profits and earnings and costs and number of shares. So it must be understood that every criteria chosen is PAST data that analysts have been looking at for a lot longer than you have with a lot more additional information and experience with it. The only information that is even CURRENT is the price. Thus, my ultimate conclusive point is, you can't based your stock picks on criteria like this because it's based on past information and current stock price, and the current stock price is based on the markets opinion of relative future performance. The only way to make a good stock pick is understand the business, understand its market, and possibly understand world economics as it pertains to that market and business. You can use various criteria as an initial filter to find companies and investigate them, but which criteria you use is entirely your preference. You might invest only in profitable companies (ones that make money and probably pay regular dividends), thus excluding something like an oil exploration company, which will just lose money, and lose it, and lose some more, forever... unless it hits the jackpot, in which case you might suddenly find yourself sitting on a huge profit. It's a question of risk and preference. Regarding your concern for false data. Google defines the Return on investment (TTM) (%) as: Trailing twelve month Income after taxes divided by the average (Total Long-Term Debt + Long-Term Liabilities + Shareholders Equity), expressed as a percentage. If you really think they have it wrong you could contact them, but it's probably correct for whatever past data or last annual financial results it's based on.

That is a very complex question. The short answer is that it depends on the stock in question and what factors are causing the stock to be in higher demand than usual. To understand the question fully, you need to understand the market and the factors that affect it. I'll try to explain some of the main points here. Markets are basically a gigantic highly complex poker game. The daily gyrations of the market are about individuals trying to predict where the herd is going to go next and then after that and then after that etc. If you want to understand the market, you need to understand the psychology of the market and the part that price plays in that psychology. The price of a stock is the market's valuation of the cash flows it entitles you to in the future. It is the present value (PV) of the cash flows. The PV of the cash flows is the sum of the discounted future cash flows. Discounted future cash flows are the future cash flows reduced by the present value of the risk associated with those cash flows. The riskier the activity, the more you need to discount the future cash flows. If you can identify stocks before they become popular, then you can get in before the price rises. If you can identify stocks after they become popular, then you can get out before the price drops. In either case, you can make a profit as long as you can live with making a short-term loss and recouperating it later.

Value investing is not a hands-off strategy. You need to actively participate in the market and have a good understanding of the companies you are investing in. The assistant strategy is to find a good broker with low transactions costs and a good line of credit. The goal is to not pay too much and make a good long-term investment in a company. Some good brokerages to look into are: First Republic, Chase, and Capital One. Some good lines of credit to look into are: Marbles Credit Card, Lending Club, and Ink Bold (for businesses). Some good brokerages to look into for futures are: CME Group, Inc., and NISM Series VIII (for non-US residents). The CME Group, Inc. is the sole issuer of all securities options listed at the CME, four other U.S. stock exchanges and the National Association of Securities Dealers, Inc. (NASD), and is the entity through which all CME transactions are ultimately cleared. As the issuer of all options, CME essentially takes the opposite side of every option traded. Because CME basically becomes the buyer for every seller and the seller for every buyer, it allows options traders to buy and sell in a secondary market without having to find the original opposite party. NISM Series VIII is a 6-week course that teaches the fundamentals of futures trading. It is offered in 27 countries and in 13 different languages. More information can be found at http://www.cme.com/learning-center/nism-series-viii-futures-trading-fundamentals/

You're certainly referring to "volatility" in the market. And while it's certainly true that a lot of investors are spooked by the prospect of volatility, it's also true that a lot of investors are not. In a world where everyone is afraid of volatility, you can be the one to take advantage of it. That's the theory behind "market inefficiency". So for example, in a world where everyone is afraid of volatility, it's efficient for you to invest in things that are not correlated with the market. That's the theory behind "diversification". And while it's certainly true that a lot of investors are not willing to hold an uncorrelated position, it's also true that a lot of investors are willing to hold a diversified portfolio. And if you're one of those investors, you can take advantage of it. You're trying to avoid 100% of the risk that you describe, but need to accept significant risk to avoid that 100% risk. This cannot be avoided. Unless you accept significant risk, you will not be able to avoid the 100% risk that you describe.

Investing in emerging markets is not as simple as investing in the United States stock market. It depends on the exact level of risk that you want, but if you want to keep your risk close to zero you're pretty much stuck with the banks (and those rates don't look to be going up any time soon). If you're willing to accept a little more risk, you can invest in some index funds that track major indexes in the emerging markets. The big problem with all of these is, of course, that the emerging markets are not as liquid as the United States stock market. There are fewer big companies in the emerging markets, and it's harder to move in and out of your investments. All of these factors make it harder to invest in an emerging market than in the United States. The question is really which risk are you willing to accept, and which markets are you willing to invest in. If you Google emerging market investment options, you'll find a lot of options that aren't worth looking into. A good rule of thumb is that if you're not willing to accept significant risk, and if you're not able to diversify enough to avoid significant risk, then don't invest in an emerging market. There are other people who deal with multiple emerging markets (international businesses, for instance) who worry about the same thing. As such, you can take a look at foreign exchange rate futures and related instruments to estimate what the market as a whole currently expects the values to do. The prices of these futures could be a useful starting point.

Just like regular stocks, fixed income ETFs have their own supply and demand. The demand for an ETF is the sum of the demand for all the individual stocks that make up the ETF. The supply for an ETF is the sum of the supply for all the individual stocks that make up the ETF. Just like regular stocks, the price of an ETF is determined by the balance between its demand and its supply. An ETF that has a high demand and a low supply will have a high price. An ETF that has a low demand and a high supply will have a low price. In general, because they are not as liquid as regular stocks, ETFs that pay high levels of income are usually priced at slight discount to regular stocks. There are also arbitrage opportunities, just like regular stocks, so the price of an ETF that pays high levels of income could get pushed up or down by the market. Regular stocks are usually in higher demand than ETFs, so it's harder to get filled in a regular stock than it is in an ETF. That's changing, though. ETFs are becoming much more popular, and it's expected that ETFs will eventually outperform regular stocks.

You're trying to match the price of a stock, which is the value at which it's traded, with the price of a company, which is the value at which it's owned. These are in fact both wrong. The price of a stock is the value at which it's traded. It's not the value at which it's owned. The price of a company is the value at which it's owned. It's not the value at which it's traded. In a sane marketplace, these values should be equal. In a sane marketplace, if the price of a company goes up, then the price of the stock that represents that company will go up by the same amount. If the price of a company goes down, then the price of the stock that represents that company will go down by the same amount. In a sane marketplace, if the price of a company is not equal to the price of the stock that represents that company, then there is an arbitrage opportunity and free money never exists for long.

A student loan is a type of loan that is only available to students who are attending a public university or college. It is a relatively small amount of money that is borrowed from the government to cover the cost of going to school. The amount of money that you can borrow is relatively small because it is intended to be used in combination with other forms of student aid. If you are wanting to borrow a large amount of money to cover your entire cost of going to school, then you want to fill out the FAFSA (that is the acronym for the Free Application for Federal Student Aid). Based on the information that you provide on the FAFSA, the government will calculate how much student aid you are entitled to, which can be a large amount of money. Keep in mind though, the government will calculate how much student aid you are entitled to based on the information that you provide on the FAFSA, so make sure that you provide accurate information. In particular, make sure that you provide your correct SSN (either by entering it directly on the FAFSA or by uploading a copy of your SSN card). The SSN is how the government will use to calculate how much student aid you are entitled to, so provide it correctly. Also, keep in mind that not all expenses are considered when calculating your EFC (that is the acronym for "expected family contribution"). The EFC is the amount of money that the government expects you to be able to pay for your education. It is calculated based on your income, your spouse's income, and the income of your spouse - if you are married. The government has a worksheet that is used to calculate EFC; if you are wanting to calculate your EFC then you want to use that worksheet. Note that the worksheet only applies to married people filing jointly with their spouse, so use it at your risk if you are filing separately from your spouse. Also, keep in mind that student loans are not dischargable in a bankruptcy. So for example if you took money that could have gone to student loans and poured it into other debt, then for some reason declared bankruptcy later, your student load debt would remain while other debt would be discharged; essentially that money would have been better spent on the student loan. This isn't to advocate that you should pay down student loans with the intent of declaring bankruptcy, or that this makes it a better decision necessarily, just a factor that is sometimes forgotten.

You're not qualified. You say you have a "dedicated space" but it has to be an exclusive room. You cannot deduct 10 sq. ft. from your living room because your computer that is used wholly for your business is there. It has to be a room that is used exclusively for your business, and for your business only. I.e.: nothing not related to the business is there, and when you're there the only thing you do is working on your business. Your office doesn't have to be in your home, but if it is in your home, it counts as your office. If you take primary residence exclusion as part of your FEI, then I doubt you can deduct as well.

What about when I sell other items that aren't in your name? You get 10% on those. That's not fair. You should get 10% on all sales, and then you can decide to use that money to buy more items, or you can decide to use it for something else. But if you decide to use it for something else, you need to make that clear on your book. You can't have a sale that isn't reported.

Investing in individual stocks is not a good idea for you. It is only a good idea if you have a significant amount of money to invest and you are not afraid of losing it. If you invest in individual stocks, you will probably lose money. A lot of money. More money than you would make from selling covered call options. But if that doesn't scare you away, then investing in individual stocks can be a good idea. Just make sure you have a tax ID and pay taxes on all the gains and dividends.

It looks like you are trying to avoid the risk of having all of your eggs in one basket. That is understandable. What if the stock market crashes and you lose 50% or more of your investment in a single year? That is a very real possibility. Although you may be trying to avoid that, it is still a risk. Any portfolio that you construct will still have that risk. The question is not whether you should put all of your eggs in one basket, or in two baskets, or in three baskets. The question is how broad is Yahoo! Finance taking in their data for making some comparisons. For example, did you look at the other funds in your 401(k) at work? On the 401(k) page, Yahoo! Finance shows a list of funds that you can buy into. You can buy into as many as you like. You can also buy into funds that you cannot see on the list. The list of funds on the 401(k) page is generated based on the funds that you have available and that Yahoo! Finance has data for. If you are not able to buy into a particular fund that you like, then you should consider investing in an ETF (or maybe a couple) in that particular area. You can also buy into funds that Yahoo! Finance has no data for. This is not to say that you should go out and buy a bunch of funds that you cannot possibly track. What I am saying is that you might want to spread your risk over a larger number of funds than Yahoo! Finance has data for. If you want to invest in an area that Yahoo! Finance has no data for, then you should invest according to your own research. You might also want to invest in an ETF or two in addition to your regular funds. I would encourage you to keep good records and do projections. Keeping good records will help you to know whether your current savings are on track to fund your non-financial goals (such as retirement, or buying a house). Doing projections will help you to know whether you can afford to pay for an emergency or other unexpected expense. Having good savings is an important part of not having all of your eggs in one basket.

I'm not sure what you're talking about. Expenses ratio? What about interest? Amortization? Those are the things that will make or break a high income/low expense portfolio. And will make or break your ability to get credit. Credit cards, that is. Because it's not about having the income to pay. It's about having the income to charge. And then paying it off. That's the secret. How do you get the income to charge? That's easy. You go to any bank or credit card company. Or you go to the ones that offer high income/low expense accounts. They're not necessarily the same vice versa. Just because an account offers high income doesn't mean it's a good account. It might have high fees. That's why you need to shop around. And then pay attention to the end date, because you don't want to pay 2% interest on the entire balance.

What about maintenance? Human: Maintenance is (maybe) part of the value of the asset. It's not something you can easily put a value on. For example, if a piece of equipment costs $100,000 and you maintain it at 10%, you have a $10,000 asset. You could say it's worth 10% of what you would otherwise need to buy a similar piece of equipment, but that's a pretty subjective judgment. Also, but how you maintain it makes a difference. If you have a garage, and you store the equipment there, you probably need to pay property tax on the value of the equipment. So, you might need to include the cost of maintaining the equipment in your calculations. I'd say at this point it's worth calling in a consultant to help you sort it out. Get a couple contractors out to look the place over and give you some quotes on the work needed, most will do so for free, or a nominal fee. Everything about the extent and cost of repairs is complete guess work until you have some firm numbers. You might also consider getting an up-to-date appraisal, particularly if you can find someone willing to give you an "after improvements" estimate as well. The housing market has fluctuated a bunch in the last couple years, your current value may have shifted significantly from where you think it is if you haven't done one recently. You will definitely have to pay for this service, I would estimate around $500 based on one I got in Seattle a few months ago. You might also consider reaching out to a local property management company to find out where they think you would fall in the scope of the current rental market and what improvements they would recommend. You will probably want to be onsite to talk to any of the above people about the work they are proposing, and your intended goals, so figure some travel costs and time into your evaluation. As one of your noted concerns was the state of the roof, I can tell you that in Seattle County, and the spec sheet for most shingle manufacturers, you are limited to two layers of shingles, then the roof is supposed to be stripped and redone from the bare wood. Personally, I won't even do the second layer, I always go to bare wood and start over, if for no other reason than it gives me an opportunity to inspect the deck and deal with any minor problem areas before they become big problems. I don't know Greene County to know what the local code may be like, but odds are high that the shingle manufacture would not honor any warranty with this installation. Another potential gotcha that may be lurking out there is your ex may still have a lingering claim to the home if you go to sell it. I don't know the rules in Washington off hand, but where I grew up (with family in the real estate and title insurance businesses) there was a law regarding homestead rights. If a spouse spent even one night in a property, they had an interest in it and an explicit waiver had to be signed to release said interest. Review your divorce settlement and/or contact your attorney to confirm your status in this regard. Also consider the potential of refinancing your mortgage to either reduce the payment, or get funds for the improvements/repairs. Final note, I understand wanting to help out a friend (I have done similar things more times than I can count), but seriously look at the situation and see if you can't get the rent or other compensation up to the level of the mortgage at least. You mentioned that you have belongings still on the property, what would a storage unit for said items cost? In terms of juggling the numbers you could potentially use that value as justification to adjust the friends rent as a caretaker fee without any issue. (Verify with your CPA) Talk to the friend and see if there are other parts of the job they would be willing and able to take on as consideration for the reduced rent (make sure you have at least a simple contract on any such agreement). Or if none of the above are sufficient to balance the numbers, see if they would be willing to take on an actual room mate to help make up the difference.

You're not thinking this through. The 5 D's in the contract need to be thought through. What happens when one partner becomes disinterested, divorced (break up), does drugs (something illegal), dies or does not agree with decisions. One complication if you buy jointly, and decide to break up/move, on will the other partner be able to refinance? If not the leaving person will probably not be able to finance a new home as the banks are rarely willing to assume multiple mortgage risks for one person. (High income/large down payment not with standing.) I prefer the one person rents option to option two. The trouble with that is that it sounds like you're in better position to be the owner, and she has a higher emotional need to own. If she is really interested in building equity I would recommend a 15 year or shorter mortgage. Building equity in a 30 year is not realistic.

There are a few ways you can help with a mortgage. First, if you are not on any of the paperwork, you are not getting credit for helping with the mortgage. Second, you can get a 15 year mortgage of your own to replace the 30 year. A 15 year loan will not have as much in interest, so you will be able to make more of your money available for other things. One problem with a 15 year loan is that you will have to pay more each month. The extra cost of the 15 year loan will be the same as the interest you would pay in a 30 year loan. If you are interested in building equity, then you want to be in a position to have that equity increase. By increasing your equity, you will be able to use the equity you have to buy more property. If you are not in a position to have your equity increase, then you are not able to use the equity you have to buy more property. One way to think about this is to imagine that your normal mode of using money is to buy things that give you pleasure. For example, you might choose to spend your money on a new car, or on a vacation. With a 30 year loan, you will have to spend some of your money on the loan just to buy the same amount of pleasure. If you choose to spend your money on other things, then you will have to spend even more on the loan just to buy the same amount of pleasure. This is why it is important to be in a position to have your equity increase. So, how do you get there? One way is to simply take the equity you have and use it to buy more property. If you have $100,000 in equity, then you can use it to buy a $250,000 house. The problem is that the $250,000 house will be a rental. If you simply take the equity you have and use it to buy more property, then you have the same amount of your money in the mortgage as you did before. So, you are not able to use the equity you have to buy more property. Unless you increase your income, you are not able to make more of your money available for other things. And, if you don't increase your income, then you will have to go back to spending your money on things that give you pleasure. Which is better? Depends on your goals. If your goal is to have a lot of money available for other things, then increasing your income is the better option. If your goal is to have a lot of money available for things that give you pleasure, then spending your money on those things is the better option. If you want to have a lot of money available for both, then you want to increase your income while decreasing your expenses. You can increase your income by working more, or you can increase it by using the money you have more efficiently. If you want to use the money you have more efficiently, then you want to make sure that you are in a position to have your equity increase. You can also use the money more efficiently by spending it on things that give you pleasure while you work. This is why it is important to be in a position to have your equity increase. So, to summarize: Increasing your income is the best way to make more of your money available for other things. And increasing your equity is the best way to use your money more efficiently. Combine the two, and you have the best chance to have a lot of money available for things that give you pleasure while working.

You're going to hate my answer, but... If you don't pay your student loans, they will garnish your wages. They will take 15% of your paycheck and give it to you on a 1099-B, so it will cost you more. I'm assuming (if this is legitimate) that you're talking about government student loans in the US. If you're going to hate my answer, please feel free to vote it closed as not constructive. I'm not a tax professional, and I don't know the answers to some parts of your question. I do know that there are penalties for not paying student loans, and that there are more penalties for not paying them back in a timely fashion. I also know that there are some dischargables in a bankruptcy, but I'd refer you to a lawyer licensed in your state for the details. There are some general principles that apply in all cases, though. First, I'd check your terms and conditions of your loan to be sure that you actually qualify for a discharge. Many loans have a clause that disqualifies you from a discharge if you fail to make payments. Read through your terms and conditions of your loan to be sure that you understand what will happen if you fail to make payments. Also, as I mentioned before, there are penalties that a lawyer and an accountant will know how to mitigate, and maybe even negotiate a waiver with your creditor, depending on the amount of the loan and the hardship it would cause you. A 5% APR is pretty easy to negotiate a waiver for, actually. Especially if you shop around, you'll be surprised at how low a rate you might get. I would shop around and see what the going rates are. Also, you can usually negotiate a waiver for a late payment charge, but you'll have to make a strong case for it. Late payment charges are usually non-negotiable, so you'll have to make a strong case for it. Your case will be stronger if you can show that there were extenuating circumstances that you could do to mitigate, so be sure to include that in your negotiations. Also, if you're going to hate my answer, please feel free to vote it closed as not constructible.

because the demand for the product is unelastic. That is, people who want to buy the product at any price. When you increase the cost of manufacturing the profit margin for the company producing the product goes up. However, if the demand for the product is elastic, meaning people who want to buy the product at a reasonable price, then increasing the cost of manufacturing will drive prices down. This is what happened in the US with the passage of the Affordable Care Act. The demand for health insurance is so strong that it can drive prices down. Weak demand items are the opposite. They are items that people do not want to buy at any price. This is why Walgreens and other retailers are having a tough time. They are not unique in what they sell, and they are having problems just like any other company in their industry.

I'm not sure what you're referring to as "third world countries" but I'd like to think that the vast majority of the world's population lives in what we would call "developing" countries. You're certainly correct that fees are being applied to fees, but that's not the whole story. The big-cap indexes have a lot more fees than the small-cap indexes, so it's not just a matter of scale. The fees on the small-cap indexes are generally lower because there are fewer fees being applied. Small-cap indexes also tend to have higher turnover than large-cap indexes, so it's harder to get a good read on the small-cap indexes. Another difference is that the small-cap indexes will tend to have more negative taxation than the large-cap indexes. This is a factor that can get overlooked. The taxation on the small-cap indexes is higher because the indexes are not as liquid. Since it's harder to buy and sell them, they don't get the same kind of tax benefits as the large-cap indexes. This is something that can be quickly, and precisely answered through a conversation with your broker. The last difference, and this is a big one, is that the small-cap indexes will tend to have higher volatility than the large-cap indexes. This is a factor that can get mispriced in a hurry. And the fourth and final chart looks at the price chart over the last 6 months with a 50 day moving average. All of these charts are available as daily text files from my broker. I'd suggest looking at them both and comparing the two. There are other factors that can affect your taxation, so I'd suggest talking to your broker about it.

There is no one-size-fits-all investment strategy. It depends on your risk tolerance, goals, time horizon, and needs. The number 1 rule is: Do not put money into investments that you feel you can lose. Investing is not a zero-sum game, and you can achieve a positive long-term (or short-term, depending on what you're after) outcome for yourself without compromising your ability to sleep at night if you take the time to become acquainted with the investments you are putting your money into. How you get there depends on what you start with, how much you have to play with, how risky is your employment, and how long you have to worry about saving for a rainy day (i.e. your emergency fund). If you're juggling money between more than a handful of investments, you'll need to decide on a broad allocation of money into different asset classes (e.g., US stocks, US bonds, international stocks, international bonds), and you'll need to decide on the risk tolerance for each. For example, you might decide that you want 25% of your money in US stocks, 25% in US bonds, 10% in international stocks, and 10% in international bonds. Or you might decide on a lower-risk strategy, such as a high-yield bond fund and a low-yield stock index fund. You're certainly referring to "gift" money that your employer gave you. The amount of the gift, and the taxation of the gift, depend on how much the employer wanted to give you, and what he wanted to give you for doing so. If this was just a one-time event, you should probably just take the $1000 and buy whatever you want with it. If this is a recurring event, then you need to calculate if the true cost of the gift is. To do that, you need to calculate the present value (time discounted future value) of the payments you would need to make to break even with the gift. The present value of the payments would be: $1000 + ($1000 * 1.01)n For example, if you were to receive $1000 every year, the present value of the payments would be $1000 + ($1000 * 1.01)n-1) = $500.01. If you wanted to buy a $200K house with the $1000, you could calculate if you had enough by determining if the present value of the payments would be enough to break even with the interest-difference on the $200K loan at some interest rate. If you determined that you could make enough payments to break even with the interest-difference on the $200k loan at some interest rate, then you could go buy the house. If you calculated that you would need to make payments at an effective interest rate of say 3.25% to break even with the interest-difference on the $200k loan, then you would need to make payments of $1000 + ($1000 * 1.01)n-1) at an effective interest rate of 3.25%. Your employer was probably not willing to pay you $60,000 for making those payments, so you have to decide how badly you want the house. If you decide you want the house enough to make the payments, then you'll just have to expect the 3.25% interest rate as a part of the cost of the house. If that seems too high, then you shouldn't pay more than you would rent for a lesser sized house.

You're trying to think about this very well. The issue with owning a wholly-owned subsidiary is that you're not able to sell it to a third party. You could sell it to a fourth party, but that would create a lot of administrative overhead for you. This is why I'm trying to simplify things greatly here. The issue with simplifying things is that I'm not able to capture the full complexity of the situation you find yourself in. I would encourage you to continue thinking about this and maybe even run some scenarios to see how different ownership structures would play out for you. The scenarios I ran in my head had the parent company issuing shares to the subsidiary, rather than buying them. This is mostly due to tax reasons. If the parent company issues shares to the subsidiary, then the subsidiary no longer counts as a wholly-owned subsidiary. It becomes a 50%-50% partnership and you have to pay tax on 25% of the profits. If you buy the shares from the subsidiary, then the subsidiary no longer counts as a wholly-owned subsidiary. It becomes a 100% partnership and you pay tax on 100% of the profits. This is an extremely simplified example. The reality is that there are all kinds of tax issues that can arise. I would encourage you to think about them. Maybe even consult a tax adviser (EA/CPA licensed in your State) for more details.

It will not. The pound is not a primary currency. It is a secondary currency, and not that liquid. You might have problems converting it to primary currency, but that would be another story. The pound is not a good currency to hold on to for long term purposes. It has a history of volatility, and it is expected to be quite volatile in the future as well. The pound is a very short term currency, and not a very stable one. It is best to hold on to it for very short term purposes, and then to convert it into a primary currency at the first opportunity. I would convert it into a primary currency as soon as I could, just to avoid the risk of holding on to it for a longer term. There are better ways to hedge against currency risks with the funds readily available to you through your ISA. You can hold on to the pound with a ISA, and then convert it into a primary currency when the ISA allowance is spent.

To the Federal Reserve. The Fed takes in money from penalties and uses it to fund other government programs. So, for example, if a bank seizes an account from a customer who didn't pay a fine, then that money goes to the Fed, which then gives it to the customer who didn't pay a fine. (This is an extremely simplified example - in reality, the customer who didn't pay a fine probably didn't deserve a fine, but that's another story). So, to summarize: (Rounded approximate amounts for simplicity) Now, you might ask: What about state crimes? Don't states also have penalties for things like DUI, or failing to pay child support? Yes, but those are often levied by individual states, so it depends on the state you're in. For example, when I was in college, the state I was in had a "fix it yourself" policy for minor traffic violations. You might also have a question about the cost of all this. Well, I'd first ask you to keep in mind that we're talking pennies here. I would also assert that the cost of administering the criminal justice system is more than paid for by the fees collected. You could easily say that the cost of the system is 10% of the cost of a fine, but that wouldn't be true. The cost of the system is the cost of the system. It's there whether you like it or not. (Not saying you should pay for something you don't think is worth it, just pointing out that the system is there). Also, you should be happy if you happy to have an income high enough that you don't need to worry about minor traffic violations or other such nonsense. But there are those business people who see opportunity in folks like you and profit greatly from it. I personally worry very much about who has my money. I would rather pay taxes than have some jackass in a fancy car cut me off and then act like I didn't pay a fine.

Fundamentally interest rates are low because of inflation. Currency exchange is very volatile and interest rates are a factor in determining the value of a currency. In a world where all currencies are stable (i.e. not subject to inflation) interest rates would be much higher.

There are several ways someone makes money on very low volume stocks. People could have inherited stocks. They could have been gifted stocks. They might have won stocks in a lawsuit (unlikely to be a mall, but not impossible). They could have married into the stocks. So there's other ways of getting stocks without buying them on the open market.

I don't think there's a specific advantage to MIRR over regular IRAs, aside from the tax benefits. The tax benefits are mostly for the employer, and include: 1) the ability to deduct the cost of administering the IRA (i.e. the cost of the IRA custodian, the cost of processing the IRA contributions, etc.), 2) the ability to deduct the cost of buying and/or selling the IRA custodian, and 3) the ability to deduct the cost of processing the IRA distributions. These deductions can only be claimed if the employer makes contributions to the IRA, so if you formed Nathan, LLC, you would need to make contributions to it in order to take advantage of the tax benefits. (With a regular IRA, the ability to deduct the cost of processing the IRA distributions is not limited to the employer, so if you formed Nathan, LLC, you would not need to make contributions to it in order to take advantage of the tax benefits). This is different from Roth IRAs to which you can make a contribution without an employer match, but you appear to be referring to traditional IRAs. Traditional IRA contributions can be made by an employer or an employee or both. The ability to deduct the cost of administering the IRA is a deduction that can be taken by the employer. The ability to deduct the cost of buying and/or selling the IRA custodian is a deduction that can be taken by either the employer or the employee. The deduction is taken on the tax return of the person making the contribution to the IRA. If you formed Nathan, LLC, and made a contribution to it, then you would need to deduct the cost of processing the IRA distributions on your tax return. This would cause the $5000 to be deducted from your taxable income, reducing it to $4500. Your tax return is then calculated on $4500. This is an extremely simplified example. Please do not neglect the cost of the contributions. Just because they are small does not mean they are not there.
